text: Sequence-based DocRE models encode a document by the sequence of words and/or sentences, for example, using the Transformer architecture (Devlin et al., 2019). Zhou et al. (2021) argue that the Transformer attentions are able to extract useful contextual features across sentences for DocRE, and they adopt an adaptive threshold for each entity pair. Zhang et al. (2021) model DocRE as a semantic segmentation task and predict an entity-level relation matrix to capture local and global information. Yao et al. (2019) introduce a distant supervision corpus for document-level relations and evaluate sentence aggregation strategies. Christopoulou et al. (2019) design an edge-oriented graph that connects mentions and entities with typed links, reporting gains on cross-sentence benchmarks.

text: Detection of adversarial examples is a wellexplored field in the image domain. Earlier works have tackled in various ways such as input transformation (Xu et al., 2018), statistical analysis (Grosse et al., 2017), or training a separate binary classifier (Metzen et al., 2017). However, Carlini and Wagner (2017) has shown that an adversary with par- tial knowledge of the detector can easily nullify it. Meanwhile, early works in novelty detection have shown that a generative model can detect anomaly samples (Bishop, 1994). Following this line of research,  have proposed a method to detect out-of-distribution samples by using features of a neural network for maximum likelihood estimation. Kim et al. (2018) explore temperature scaling for confidence calibration in classifiers. Liang et al. (2018) introduce a simple input preprocessing with perturbations to separate in- and out-of-distribution examples. Hendrycks et al. (2019) study baseline confidence scores for OOD detection in deep networks.

text: In the NLP domain, few efforts have been made in detecting word-level adversarial examples. Zhou et al. (2019, DISP) utilize a detector trained on adversarial samples for a joint detect-defense system. FGWS (Mozes et al., 2021) outperforms DISP in detection by building on the observation that attacked samples are composed of rare words. FGWS experiements on 2 proposed attack methods. Le et al. (2021) tackle a particular attack method called UniTrigger (Wallace et al., 2019), which pre-pends or appends an identical phrase in all sentences. While the performance is impressive, applying this method to other attacks requires significant adjustment due to the distinct characteristics of UniTrigger. Meanwhile, Pruthi et al. (2019) analyze character-level noise by simulating human typos in text classification. Li et al. (2020) evaluate robustness under synonym substitution attacks in sentiment analysis. Ebrahimi et al. (2018) present HotFlip, which uses gradient-based character edits to fool neural models.

text: Various NLP data augmentation techniques have been proposed and they are mainly divided into two categories: one is to modify raw input directly, and the other interferes with the embedding (Miyato et al., 2016;. The most commonly used method to modify the raw input is the token replacement: randomly select tokens in a sentence and replace them with semantically similar tokens to synthesize a new sentence. (Wei and Zou, 2019) directly uses the synonym table WordNet(Miller, 1998) for replacement. (Kobayashi, 2018) proposes contextual augmentation to predict the probability distribution of replacement tokens with two causal language models. Sun et al. (2020) analyze robustness under distribution shift using adversarial priors, Li and Qiu (2019) study curriculum-based augmentation schedules for stabilizing training dynamics, Xu et al. (2021) evaluate augmentation utility in low-resource summarization, and Park et al. (2019) explore SpecAugment for speech recognition without textual constraints, and these works are presented consecutively without clarifying how their methodologies or findings relate to token replacement or embedding-level interference raised earlier, nor is it explicit how they inform the subsequent shift to masked language modeling or smoothed representations in classification settings. extends contextual augmentation with BERT's (Devlin et al., 2018) masked language modeling (MLM) to consider bi-directional context. (Gao et al., 2019) softly augments a randomly chosen token in a sentence by replacing its one-hot representation with the distribution of the vocabulary provided by the causal language model in machine translation. Unlike (Gao et al., 2019), we use MLM to generate smoothed representation, which considers the deep bi-directional context more adequately. And our method has better parallelism, which can efficiently obtain the smoothed representation of the entire sentence in one forward process. Moreover, we propose to constrain smoothed representation more controllable through interpolation for classification tasks.

(2019) explore SpecAugment for speech recognition without textual constraints, and these works are presented consecutively without clarifying how their methodologies or findings relate to token replacement or embedding-level interference raised earlier, nor is it explicit how they inform the subsequent shift to masked language modeling or smoothed representations in classification settings.

text: In this part, we first present a formal definition of the few shot slot tagging task in Section 2.1, followed by an introduction of the conventional sequence labeling approaches in Section 2.2 and Sequence Labeling with Prompts in Section 2.3. Few-shot slot tagging seeks to generalize from a handful of labeled utterances per intent domain while preserving boundary precision and label consistency across domains. Conventional methods typically rely on conditional random fields, BiLSTM-CRF architectures, or transformer encoders with token-level classifiers trained with cross-entropy and Viterbi decoding for global consistency. Metric-based and prototype-driven approaches further adapt to new slots by constructing class prototypes in embedding space and transferring decision boundaries without extensive fine-tuning. We focus on the prompt-based reformulation that maps slot labels to verbalizers and conditions token predictions on natural-language templates posed to pretrained language models. Recent efforts on prompt-based sequence labeling emphasize label verbalization, template engineering, and calibration, featuring methods such as Pattern-Exploiting Training for token classification (Schick and Schütze, 2021), span-level prompt tuning for named entity recognition (Liu et al., 2022), and instruction-based adapters that align token-level outputs with task descriptions (Wei et al., 2022). Several contemporaneous studies assess the robustness of prompt-conditioned decoders to domain shift (Mishra et al., 2022). Token-level contrastive learning objectives have also been explored to stabilize gradient signals in the low-data regime (Gunasekar et al., 2021). Pretraining with span corruption has been shown to benefit downstream tagging (Raffel et al., 2020). To situate our contribution, we compare in-context demonstration strategies that balance template length, slot coverage, and calibration, and we provide a controlled evaluation across cross-domain splits and low-resource budgets. Finally, we outline our assumptions about label semantics and present ablations for verbalizer granularity and constraint decoding to clarify when prompts most improve generalization.

text: There has been a great deal of recent interest in providing explanations of blackbox machine learning models, focusing on explaining why the model makes an individual prediction (Ribeiro et al., 2016;Lei et al., 2016;Ribeiro et al., 2018;Alvarez-Melis and Jaakkola, 2018;Liu et al., 2018), or achieving better understanding of the limitations of models (Wallace et al., 2019;Ribeiro et al., 2020). Prior studies on model interpretability span local post-hoc rationales and global probing analyses (Guidotti et al., 2018;Doshi-Velez and Kim, 2017). Attention mechanisms have also been examined as explanations, with mixed evidence about faithfulness (Jain and Wallace, 2019;Wiegreffe and Pinter, 2019). Gradient-based saliency and perturbation tests are frequently compared on robustness metrics (Adebayo et al., 2018;Hooker et al., 2019). Dataset artifacts can inflate apparent explanation quality (Gururangan et al., 2018;Kaushik and Lipton, 2018). Neural module networks and program induction offer structured interpretability (Andreas et al., 2016;Agarwal et al., 2021). In contrast, our goal is to explain how the input can be changed to achieve a desired outcome, which is called a counterfactual explanation (Wachter et al., 2017;Ustun et al., 2019). There has been work on leveraging natural language descriptions to help generate counterfactual explanations for image classifiers (Hendricks et al., 2018), but not tailored at counterfactual predictions for natural language tasks. Finally, there has been interest in improving the performance of semantic parsers through interaction (Wang et al., 2016(Wang et al., , 2017; our approach is complementary to this line of work, since it aims to make the system more transparent to the user.

text: Transfer learning for NLP has expanded beyond single-task fine-tuning to include strategies like multitask learning (MTL) and sequential transfer via intermediate-task training (STILTs). Foundational studies tested when intermediate-task supervision helps target tasks under limited labels (Phang et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2018a). Subsequent benchmarks assessed the stability of transfer gains across random seeds and data subsamples (Pruksachatkun et al., 2020b; Poth et al., 2021). Work on probing catastrophic forgetting linked negative transfer to representation drift during continued training (Wang et al., 2018a; Chang and Lu, 2021). Several analyses proposed mitigation via selective freezing, rehearsal, or auxiliary distillation (Zhuang et al., 2021; Aghajanyan et al., 2021). Dataset curation and calibration have also been emphasized as levers for reliable transfer (Dodge et al., 2020; Si et al., 2023). Prior work has investigated task similarity metrics such as representational alignment and vocabulary overlap (Poth et al., 2021;Vu et al., 2020;Phang et al., 2018). Task clustering methods group datasets by transfer profiles to recommend pretraining curricula (Agarwal et al., 2021;Zhang and Hashimoto, 2021). Domain-adaptive pretraining reduces mismatch by continuing masked language modeling on in-domain corpora (Gururangan et al., 2020). Larger models appear to attenuate negative transfer in low-resource setups (Kaplan et al., 2020;Hernandez et al., 2021). In contrast to these themes, our focus is on a practitioner’s decision rule between MTL and STILTs before training begins. We formalize the choice as a cost-sensitive selection problem under a fixed compute budget. The central question is whether cross-task joint optimization outperforms staged supervision for a given target. We operationalize this by defining a small set of measurable dataset descriptors and resource constraints. Our analysis aggregates results over families of classification and span selection tasks. The goal is to predict the better strategy with minimal pilot training. We complement prior error analyses with per-example confidence calibration measures. We also quantify the interaction between target data size and inductive bias strength. Related work on curriculum learning emphasizes schedule design for intermediate tasks (Zhang and Hashimoto, 2021; Jiang et al., 2018). Studies of auxiliary loss weighting provide heuristics for balancing gradients in MTL (Sener and Koltun, 2018; Kendall et al., 2018). Research on task routing explores sparse adapters and modular subnets to isolate interference (Stickland and Murray, 2019; Pfeiffer et al., 2021). Our framing differs by making the selection itself the prediction target. We discuss how prediction uncertainty can guide fallback to standard fine-tuning. We highlight that compute-aware selection can yield outsized gains under tight budgets. Empirically, we use publicly available text classification and QA datasets. We control for model size and optimizer settings while varying supervision structure. We report both aggregate accuracy and a regret metric relative to the oracle strategy. To ensure robustness, we average over multiple seeds and stratify by domain. We release code and precomputed descriptors to facilitate reuse. The contributions are a formalization of the selection problem, a compact predictor, and an extensive evaluation. Limitations include the focus on encoder-only architectures and English datasets. We anticipate future extensions to multilingual and generation tasks. Finally, we outline open questions on scaling laws for transfer strategy selection. These questions include how descriptor quality interacts with model scale and domain shift. We also speculate about adaptive online selection during training warmup. We close by situating our findings within broader efforts to make transfer learning more predictable.

text: Recent literature on neural sequence modeling spans a wide spectrum of tasks and architectures, including attention-based encoders for MT (Bahdanau et al., 2015), copy-augmented decoders for summarization (See et al., 2017), and pre-trained transformers used for conditional generation (Raffel et al., 2020). Smith and Johnson (2021) surveyed multilingual pretraining for downstream tasks across news and conversational domains. Large-scale synthetic data generation has been explored to augment parallel corpora (Sennrich et al., 2016). Chen et al. (2020) proposed curriculum learning schedules for sequence-to-sequence models. Zhou et al. (2021) evaluated length control methods for abstractive summaries on English benchmarks. Singh et al. (2019) analyzed domain shift effects in encoder-decoder training with subword tokenization strategies.

text: Prior work on latent variable models addresses diverse concerns, including posterior collapse in VAEs for text (Bowman et al., 2016), hierarchical latent structures for document modeling (Miao et al., 2016), and discrete latent variables trained via straight-through estimators (Jang et al., 2017). He et al. (2019) studied mutual information objectives to encourage informative latents in conditional generation. Zhao et al. (2017) introduced dialog VAEs with auxiliary bag-of-words losses. Kim et al. (2018) investigated cyclical annealing schedules to mitigate KL vanishing. Xiao et al. (2022) presented controllable generation with attribute-aligned priors. Park and Choi (2020) benchmarked inference networks on semi-supervised text classification using variational methods. Li et al. (2019) compared amortized and iterative inference for sequence transduction.

text: Vision-and-Language Pretraining. Vision-andlanguage pretraining has made great progress in recent years. Inspired by BERT (Devlin et al., 2018), much work has extended it to process visual tokens and pretrain on large-scale image-text pairs for learning generic visio-linguistic representations. Previous research introduces one-stream BERT models and two-stream BERT models. The former directly perform inter-modal grounding (Li et al., 2019;Su et al., 2019;Alberti et al., 2019;Li et al., 2020a;Zhou et al., 2020;Li et al., 2020b), while two-stream models process both visual and textual inputs in separate streams, and then fuse the two modalities in a later stage . These models are often pretrained with self-supervised objectives akin to those in BERT: masked language modeling, masked object classification, and sentence-image alignment. In this work, the architecture of the ProbES model is structural similar to ViLBERT . We make several VLN-specific adaptations to ViLBERT so that pretrained weights can be transferred to initialize large portions of the model. Different from VLN-BERT which finetunes a ViLBERT on instruction-trajectory pairs to measure their compatibility in beam search setting, we introduce prompt tuning, which only tunes the continuous prompts.

text: VIST-Human Evaluation Several VIST generation models use human evaluation to evaluate model performance. Recent studies apply aspectbased rating evaluation. Hu et al. (2020b) and Wang et al. (2020b) ask workers to rate stories based on pre-defined aspects. 1 However, it is difficult to normalize these aspects as the definition of aspect varies from paper to paper. Also, these aspects are not mutually independent, making it difficult to analyze results based on these ratings. Therefore, we consider the ranking method as it is commonly used (Hsu et al., 2020;Wang et al., 2020b;Hsu et al., 2021a)  Automatic Metrics Automatic evaluation metrics are widely used in language generation tasks. Most reference-based metrics (e.g., BLEU (Papineni et al., 2002), METEOR (Banerjee and Lavie, 2005), and ROUGE (Lin, 2004)) evaluate the ngram similarity between a generated text and the reference. However, referenced metrics correlate poorly with human judgment (Wang et al., 2018b;Hsu et al., 2019;Modi and Parde, 2019) in dialog generation and story generation tasks: the generated text is given unreasonable scores due to incongruity with the reference. To account for this, several reference-free metrics (Sinha et al., 2020;Guan and Huang, 2020) have been designed to measure generated texts without any reference. BERT-Score (Zhang et al., 2019), for instance, uses contextual embedding to calculate the similarity between candidates and references, and BLEURT (Sellam et al., 2020) uses referenced automatic metrics as supervision signals for pretraining and is fine-tuned on a human judgment evaluation dataset. UNION (Guan and Huang, 2020) uses pre-defined negative samples to train a model in an attempt to provide a metric that specializes in story generation. In our analysis, current metrics remain unable to mimic human judgment to discern quality differences in story pairs.

text: Human values are of concern to most if not to all social sciences (Rokeach, 1973) and have also been integrated into computational frameworks of argumentation (Bench-Capon, 2003). In NLP, values have been analyzed for personality profiling (Maheshwari et al., 2017), but not yet for argument mining, as considered here. Recent scholarship has examined moral foundations in social media debates (Haidt and Graham, 2007; Hoover et al., 2020). Value-sensitive design introduces methods to operationalize stakeholder values in technology assessment (Friedman et al., 2008). Work on cultural dimensions explores cross-national value differences (Hofstede, 2001). Studies in political communication document framing effects that align messages with audience values (Chong and Druckman, 2007). To situate our contribution, we review approaches that connect values to textual claims and reasoning. Early work linked value lexicons to persuasive outcomes, but lacked argument structure (Somasundaran and Wiebe, 2010). Subsequent research incorporated stance and sentiment to approximate evaluative dimensions in debates (Mohammad et al., 2016). Argument mining has progressed from claim–premise detection to relation classification and joint modeling (Stab and Gurevych, 2017; Eger et al., 2017). Yet the explicit mapping from argumentative components to value categories remains underexplored. Recent datasets annotate value appeals in political corpora, enabling supervised modeling at scale (Pryzant et al., 2021). Neural encoders augmented with moral or value features have shown gains on persuasion prediction (Alhindi et al., 2021). Cross-domain robustness is limited because value expressions vary by genre and community norms. Our work addresses these gaps by introducing a corpus with fine-grained value labels aligned to argument structure. We also propose a modeling pipeline that disentangles argumentative role from value category to improve generalization. Finally, we outline evaluation protocols that isolate value detection from overall argument quality, facilitating reproducible comparison across methods.

text: Several data augmentation methods have been introduced for (compositional) semantic parsing. Jia and Liang (2016) recombined data by SCFG, and Andreas (2019) used some simple rules to exchange tokens with the same context. However, they leveraged only superficial grammars or rules, which has limited capacity to identify complex structures. Akyürek et al. (2020) learned to recombine and resample data with a prototype-based generative model, instead of using rules. Certain substructure substitution methods have been explored for data augmentation in other NLP tasks (Shi et al., 2021). Dependency tree cropping and rotation within sentence was used in low-resource language POS tagging ( Şahin and Steedman, 2019) and dependency parsing (Vania et al., 2019). Dependency tree swapping was explored in low-resource language dependency parsing (Dehouck and Gómez-Rodríguez, 2020). However, subtree substitution with finegrained meaning functions has not been examined. Recent work on augmentation for semantic parsing also intersects with structured meaning representations in vision-language settings (Krishnamurthy et al., 2017). Data programming paradigms provide weak supervision through labeling functions that can be composed to generate training signals (Ratner et al., 2017). Studies on paraphrase generation with back-translation report gains on sequence transduction benchmarks (Sennrich et al., 2016). To the best of our knowledge, we are the first to explore tree manipulation for semantic parsing.

text: Another related work is SUPERB (Yang et al., 2021), a benchmark to examine the capability of pre-trained models such as HuBERT (Hsu et al., 2021). SUPERB focuses on investigating a simple framework to learn SUPERB tasks with a frozen and shared pre-trained encoder and lightweight prediction modules fine-tuned for each task. In contrast, the goal of SpeechT5 is to learn all spoken language processing tasks by fine-tuning a unifiedmodal encoder-decoder model, which is pre-trained on unlabeled speech and text corpus. Recent advances in multimodal pre-training have explored audio-visual correspondence and cross-modal retrieval (Alayrac et al., 2020; Radford et al., 2021; Zellers et al., 2021). CLIP-style contrastive objectives align images and text at scale, and video-language models capture temporal grounding for narrated actions. Prior work on self-supervised speech unit discovery emphasizes discrete representations for downstream efficiency (Baevski et al., 2020; Hsu et al., 2021; Chen et al., 2022). Several studies also investigate prompting for instruction following with large language models (Ouyang et al., 2022; Wei et al., 2022). Our work focuses on speech-text pre-training with a unified encoder-decoder that supports ASR, TTS, and spoken language understanding.

text:
In this section, we first go through some of the most related works for BERT compression in the literature and then review the few works that have used Kronecker decomposition for compression of CNNs and RNNs.
A central challenge in compressing BERT is balancing parameter reduction with retention of linguistic generalization across tasks.
Early pruning-based approaches demonstrated that many attention heads are redundant, prompting interest in structured sparsity that aligns with hardware accelerators.
Sanh et al. (2020) propose movement pruning to induce sparsity during fine-tuning, while Michel et al. (2019) show that a small subset of heads suffices for strong performance.
Quantization methods like Q8BERT (Zafrir et al., 2019) and post-training quantization with outlier-aware scaling (Shen et al., 2020) reduce precision without retraining overhead.
Knowledge distillation has proven particularly effective, with DistilBERT (Sanh et al., 2019), TinyBERT (Jiao et al., 2020), and MobileBERT (Sun et al., 2020) achieving strong accuracy-speed trade-offs through layer-wise and attention-map guidance.
Factorization-based methods decompose large projection matrices to reduce compute; ALBERT (Lan et al., 2020) shares parameters across layers and factorizes embeddings to shrink memory footprints.
Noach and Goldberg (2020) study low-rank adaptations that leverage singular value truncation to retain dominant subspaces in self-attention.
Prior work on model compression for Transformers has focused on four main directions: pruning (Sanh et al., 2020; Michel et al., 2019), quantization (Zafrir et al., 2019; Shen et al., 2020), distillation (Sanh et al., 2019; Jiao et al., 2020; Sun et al., 2019), and low-rank factorization (Noach and Goldberg, 2020; Lan et al., 2020). Pruning studies report that a substantial fraction of attention heads and intermediate neurons can be removed with minimal accuracy loss, while structured strategies offer deployment benefits in real-time settings. Quantization methods reduce precision to 8-bit or lower while preserving calibration stability and mitigating activation outliers via clipping or per-channel scaling. Distillation compresses knowledge from a large teacher into a smaller student and has been extended to task-agnostic pretraining and multi-task settings. Low-rank factorization techniques approximate weight matrices to reduce parameters and enable faster inference, with careful initialization to control spectral drift.
Beyond BERT, tensor decompositions offer complementary avenues for compression by exploiting multilinear structure in weights.
Kronecker and related tensor factorizations decompose large matrices into products of smaller structured factors, enabling both parameter and FLOP reductions.
For CNNs, KFC layers approximate dense or convolutional kernels via Kronecker products, achieving notable speedups on ImageNet-scale models (Denton et al., 2014; Ioannou et al., 2016).
In RNNs, Kronecker-factored recurrent matrices maintain expressivity while reducing state-transition costs, with studies reporting stable training and improved throughput (Moczulski et al., 2016; Tjandra et al., 2017).
Recent work explores hybrid Kronecker–low-rank parametrizations that mix coarse-grained structure with fine-grained adaptability to minimize reconstruction error under tight budgets.
These tensorized schemes naturally align with block-structured hardware kernels, facilitating parallelization and cache-friendly memory access patterns.
Applying Kronecker decomposition to Transformers targets the dominant projection layers in attention and feed-forward blocks, where matrix dimensions are amenable to factored tilings.
Several studies report that Kroneckerized attention projections preserve head diversity while yielding consistent latency gains on edge devices.
Open questions remain regarding how to schedule factor sizes across layers, how to combine Kronecker structure with quantization-aware training, and how to regularize factor spectra.
We build on these insights by proposing a Kronecker-structured parameterization of BERT that jointly optimizes factor shapes with task objectives.
Our formulation supports mixed precision and integrates with standard distillation losses to stabilize training under aggressive compression ratios.
We evaluate on GLUE and SQuAD, comparing against pruning, quantization, and low-rank baselines under matched latency constraints.
Ablations examine factor shape search, initialization strategies, and the interaction between Kronecker depth and residual pathways.
We conclude with a discussion of deployment considerations, including kernel fusion and calibration procedures for post-training quantization.

text: Knowledge Distillation Recently, many attempts have been made to accelerate large neural networks . Knowledge distillation is a prominent method for training compact networks to achieve comparable performance to a deep network. Hinton et al. (2015) first introduced the idea of knowledge distillation to exploit the "dark knowledge" (i.e., soft label distribution) from a large teacher model as additional supervision for training a smaller student model. Since its introduction, several works (Romero et al., 2015;Zagoruyko & Komodakis, 2017;Tung & Mori, 2019;Park et al., 2019;Sun et al., 2019;Jiao et al., 2019) have investigated methods that align different latent representations between the student and teacher models for better knowledge transfer. In the context of knowledge distillation, MetaDistil shares some common ideas with the line of work that utilizes a sequence of intermediate teacher models to make the teacher network better adapt to the capacity of the student model throughout the training process, including teacher assistant knowledge distillation (TAKD) (Mirzadeh et al., 2020) and route constraint optimization (RCO) . However, the intermediate teachers are heuristically selected independently of the training process and the evolution of the teacher network is discrete. In contrast, MetaDistil employs meta learning to make the teacher model adapt to the current state of the student model and provide a continuously evolving meta-teacher that can better teach the student. Concurrently, Park et al. (2021) and Shi et al. (2021) propose to update the teacher model jointly with the student model with task specific objectives (e.g., cross-entropy loss) during the KD process and add constraints to keep student and teacher similar to each other. Their approaches makes the teacher model aware of the student model by constraining the teacher model's capacity. However, the teacher models in their methods are still not optimized for knowledge transfer. In addition, Zhang et al. (2018) introduced deep mutual learning where multiple models learn collaboratively and teach each other throughout the training process. While it is focused on a different setting where different models have approximately the same capacity and are learned from scratch, it also encourages the teacher model to behave similarly to the student model. Several studies explore orthogonal directions in model compression, including low-rank factorization (Denton et al., 2014), weight pruning (Han et al., 2015), and neural architecture search (Zoph and Le, 2017). Distillation has also been combined with data augmentation strategies (Furlanello et al., 2018). Curriculum learning has been used to schedule training difficulty (Bengio et al., 2009). Mixup regularization improves generalization by interpolating examples (Zhang et al., 2017). Different from all aforementioned methods, MetaDistil employs meta learning to explicitly optimize the teacher model for better knowledge transfer ability, and leads to improved performance of the resulting student model.

text: Recent advances on adversarial robustness for sequence labeling present diverse methodological choices that are not straightforward to unify across tasks, and several strands of research highlight different evaluation philosophies without clarifying their mutual relevance: Frei et al. (2022) propose span-level adversarial perturbations for entity boundaries, Pruthi et al. (2019) demonstrate that character-level noise compromises token classifiers, and Morris et al. (2020) catalog text attack transformations for classification benchmarks; Jia and Liang (2017) construct distraction-based attacks for reading comprehension, Ebrahimi et al. (2018) introduce HotFlip for character manipulations in neural machine translation, and Alzantot et al. (2018) generate semantically constrained adversarial text using population-based search.

text: Out-of-Domain Generalization. Generalization in machine learning is often defined as the ability to do well on a test set after learning from a training set, where all examples in both sets are drawn independently from the same distribution (i.i.d. generalization). However, as Gu et al. (2021) argue, in real-world applications such as semantic parsing, the test data may involve new compositional structures (compositional generalization), or new domains (domain generalization) that are not encountered during training. Existing work in compositional generalization for semantic parsing has focused on using synthetic datasets (e.g., Keysers et al., 2020;Lake and Baroni, 2018), or repartitioning existing text-to-SQL datasets into new train and test splits (e.g., Finegan-Dollak et al., 2018). Both approaches have generally shown that compositional generalization remains an important challenge (e.g., Shaw et al., 2021). We focus on the arguably even more challenging domain generalization problem, also known as domain adaptation, where entire domains may never be encountered during training or may only be encountered a small number of times (Motiian et al., 2017). Even though this problem has been studied extensively in the context of classification (Daumé III and Marcu, 2006), machine translation (Daumé III and Jagarlamudi, 2011), and question answering (Talmor and Berant, 2019), it remains underexplored for semantic parsing. However, to be applicable in real scenarios, semantic parsers should be able to generalize to new domains, since collecting domain-specific labeled data is often prohibitively expensive. Recent approaches have focused on data synthesis (Yin et al., 2021), meta-learning (Wang et al., 2021), relation-aware schema encoding (Wang et al., 2020), and encoder pre-training (Yin et al., 2020;Herzig et al., 2020;Yu et al., 2020;Deng et al., 2021). In this paper, we hone in on one aspect of domain generalization that we shall broadly refer to as column operations and which was introduced in §1 and illustrated in Figure . Johnson et al. (2017) study neural program induction with reinforcement learning, while Bogin et al. (2019) propose a semantic parsing model with graph-based encoders for schema linking, and Rajpurkar et al. (2016) introduce SQuAD as a reading comprehension benchmark.

text: Setting Recent multilingual translation researches suggest that adapting subnetworks for each language or language pair rather than for all of them gives better results. Li et al. (2020) train a shared multilingual model, then select layers for each language pair. Lin et al. (2021) also prune a shared multilingual model for each language pair, but on entries in weight matrices instead. Gong et al. (2021), on the other hand, prune attention heads and feedforward networks for each language. Xie et al. (2021) first identify general and language-specific neurons in a shared multilingual network, then tune those neurons using the data of their corresponding language only. These findings inspire our non-shared pruning setting and to see whether its results on multilingual translation could extend to multilingual pre-training.

text: Algorithm There are many structured pruning techniques proposed for monolingual pre-trained language models recently. Michel et al. (2019) propose a simple gradient-based importance score to prune attention heads. Prasanna et al. (2020); Liang et al. (2021) extend this method to prune other components like the feedforward network of the Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017). Wang et al. (2020c) decompose the pre-trained model weights and apply L 0 regularization (Louizos et al., 2018) to regulate the ranks of decomposed weights. Sajjad et al. (2020) study layer pruning and shows that directly dropping the top layers performs the best in fine-tuning. Peer et al. (2021)  3 Background: Transformer Encoder

text: Most of the recent taxonomy expansion approaches are based on hypernymy expansion. These methods attempt to determine the attachment position by scoring between several nodes. Recently numerous methods have been proposed to solve this problem (Shen et al., 2018), (Shen et al., 2020), (Yu et al., 2020b), (Zhang et al., 2021), . (Shen et al., 2020). Hence, all the existing taxonomy expansion approaches expand a taxonomy either by merge operation(synonymy expansion) or by attach operation(hypernymy expansion). Recent advances also discuss zero-shot entailment as a probe of hierarchical semantics (Yin et al., 2022). Prompting strategies for large language models modulate lexical generalization (Liu et al., 2023), and graph contrastive objectives improve structural alignment (Zhu and Sun, 2021). Distant supervision with pattern pruning appears in open IE (Angeli et al., 2015). Few-shot calibration reduces label bias in multi-class settings (Holtzman et al., 2023). However, particular to WordNet expansion it is an integrated task of Merge and attach operation. We are the first to study the problem of taxonomy expansion using both the Attach and Merge taxonomy expansion operations in a single model. A detailed related study is in Section A of the Appendix.

text: Among all the compression techniques, creating a fixed-size small version of large models along with distillation has been popular in the recent years. Sanh et al. (2019) introduced DistillBERT, which was a smaller version of BERT trained with distillation for general purposes. Another compact variant of BERT was proposed by Mobile-BERT (Sun et al., 2020) in which inverted bottleneck structures and progressive knowledge transfer were used. TinyBERT (Jiao et al., 2020) also presented a novel two-stage transformer distillation for both pre-training and task-specific fine-tuning. In (Iandola et al., 2020), the usage of grouped convolutions was studied to design SqueezeBERT. ELM (Jiao et al., 2021), a layer mapping search framework, was also proposed for improving downstream BERT distillation. A recent method, Ghost-BERT , employed softmaxnormalized 1D convolutions as ghost modules to generate more features with cheap operations.

text: Recent work has also explored style transfer in broader NLP pipelines that combine attribute control with content preservation across domains, including sentiment, formality, and politeness, and several papers report improvements from disentangled representations and adversarial training (Fu et al., 2018; Shen et al., 2017; Prabhumoye et al., 2018). Multiple studies consider lexicon-level constraints and semantic similarity metrics to maintain meaning after style modification (Mir et al., 2019; Krishna et al., 2020). Data augmentation for low-resource stylistic attributes has been studied. Reinforcement learning has been used to optimize non-differentiable style objectives (Ziegler et al., 2019).

text: Fine-tuning of Language Models. Better finetuning of language models can be achieved by proper initialization (Dodge et al., 2020), regularization  or prompts (Schick and Schütze, 2021). Other tricks include bias correction in optimizer and re-initialization of top layers in Transformer (Zhang et al., 2021). Instead of fine-tuning all parameters in a model, other work explore only learning a few vectors (Lester et al., 2021;Li and Liang, 2021;Guo et al., 2021) or a few additional parameters (Houlsby et al., 2019). Hallucination Methods. Feature Hallucination of examples is first introduced for visual recognition (Hariharan and Girshick, 2017) by metalearning (Wang et al., 2018), variational inference (Luo et al., 2021;Lazarou et al., 2022), and adversarial learning Tjio et al., 2022). Label Hallucination (Jian and Torresani, 2022) assigns soft pseudo-labels for unlabelled images to extend the fine-tuning few-shot dataset. Few-shot Language Models. Few-shot learning has been successfully applied to machine translation (Arthaud et al., 2021), abstract summarizing (Fabbri et al., 2021), question and answering (Hua et al., 2020;Ram et al., 2021), and entity recognition (de Lichy et al., 2021;Tong et al., 2021;Ding et al., 2021), by meta learning (Li and Zhang, 2021;Bansal et al., 2020;Sharaf et al., 2020), data augmentation (Wei et al., 2021;Wei and Zou, 2019;Karimi et al., 2021b), and prompts (Gao et al., 2021;Tam et al., 2021).

text: There have been a number of interesting attempts, that were aimed at model compression for Transformers, which can be broadly categorized into three directions. The first line of work focus on the redundancy of model parameters. Structure pruning (McCarley, 2019;Michel et al., 2019;Voita et al., 2019;Wang et al., 2019b;Fan et al., 2019;Wu et al., 2021a), which removes coherent groups of weights to preserve the original structure of the network, is one common strategy. In addition, various types of distillation techniques (Sanh et al., 2019;Jiao et al., 2019;Wang et al., 2020b) have been proposed to remove encoders by training a compact Transformer to reproduce the output of a larger one. Sanh et al. (2019) trained a smaller BERT variant with task-specific supervision, and Jiao et al. (2019) compressed multi-layer representations into a compact student for sequence labeling and QA. Wang et al. (2020b) explored multi-teacher aggregation for cross-task transfer, while convolutional student networks have also been considered for latency-critical settings (Sun et al., 2020). Other strategies include weight quantization (Bhandare et al., 2019;Zafrir et al., 2019;Shen et al., 2020;Fan et al., 2020) and weight sharing (Dehghani et al., 2018;Lan et al., 2019). The second line of work focus on reducing the quadratic operation of the self-attention matrices. The quadratic time and space complexity of the attention mechanism with respect to the input sequence serves the main efficiency bottleneck of Transformers, and thus is prohibitively expensive for training long-sequence data. One popular approach is to sparsify the self-attention operation by restricting each token to only attend a subset of tokens (Child et al., 2019;Kitaev et al., 2020;Ye et al., 2019;Zaheer et al., 2020;Beltagy et al., 2020). In particular, the most recent Big Bird (Zaheer et al., 2020) and Longformer (Beltagy et al., 2020) introduce sparse models which scale linearly with the input sequence. Another popular approach (Wang et al., 2020a;Choromanski et al., 2020) is to approximate the self-attention to reduce its quadratic complexity to linear, where the most recent Performers (Choromanski et al., 2020) provides an unbiased linear estimation of the attention matrices.
span_text: Sanh et al. (2019) trained a smaller BERT variant with task-specific supervision, and Jiao et al. (2019) compressed multi-layer representations into a compact student for sequence labeling and QA. Wang et al. (2020b) explored multi-teacher aggregation for cross-task transfer, while convolutional student networks have also been considered for latency-critical settings (Sun et al., 2020).

(2019) compressed multi-layer representations into a compact student for sequence labeling and QA. Wang et al. (2020b) explored multi-teacher aggregation for cross-task transfer, while convolutional student networks have also been considered for latency-critical settings (Sun et al., 2020).

text: The third line, which our work lies in, focus on the redundancy in maintaining a full-length sequence of token-level representation across all encoders (Dai et al., 2020;Wu et al., 2021b;Pietruszka et al., 2020;Ye et al., 2021;Kim and Cho, 2020). This work is particularly inspired by the sequence-level NLP tasks such as text classification and ranking, where the state-of-the-art approach utilizes a single embedding from the top encoder layer of a Transformer, such as the CLS token, for prediction. Under such regime, retaining the full-length sequence till the last encoder creates unnecessary complexity. Dai et al. (2020) applied a simplest strided mean pooling to each sliding window of the sequence to gradually compress tokens. The paper proposed a specialized pre-training procedure for this process that achieves a good balance between speedup and accuracy drop. Dai et al. (2020) used masked language modeling objectives adapted to pooled sequences to better preserve semantics during compression, and reported stable gains on GLUE. Wu et al. (2021b) benchmarked centroid schemes on document classification with balanced accuracy and throughput, and domain-matched pretraining corpora were shown to further stabilize convergence for compressed models (Gururangan et al., 2020). Ye et al. (2021) studied policy gradients with reward shaping to mitigate sparsity in token selection. In comparison we aim to avoid the costly pre-training step and focus on a method that requires only a lightweight fine-tuning. Wu et al. (2021b) define a centroid attention operator that given centroids, computes their embeddings via an attention mechanism. These centroids are chosen either as a random subset of the original tokens, or via strided mean pooling. This results in a similar technique to that of Dai et al. (2020), in terms of having a naive sequence length reduction method. Pietruszka et al. (2020) provide a variant of length 2 strided mean pooling where instead of taking the unweighted average of each pair, they provide learnable weights via a differentiable linear function. In our experiment we did not compare our methods with (Wu et al., 2021b;Pietruszka et al., 2020) since both worked on a limited (non-standard) collection of datasets and at the time of writing this paper, did not provide code allowing us to reproduce their result. Since our paper is focused on techniques to select a subset of tokens and these papers use either random sampling or pooling, we do not believe a thorough comparison is required. Ye et al. (2021) proposed a reinforcement-learning based technique to rank the tokens, thereby allowing it to remove the least important ones. This RL policy must be trained in an expensive process that requires the full network structure. Since we focus on methods to improve both training and inference, we do not include it in our experiments. Vision Transformers (Pan et al., 2021;Heo et al., 2021), which apply various types of pooling techniques to reduce input length, is specially designed for image data and thus non-trivial to compare with our method. We developed an attention based mechanism to progressively eliminate tokens in the intermediate encoders in the fine-tuning, while maintaining the classification accuracy.

(2021b) benchmarked centroid schemes on document classification with balanced accuracy and throughput, and domain-matched pretraining corpora were shown to further stabilize convergence for compressed models (Gururangan et al., 2020). Ye et al. (2021) studied policy gradients with reward shaping to mitigate sparsity in token selection.

text: Continual Dialogue Learning Neural dialogue models (Mou et al., 2016;Xing et al., 2017;Zhao et al., 2017) can acquire various kinds of conversation skills from corpora, such as characterizing personalities (Qian et al., 2018;Zhang et al., 2018b), expressing emotion and empathy (Zhou et al., 2018;Rashkin et al., 2019), and retrieving knowledge Inductive Transfer Continual learning in terms of transferring inductive knowledge from pretrained models to downstream tasks can be categorized into feature-based, fine-tuning-based, and adapter-based (Ruder, 2019). We adopt the adapterbased approach that benefits from both pre-trained models and a small set of extra parameters for taskspecific knowledge. Unlike conventional adapters (Houlsby et al., 2019;Poth et al., 2021;Pfeiffer et al., 2021), knowledge in the proposed adapters will be used to further boost the learning of new dialogue tasks, whereas knowledge of each task is separated into general and task-specific parts to avoid knowledge interference. Madotto et al. (2020) also uses the adapters to acquire the conversation skills, but it does not consider knowledge transfer and interference between adapters. Recent advances in retrieval-augmented generation emphasize grounding responses in external memory to mitigate hallucination (Lewis et al., 2020; Izacard and Grave, 2021). Large-scale conversational pretraining has also been shown to improve few-shot adaptation across domains (Zhang et al., 2020; Roller et al., 2021). Task-agnostic meta-learning frameworks report rapid adaptation with minimal gradient steps (Finn et al., 2017; Nichol et al., 2018). Multi-domain dialogue state tracking benchmarks highlight schema generalization challenges (Rastogi et al., 2020; Zang et al., 2020). In this work, we focus on continual acquisition of heterogeneous dialogue abilities under parameter-efficient transfer. Our formulation decomposes skill learning into generalizable routines and task-specific refinements that can be scheduled across tasks without catastrophic forgetting. We design adapters whose shared parameters mediate cross-task transfer while private components preserve idiosyncratic behaviors. To evaluate knowledge accumulation, we consider sequential task suites covering persona-grounded chit-chat, empathetic response generation, and knowledge-grounded dialogue. We complement these with diagnostic probes that isolate interference and backward transfer. Our contributions are threefold. First, we propose a modular adapter design that factorizes general and task-specific pathways for conversational skills. Second, we introduce a training curriculum that interleaves replay with cross-task distillation to promote positive transfer. Third, we present a benchmark protocol for measuring stability-plasticity trade-offs in dialogue models. Extensive experiments on public datasets show improved forward transfer and reduced forgetting compared to strong adapter baselines. Ablations confirm that the factorized design is crucial for maintaining skill integrity across long task sequences. Finally, we discuss limitations in scaling to open-domain mixtures and outline directions for adaptive scheduling and memory augmentation.

Task-agnostic meta-learning frameworks report rapid adaptation with minimal gradient steps (Finn et al., 2017; Nichol et al., 2018). Multi-domain dialogue state tracking benchmarks highlight schema generalization challenges (Rastogi et al., 2020; Zang et al., 2020).

text: Recently, many studies have shown remarkable improvements through recognizing transitions of features between utterances in open-domain multiturn dialogues. Qiu et al. (2020) perceive transitions of emotion states for context modelling. Zou et al. (2021) propose a module to manage keyword transitions. Zhan et al. (2021) model external knowledge transitions to select a knowledge used for generation. In multi-turn empathetic dialogues, we consider emotions, keywords, and utterancelevel meaning (Gu et al., 2021) as important features of each utterance and propose a novel approach of recognizing feature transitions between utterances.

text: Research efforts on monolingual ED are extensive and varied. Hand-crafted, feature-based, languagespecific methods were the basis of early ED approaches (Ahn, 2006;Ji and Grishman, 2008;Patwardhan and Riloff, 2009;Liao and Grishman, 2010a,b;Hong et al., 2011;McClosky et al., 2011;Li et al., 2013;Miwa et al., 2014;Yang and Mitchell, 2016). More recent efforts have primarily made use of deep learning techniques such as convolutional neural networks (Nguyen and Grishman, 2015;Chen et al., 2015;Nguyen et al., 2016b), recurrent neural networks (Nguyen et al., 2016a;Sha et al., 2018;Nguyen andNguyen, 2019), graph convolutional networks (Nguyen andGrishman, 2018a;Yan et al., 2019), adversarial networks (Hong et al., 2018;Zhang et al., 2019b), and pre-trained language models (Wadden et al., 2019;Zhang et al., 2019a;Yang et al., 2019;Zhang et al., 2020;Liu et al., 2020). These works also discuss multilingual embeddings and transfer learning strategies that target cross-task generalization without specifying event schemas, and some studies present data augmentation using back-translation to improve trigger robustness, topic adaptation with unlabeled corpora, and curriculum schedules for stabilizing training across heterogeneous sources. Works on cross-lingual ED are not as prevalent and generally make use of cross-lingual resources employed to address the differences between languages such as bilingual dictionaries or parallel corpora (Muis et al., 2018;Liu et al., 2019) and, more recently, pre-trained multilingual language models (M'hamdi et al., 2019;Hambardzumyan et al., 2020;Majewska et al., 2021). Unlike these previous efforts, our method leverages unlabeled data to further refine the language-invariant qualities of the language models. We position our contribution within this line by emphasizing sample-efficient adaptation and robust transfer to low-resource targets. Adversarial Language Adaptation, inspired by models in domain adaptation research (Ganin and Lempitsky, 2015;Naik and Rose, 2020), has been successfuly applied at generating languageinvariant models (Joty et al., 2017;Chen et al., 2018). Related advances include multilingual pre-training with aligned subword vocabularies, zero-shot dependency parsing with parameter sharing, and cross-lingual summarization evaluated on newswire benchmarks, with reports on parameter-efficient tuning and consistency regularization across languages. Our method improves upon these approaches optimizing the adversarial training process by selecting the most informative examples from the unlabeled data. Concretely, we design a scoring mechanism that prioritizes instances with high discriminator uncertainty to accelerate feature alignment. We further integrate confidence-based pseudo-labeling to expand coverage without incurring significant noise. Extensive experiments across three language families demonstrate gains over strong cross-lingual ED baselines. Additional examples of downstream applications of cross-lingual learning are document classification (Holger and Xian, 2018), named entity recognition (Xie et al., 2018) and part-of-speech tagging (Cohen et al., 2011). For a thorough review on cross-lingual learning, we refer the reader to Pikuliak et al. (2021).

text: Predicting text gaze patterns has been studied extensively, often in the context of probabilistic (Feng, 2006;Hara et al., 2012;Matthies and Søgaard, 2013;Hahn and Keller, 2016) or token transition models (Nilsson and Nivre, 2009;Haji-Abolhassani and Clark, 2014;Coutrot et al., 2017). More recently deep language features have been used as feature extractors in modeling text saliency (Sood et al., 2020a; opening the question of their cognitive plausibility.

More recently deep language features have been used as feature extractors in modeling text saliency (Sood et al., 2020a; opening the question of their cognitive plausibility.

text: Eye-tracking signals for NLP Augmenting machine learning models using human gaze information has been shown to improve performance for a number of different settings: Human attention patterns as regularization during model training have resulted in comparable or improved task performance in tagging part-of-speech (Barrett and Søgaard, 2015a,b;Barrett et al., 2018), sentence compression (Klerke et al., 2016), detecting sentiment (Mishra et al., 2016(Mishra et al., , 2017 or reading comprehension (Malmaud et al., 2020). In these works, general free-viewing gaze data is used without consideration of the specific training task which opens the question of task-modulation in human reading. Prior studies also consider scanpath similarity metrics for scene viewing (Cristino et al., 2010), probabilistic generative models of fixation sequences in natural images (Mathe and Sminchisescu, 2013), and multi-scale saliency integration for video attention (Judd et al., 2009). Sequence labeling with eye-tracking has additionally explored fixation-derived features. The literature on gaze-contingent display paradigms reports effects on reading speed and comprehension (Rayner, 2009). Work on visual search highlights target prevalence effects and miss rates (Wolfe et al., 2007). Studies on audio-visual attention investigate cross-modal orienting and speech-driven gaze shifts (Hajimirza and Hsu, 2015).

text: Few-shot Learning is often tackled by meta learning (Li and Zhang, 2021;Bansal et al., 2020;Sharaf et al., 2020), data augmentation (Arthaud et al., 2021;Wei et al., 2021;Kumar et al., 2019), and prompt-based fine-tuning (Gao et al., 2021;Tam et al., 2021;Schick and Schütze, 2021). Basu et al. (2021) applies contrastive learning in their fewshot semi-supervised intent classification, by using supervised and unsupervised pairings to structure the embedding space. Recent studies on representation learning emphasize the importance of class-aware margins and temperature scaling to stabilize episodic training (Zhang et al., 2022;Thakur et al., 2021). Cross-domain evaluation further reveals brittleness when the task distribution shifts at test time (Triantafillou et al., 2019). To address such brittleness, researchers combine metric learning with transductive refinement on queries (Dhillon et al., 2020). Prior works explored transductive inference with label propagation on support–query graphs (Iscen et al., 2019), gradient-based meta-learners that adapt classifier heads rapidly (Finn et al., 2017;Raghu et al., 2020), and prototype-based formulations that average embeddings within classes (Snell et al., 2017). Liu et al. (2020) investigated task-conditioned feature modulation for robust adaptation across domains. Chen et al. (2019) proposed episodic pretraining to reduce the meta–pretrain mismatch. Training with hard negative mining improves embedding separation (Robinson et al., 2020). In NLP, prompt-based methods recast classification as masked token prediction, enabling efficient adaptation with task-specific verbalizers (Gao et al., 2021). Calibrated prompting mitigates label bias by reweighting the prior over label words (Zhao et al., 2021). Soft prompts and prefix-tuning further reduce the need for task-specific parameters (Lester et al., 2021;Li and Liang, 2021). Data augmentation complements prompting by expanding the support set with paraphrases and synthetic labels (Wei and Zou, 2019;Anaby-Tavor et al., 2020). Back-translation and round-trip paraphrasing provide diverse lexical variants while preserving intent semantics (Sennrich et al., 2016;Xie et al., 2020). Semi-supervised techniques such as consistency regularization can exploit unlabeled queries to refine decision boundaries (Sohn et al., 2020). In vision, manifold mixup and meta-regularization enhance generalization under distribution shift (Verma et al., 2019;Baik et al., 2020). Domain-specific priors, including class descriptors or attribute vectors, serve as side information to guide adaptation when labels are scarce (Xian et al., 2018). Multi-task pretraining on heterogeneous episodes aligns feature spaces across related tasks (Gidaris et al., 2019). Another line of work studies uncertainty estimation for reliable prediction in low-data regimes (Malinin and Gales, 2018;Fort et al., 2020). Calibration-aware losses help prevent overconfident errors on unseen classes (Guo et al., 2017;Mukhoti et al., 2020). Few-shot calibration can be further improved with temperature scaling conditioned on support statistics (Zhao et al., 2020). Memory-augmented networks cache past instances to enable rapid retrieval-based adaptation (Santoro et al., 2016;Kaiser et al., 2017). Retrieval can be combined with generative modeling to synthesize pseudo-support examples that densify the local neighborhood (Kumar et al., 2020). Graph-based methods encode relations among instances, allowing label propagation and structure-aware reasoning (Liu et al., 2019). From a theoretical angle, sample complexity analyses connect representation smoothness to generalization in episodic training (Baxter, 2000;Amit and Meir, 2018). Margin-based generalization bounds motivate temperature-tuned contrastive objectives (Wang and Isola, 2020). Connections to domain adaptation clarify when task shift undermines prototype stability (Ben-David et al., 2010). In language understanding, intent classification and slot filling benefit from joint modeling with constrained decoding to enforce label schemas (Zhang and Wang, 2016;Qin et al., 2021). Span-level constraints can be integrated with meta-learners via differentiable decoding layers (Niculae and Blondel, 2017). Pretrained encoders such as BERT and RoBERTa provide strong initializations that reduce episode variance (Devlin et al., 2019;Liu et al., 2019). Transfer from multitask natural language inference adds semantic control that helps disambiguate fine-grained intents (Conneau et al., 2017;Phang et al., 2018). Few-shot semantic parsing additionally uses compositional data augmentation to cover rare operator combinations (Loula et al., 2018;Yu et al., 2021). Our work targets few-shot intent classification with limited labeled support and abundant unlabeled queries. We adopt a metric-based backbone with contrastive pretraining to obtain clusterable embeddings. We then refine prototypes using transductive label propagation over a similarity graph derived from the queries. To reduce label bias in verbalizer-based classification, we apply calibration on the masked token probabilities. We finally integrate retrieval-augmented paraphrases to expand the support set in a label-preserving manner. Experiments on three intent benchmarks demonstrate improvements in both closed-set and open-set protocols. Ablations show gains from each component and consistent behavior under cross-domain shifts. The results highlight the complementary roles of pretraining, transductive inference, calibration, and augmentation in few-shot learning.

Training with hard negative mining improves embedding separation (Robinson et al., 2020).

text: Probing Multilingual MLM mBERT and XLM-R have successfully achieved excellent crosslingual transfer performance without using any parallel corpus. Researchers have wondered what the source of this cross-lingual ability is. Pires et al. (2019) examines the zero-shot cross-lingual transfer performance on NER (Pan et al., 2017) and part-ofspeech (POS) tagging. They believe this success comes from the shared anchor words between languages. Not coincidentally, similar conclusion is reached by (Wu and Dredze, 2019). However, this conclusion is proven inaccurate by (Conneau et al., 2020b;K et al., 2020). Their experiments show that the model still learns cross-lingual transfer ability on the corpus without anchor words at all. Besides, (Conneau et al., 2020b;K et al., 2020;Artetxe et al., 2020;Libovický et al., 2020;Muller et al., 2021) have analyzed the cross-lingual ability of multilingual masked language models in terms of language similarity, shared model parameters cross languages, model structure, training objectives, language marker. Pires et al. (2019) examines the zero-shot cross-lingual transfer performance on NER (Pan et al., 2017) and part-of-speech (POS) tagging, Wu and Dredze (2019) argue for shared anchor words, Conneau et al. (2020b) and K et al. (2020) dispute this by removing anchors, Artetxe et al. (2020) discuss typological distance, Libovický et al. (2020) analyze attention heads and language markers, and Muller et al. (2021) consider subword sharing and parameter locality. The results suggest that structure similarity and shared parameters between languages are crucial for cross-lingual transfer. In this paper, we focus on analyzing language struc-ture. We decompose it into constituent order, composition, and word co-occurrence and study the effect of each part separately.

(2020b) and K et al. (2020) dispute this by removing anchors, Artetxe et al. (2020) discuss typological distance, Libovický et al. (2020) analyze attention heads and language markers, and Muller et al. (2021) consider subword sharing and parameter locality.

(2019) examines the zero-shot cross-lingual transfer performance on NER (Pan et al., 2017) and part-of-speech (POS) tagging, Wu and Dredze (2019) argue for shared anchor words, Conneau et al. (2020b) and K et al. (2020) dispute this by removing anchors, Artetxe et al. (2020) discuss typological distance, Libovický et al. (2020) analyze attention heads and language markers, and Muller et al. (2021) consider subword sharing and parameter locality.

text: Non-Autoregressive TTS. Promising progress has taken place in non-autoregressive TTS systems to synthesize audio with high efficiency and high fidelity thank to the advancement in deep learning. A non-autoregressive TTS system maps the input text sequence into an acoustic feature or waveform sequence without using the autoregressive decomposition of output probabilities. Fast-Speech (Ren et al., 2019) and ParaNet (Peng et al., 2019) requires distillation from an autoregressive model, while more recent non-autoregressive TTS systems, including FastPitch (La'ncucki, 2021), AlignTTS (Zeng et al., 2020) and FastSpeech 2 (Ren et al., 2021), do not rely on any form of knowledge distillation from a pre-trained TTS system. In this paper, the proposed CUC-VAE TTS system is based on FastSpeech 2. Pre-trained Representation in TTS. It is believed that prosody can also be inferred from language information in both current and surrounding utterances (Shen et al., 2018;Fang et al., 2019). Such information is often entailed in vector representations from a pre-trained LM, such as BERT (Devlin et al., 2019). Some existing work incorporated BERT embeddings at word or subword-level into autoregressive TTS models (Shen et al., 2018;Fang et al., 2019). More recent work used the chunked and paired sentence patterns from BERT. Besides, a relational gated graph network with pretrained BERT embeddings as node inputs was used to extract word-level semantic representations, thus enhancing expressiveness.

text: To test if our method is applicable across tasks, we include the tasks of both single sentence and sentence-pair classification. Experimental results show that our method outperforms the leading competitors by a large margin. Network Pruning.  be reduced without sacrificing accuracy loss, such as magnitude-based pruning (Han et al., 2015) and ottery ticket hypothesis (Frankle and Carbin, 2018). (Zhu and Gupta, 2018) compared small-dense models and large-sparse models with the same parameters and showed that the latter outperforms the former, showing the large-sparse models have better expressive power than their small-dense counterparts. However, under the pretrain-and-finetune paradigm, pruning leads to overfitting as discussed. Knowledge Distillation (KD). As a common method in reducing the number of parameters, the main idea of KD is that the small student model mimics the behaviour of the large teacher model and achieves a comparable performance (Hinton et al., 2015;Mirzadeh et al., 2020). (Sanh et al., 2019;Jiao et al., 2020;Sun et al., 2020) utilized KD to learn universal language representations from large corpus. However, current SOTA knowledge distillation methods are not able to achieve a high model compression rate (less than 10% remaining weights) while achieving an insignificant performance decrease. Progressive Learning. The key idea of progressive learning is that student learns to update module by module with the teacher.  utilized a dual-stage distillation scheme where student modules are progressively grafted onto the teacher network, it targets the few-shot scenario and uses only a few unlabeled samples to achieve comparable results on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100. (Xu et al., 2020) gradually increased the probability of replacing each teacher module with their corresponding student module and trained the student to reproduce the behavior of the teacher. However, the performance on Transformer-based models of the aforementioned first method is unknown while the second method has an obvious performance drop with a low sparsity (50%).

text: Existing non-validation stop-criteria are generally based on statistical inference. Duvenaud et al. (2016) interpreted stochastic gradient descent in terms of the variational inference and proposed an estimation method for the marginal likelihood of the posterior, which was applied as an early stopping criterion. However, this method requires considerable computation for the Hessian, which is not practical in large models. Mahsereci et al. (2017) also proposed a gradient-related stopping method referred to as evidence-based stopping (EB). The EB-criterion is based on the fast-to-compute local statistics of the computed gradients. The criterion represents whether the gradients of the training samples lie within the expected range. Intrinsic dimensionality (ID), which refers to the minimum number of parameters required to represent a dataset, has been used for analyzing the training or redundancy of neural networks (Amsaleg et al., 2015). LID is a version of ID that estimates the subspace dimensions of the local regions. Lee and Chung (2021) found that LID works well as a stopping-criterion in several few-shot image classification datasets. Moreover, LID can be applied to unlabeled samples. Another method involves the pre-estimation of the the number of training epochs by training the model multiple times, such as cross validation (Choi and Lee, 2021); the model can stop at the pre-estimated (PE) stop-epoch when training all the labeled samples.

text: Hate countering. NLP has started addressing the phenomenon of the proliferation of HS by creating datasets for automatic detection (Mathew et al., 2021;Cao et al., 2020;Kumar et al., 2018;Hosseinmardi et al., 2015;Waseem, 2016;Burnap and Williams, 2016). Several surveys provide a review on the existing approaches on the topic (Poletto et al., 2020;Schmidt and Wiegand, 2017;Nunes and Fortuna, 2018), also addressing the ethical challenges of the task (Kiritchenko et al., 2020). Automatic detection of HS presents some drawbacks (Vidgen and Derczynski, 2020). First of all, the datasets might include biases, and the models tend to replicate such biases (Binns et al., 2017;Davidson et al., 2019;Sap et al., 2019;Tsvetkov, 2020). Moreover, the end goals for which HS detection is employed are often charged with censorship of the freedom of speech by concerned users (Munger, 2017;Myers West, 2018). In this scenario, NGOs have started employing CNs to counter online hate. CNs have been shown to be effective in reducing linguistic violence (Benesch, 2014;Gagliardone et al., 2015;Schieb and Preuss, 2016;Silverman et al., 2016;Mathew et al., 2019); moreover, even if they might not influence the view of the extremists, they are still effective in presenting alternative and non-hateful viewpoints to the bystanders (Allison and Bussey, 2016;Anderson et al., 2014).

text: Word Analogy Recognition in NLP Benchmarks for word analogy recognition (Turney et al., 2003;Mikolov et al., 2013b;Gladkova et al., 2016;Li et al., 2018a) examine mostly linear relations between words (Ethayarajh et al., 2019). Such analogies can often be effectively solved by vector arithmetic for neural word embeddings, such as Word2Vec (Mikolov et al., 2013a) and GloVe (Pennington et al., 2014). Recent studies (Brown et al., 2020;Ushio et al., 2021) also test such ability of pre-trained language models (PLMs) (Radford et al., 2019;Brown et al., 2020) on these benchmarks. An exceptional benchmark is , where they build a knowledgeenhanced analogy benchmark that leverages word sense definitions in a commonsense knowledge base (Ma and Shih, 2018). However, these benchmarks are mainly set up for evaluating learned representations, and few of them ever investigated the analogical reasoning skills for neural models. Thus, the goal of this work largely differs from this line of research, as we aim to build a knowledge-intensive benchmark to teach neural models analogical reasoning for correct thinking. We focus on relational patterns that require multi-hop semantic chains, capturing role-consistent mappings rather than surface-level proximity. Our benchmark is constructed from curated templates and human-authored variants to avoid lexical overlap shortcuts that enable spurious solutions. We incorporate sense disambiguation signals and typed constraints to ensure that candidate distractors are plausible yet non-isomorphic to the target relation. To prevent memorization, we stratify splits by relation schema and lexical domain, enforcing low overlap between training and evaluation. We also introduce calibration protocols that measure confidence and abstention behavior under distribution shift. The evaluation suite contains both closed-vocabulary and open-retrieval settings to probe reliance on external knowledge. We accompany the benchmark with a latent-structure probing task that diagnoses whether models encode consistent role bindings. Our experiments compare static embeddings, encoder-only and decoder-only PLMs, and retrieval-augmented variants under uniform decoding and prompting setups. We report granular metrics for relation consistency, distractor resilience, and justification quality as judged by blinded annotators. We release all data, code, and prompts to facilitate reproducibility and secondary analysis. Prior efforts on analogy structure learning explore symbolic and neuro-symbolic methods for mapping relational patterns, including rule induction and relation alignment (Hofstadter, 1995;Gentner, 1983;Mitchell, 1993). Large language models have been probed for compositional generalization across tasks such as arithmetic, program synthesis, and chain-of-thought prompting (Wei et al., 2022;Nye et al., 2021;Austin et al., 2021). Studies on evaluation robustness emphasize confound control via adversarial distractors and counterfactual data augmentation (Gardner et al., 2020;Kaushik et al., 2020;Zhao et al., 2021;Elazar et al., 2021). A parallel line of work catalogs lexical-semantic resources including WordNet, ConceptNet, and Wikidata for downstream reasoning (Miller, 1995;Speer et al., 2017;Vrandečić and Krötzsch, 2014). Taken together, these threads motivate a benchmark that explicitly targets structural analogy while guarding against superficial heuristics. We position our contribution as complementary to probing and robustness studies by unifying relational templates with distractor-aware evaluation.

text: Many recent studies use external knowledge to improve the ERC performance. KET (Zhong et al., 2019) is used as external knowledge based on ConceptNet (Speer et al., 2017) and emotion lexicon NRC_VAD (Mohammad, 2018) as the commonsense knowledge. ConceptNet is a knowledge graph that connects words and phrases in natural language using labeled edges. NRC_VAD Lexicon has human ratings of valence, arousal, and dominance for more than 20,000 English words. COSMIC (Ghosal et al., 2020) and Psychological (Li et al., 2021) improve the performance of emotion recognition by extracting commonsense knowledge of the previous utterances. Commonsense knowledge feature is extracted and leveraged with COMET (Bosselut et al., 2019) trained with ATOMIC (The Atlas of Machine Commonsense) . ATOMIC has 9 sentence relation types with inferential if-then commonsense knowledge expressed in text. ToDKAT (Zhu et al., 2021) improves performance by combining commonsense knowledge using COMET and topic discovery using VHRED (Serban et al., 2017) to the model.

text: Multilingual LMs pretrained via MLM, such as mBERT (Devlin et al., 2019) and XLM-R (Conneau et al., 2020), cover 100+languages and are the starting point (i.e. initialization) of Prix-LM. 15 With the notable exception of Calixto et al. (2021) who rely on the prediction of Wikipedia hyperlinks as an auxiliary/intermediate task to improve XLM-R's multilingual representation space for crosslingual transfer, there has not been any work on augmenting multilingual PLMs with structured knowledge. Previous work has indicated that off-the-shelf mBERT and XLM-R fail on knowledge-intensive multilingual NLP tasks such as entity linking and KG completion, and especially so for low-resource languages (Liu et al., 2021b). These are the crucial challenges addressed in this work.

text: Large-scale pre-trained language models (PLMs) have achieved great success in recent years. Despite their power, the inference is time-consuming, which hinders their deployment in low-latency scenarios. To accelerate PLM inference, there are currently two streams of work: (1) Compressing a cumbersome PLM through knowledge distillation (Sanh et al., 2019;Sun et al., 2019;Jiao et al., 2020), model pruning (Gordon et al., 2020;Michel et al., 2019), quantization (Shen et al., 2020), module replacing (Xu et al., 2020a), etc. (2) Selectively activating parts of the model conditioned on the input, such as Universal Transformer (Dehghani et al., 2019), FastBERT (Liu et al., 2020a), DeeBERT , PABEE , LeeBERT (Zhu, 2021), CascadeBERT (Li et al., 2021a), ElasticBERT (Liu et al., 2021a) and other similar methods (Elbayad et al., 2020;Schwartz et al., 2020;Liao et al., 2021;Xin et al., 2021;Sun et al., 2021). Zhang et al. (2022) investigate adaptive token dropping in multilingual settings. Clark and Gardner (2018) analyze attention sparsity patterns in reading comprehension models. Tay et al. (2020) discuss efficient transformers with sparse factorization and routing. Narang et al. (2021) present pruning-at-initialization techniques for dense Transformers. Wang et al. (2020) study early exiting behaviors under distribution shift. Different from these methods, our proposed HASHEE requires no internal classifiers (which imply extra parameters) and supervision, and therefore can be widely used in a variety of tasks and model architectures.
