text:
In this section, we introduce background knowledge about contrastive learning and our phrase encoder LUKE (Yamada et al., 2020).
Contrastive learning aims to pull semantically related representations closer while pushing unrelated ones apart in a shared embedding space.
Prior work on contrastive learning has evolved along several axes, including objective design, data construction, and encoder architectures. Chen et al. (2020) formalized instance discrimination with large-batch training and momentum encoders, while He et al. (2020) emphasized dynamic dictionaries for unsupervised feature learning. Gao et al. (2021) adapted contrastive objectives to sentence embeddings with simple augmentations and in-batch negatives. Khosla et al. (2020) extended the loss to supervised settings by grouping positives via label information. Xie et al. (2020) investigated the impact of data augmentations on representation robustness. Reimers and Gurevych (2019) proposed Siamese encoders for efficient sentence similarity scoring. Wang and Isola (2020) analyzed alignment and uniformity as principles underlying contrastive objectives.
Our focus is on phrase-level alignment, where positives are constructed from entity-linked spans and their contextual paraphrases.
We adopt an InfoNCE-style objective with temperature scaling and in-batch negatives, and we couple it with hard negative mining from a large phrase index.
Recent work on phrase-level representation learning spans entity-centric pretraining and compositional encoders. Yamada et al. (2020) introduced LUKE, which augments token embeddings with entity-aware representations derived from large knowledge bases. Peters et al. (2019) studied contextualized span representations for tasks requiring boundary-sensitive features. Zhang et al. (2021) explored phrase retrieval with dual encoders trained on weakly aligned corpora. Lee et al. (2019) proposed dense passage retrieval with bi-encoders for open-domain QA. Li et al. (2020) investigated span-level contrastive objectives that match mentions across documents. Beltagy et al. (2020) built long-context transformers for document-level reasoning. Guu et al. (2020) combined retrieval and language modeling to enhance factual grounding in generation. Jin et al. (2020) examined biomedical entity linking with domain-specific encoders and curated terminology banks. Guo et al. (2022) proposed multi-granularity token-phrase alignment losses on multilingual datasets, and retrieval augmentation has also been used to stabilize training under distribution shift.
We extend LUKE with a contrastive head that projects phrase and context embeddings into a shared space suitable for retrieval and clustering.
To generate training pairs, we link mentions using a high-recall dictionary and then derive paraphrases via back-translation and distant supervision from anchor text.
Our encoder uses a lightweight phrase pooling mechanism over LUKE’s token-level outputs to capture boundary-aware semantics with minimal overhead.
We evaluate on entity typing, slot filling, and phrase retrieval benchmarks, measuring both alignment quality and zero-shot transfer.
Ablations isolate the contributions of entity-aware pretraining, hard negative mining, and the phrase pooling design.
We also analyze calibration under domain shift by tracking embedding norm distributions and temperature sensitivity.
Finally, we discuss limitations around ambiguous mentions and noisy paraphrases, and we outline a data-centric roadmap for improving phrase alignment at scale.

In this section, we introduce background knowledge about contrastive learning and our phrase encoder LUKE (Yamada et al., 2020).

(2020) formalized instance discrimination with large-batch training and momentum encoders, while He et al. (2020) emphasized dynamic dictionaries for unsupervised feature learning. Gao et al. (2021) adapted contrastive objectives to sentence embeddings with simple augmentations and in-batch negatives. Khosla et al. (2020) extended the loss to supervised settings by grouping positives via label information. Xie et al. (2020) investigated the impact of data augmentations on representation robustness. Reimers and Gurevych (2019) proposed Siamese encoders for efficient sentence similarity scoring. Wang and Isola (2020) analyzed alignment and uniformity as principles underlying contrastive objectives.
Our focus is on phrase-level alignment, where positives are constructed from entity-linked spans and their contextual paraphrases.
We adopt an InfoNCE-style objective with temperature scaling and in-batch negatives, and we couple it with hard negative mining from a large phrase index.
Recent work on phrase-level representation learning spans entity-centric pretraining and compositional encoders. Yamada et al. (2020) introduced LUKE, which augments token embeddings with entity-aware representations derived from large knowledge bases. Peters et al. (2019) studied contextualized span representations for tasks requiring boundary-sensitive features. Zhang et al. (2021) explored phrase retrieval with dual encoders trained on weakly aligned corpora. Lee et al. (2019) proposed dense passage retrieval with bi-encoders for open-domain QA. Li et al. (2020) investigated span-level contrastive objectives that match mentions across documents. Beltagy et al. (2020) built long-context transformers for document-level reasoning. Guu et al. (2020) combined retrieval and language modeling to enhance factual grounding in generation. Jin et al. (2020) examined biomedical entity linking with domain-specific encoders and curated terminology banks. Guo et al. (2022) proposed multi-granularity token-phrase alignment losses on multilingual datasets, and retrieval augmentation has also been used to stabilize training under distribution shift.
We extend LUKE with a contrastive head that projects phrase and context embeddings into a shared space suitable for retrieval and clustering.
To generate training pairs, we link mentions using a high-recall dictionary and then derive paraphrases via back-translation and distant supervision from anchor text.
Our encoder uses a lightweight phrase pooling mechanism over LUKE’s token-level outputs to capture boundary-aware semantics with minimal overhead.
We evaluate on entity typing, slot filling, and phrase retrieval benchmarks, measuring both alignment quality and zero-shot transfer.
Ablations isolate the contributions of entity-aware pretraining, hard negative mining, and the phrase pooling design.
We also analyze calibration under domain shift by tracking embedding norm distributions and temperature sensitivity.
Finally, we discuss limitations around ambiguous mentions and noisy paraphrases, and we outline a data-centric roadmap for improving phrase alignment at scale.

text:
LAMA (Petroni et al., 2019), which starts this line of work, is a collection of single-token knowledge triples extracted from sources including Wikidata and ConceptNet (Speer et al., 2017). To mitigate the problem of information leakage from the head entity, Poerner et al. (2019) propose LAMA-UHN, which is a hard subset of LAMA that has less token overlaps in head and tail entities. X-FACTR (Jiang et al., 2020a) and mLAMA extend knowledge probing to the multilingual scenario and introduce multi-token answers. They each propose decoding methods that generate multi-token answers, which we have shown to work poorly on MedLAMA. BioLAMA (Sung et al., 2021) is a concurrent work that also releases a benchmark for biomedical knowledge probing. We provide a comparison between LAMA, BioLAMA and MedLAMA in terms of (# relations, # queries, avg # answers per query, avg # characters per answer) in the Appendix. We also discuss the coverage of relations specific to biomedicine and report per-relation accuracy to contextualize differences across domains.
Probing via Prompt Engineering. Knowledge probing is sensitive to what prompt is used (Jiang et al., 2020b). To bootstrap the probing performance, Jiang et al. (2020b) mine more prompts and ensemble them during inference. Later works parameterised the prompts and made them trainable (Shin et al., 2020b; Fichtel et al., 2021; Qin and Eisner, 2021). We have opted out prompt-engineering methods that require training data in this work, as tuning the prompts are essentially tuning an additional parameterised model on top of PLMs. As pointed out by Fichtel et al. (2021), prompt tuning requires large amounts of training data from the task. Since task training data is used, the additional model parameters are exposed to the target data distribution and can solve the set by overfitting to such biases. In our work, by adaptively finetuning the model with a small set of raw sentences, we elicit the knowledge out from PLMs but do not expose the data biases from the benchmark.
Biomedical Knowledge Probing. Nadkarni et al. (2021) train PLMs as KB completion models and test on the same task to understand how much knowledge is in biomedical PLMs. BioLAMA focuses on the continuous prompt learning method OptiPrompt (Zhong et al., 2021), which also requires ground-truth training data from the task. Overall, compared to BioLAMA, we have provided a more comprehensive set of probing experiments and analysis. Our evaluation includes proposing a novel probing technique and providing human assessments of model predictions. We further ablate multilingual transfer, answer length constraints, and calibration methods across MedLAMA settings. Finally, we outline open challenges in robust biomedical probing, including handling multi-hop relations, temporal qualifiers, and cross-resource inconsistencies.

text: Our contributions add to a large body of work on grounded representation learning. Much of this work augments language modeling objectives with images (Silberer and Lapata, 2012;Lazaridou et al., 2015;Kiela et al., 2017) and videos (Sun et al., 2019). In this work, we focus on representations that encode verb semantics. Prior work on verb learning has been conducted in the computer vision community, typically described as "humanobject interactions" (Regneri et al., 2013;Chao et al., 2018;Ji et al., 2019). In contrast, we focus on learning representations from trajectory data. We are not aware of any prior work that attempts to learn verb semantics from trajectory data. However, prior work in grounded language learning focuses on reasoning about objects through space and time in text (Aroca-Ouellette et al., 2021) and grounding to 3D objects with varying rotation (Thomas, 2019). Outside of NLP, models have been trained on trajectory data for applications like human motion path forecasting (Giuliari et al., 2021) and human activity recognition (Wang et al., 2018). Our work lies at the intersection of grounded language learning and spatiotemporal machine learning, using representations of trajectory data to study verb semantics. Recent advances in multimodal contrastive learning further highlight the diversity of alignment strategies across modalities (Radford et al., 2021; Jia et al., 2021; Mu et al., 2022). Joint training regimes for audio-text and video-text have been proposed with varying degrees of supervision and pretraining data scale (Alayrac et al., 2020; Zellers et al., 2021; Xue et al., 2022). Large-scale dataset curation practices emphasize weakly supervised labels and web-scale scraping to broaden coverage across domains (Schuhmann et al., 2022; Changpinyo et al., 2021). Benchmarks for fine-grained action understanding include localized temporal annotations and hierarchical verb-noun taxonomies (Goyal et al., 2017; Smaira et al., 2020; Damen et al., 2022). Object-centric representations have been explored through slot-based architectures to disentangle entities from backgrounds (Locatello et al., 2020; Kipf et al., 2021). Learned feature pooling methods aggregate token- or region-level cues across frames to support event retrieval (Gabeur et al., 2020; Bain et al., 2021). Evaluation protocols increasingly consider robustness to distribution shift and compositional generalization (Taori et al., 2020; Hendrycks et al., 2021). Studies have proposed cross-lingual multimodal alignment leveraging parallel captions and pseudo-parallel mining (Wang et al., 2021; Bugliarello et al., 2022; Thapliyal et al., 2022). Training-time debiasing techniques adjust sampling strategies to mitigate frequency skew in action labels (Tan et al., 2020; Zhao et al., 2020). Open-vocabulary recognition systems report improvements via promptable backbones and label space expansion (Menon et al., 2022; Minderer et al., 2022). We formalize trajectory-based verb grounding as a sequence-to-representation mapping problem where paths encode interaction dynamics. Concretely, we model agent-centric trajectories as time-stamped position and velocity tuples, and we learn latent codes that correlate with verb classes annotated from textual corpora. This formulation connects motion-induced invariances with lexical semantic regularities. We demonstrate that incorporating temporal curvature and contact events improves alignment with verb frames. Our approach is complementary to visual action recognition, which relies on pixel-space cues, by emphasizing kinematic structure. We evaluate on synthetic and real-world datasets that include manipulation and locomotion scenarios. Baselines include recurrent encoders, temporal convolutional networks, and contrastive objectives that align trajectories with verb labels. We report accuracy and clustering metrics to quantify verb separability. Ablations assess the impact of trajectory noise, sampling rate, and representation dimensionality. The results show consistent gains across domains, and qualitative analyses reveal interpretable subspaces tied to motion primitives. We release code and data to facilitate reproducibility and future comparisons.

text: All the existing methods listed above are designed for general opinion summarization and, thus, are not necessarily suitable for comparative opinion summarization, as shown in the experiments. Comparative Summarization There is a line of work on extracting comparative information from single/multiple documents. Lerman and McDonald (2009) defined the contrastive summarization problem and presented early work on the problem. Their method selects sentences so that two sets of summaries can highlight differences. Wang et al. (2013) developed an extractive summarization method for a problem of Comparative Document Summarization, which is to select the most discriminative sentences from a given set of documents. Bista et al. (2019) tackled a similar problem by selecting documents that represent in-cluster documents while they are useful to distinguish from other clusters.

text: Recent work has examined mitigation strategies such as low-rank adapters and temperature-scaled decoding to partially relax representational constraints (Ma et al., 2022; Xie and Salakhutdinov, 2023). Clark and Manning (2016) survey syntactic probing tasks for LMs, and BERTology research catalogs layer-wise information localization (Rogers et al., 2020). Neural machine translation benefits from shared subword vocabularies (Sennrich et al., 2016), and retrieval-augmented generation integrates external memories to boost factuality (Lewis et al., 2020). Models with prefix-tuning modify activations through learned prompts (Li and Liang, 2021), dataset curation practices address spurious correlations (Geirhos et al., 2020), and instruction finetuning leverages human feedback to align outputs (Ouyang et al., 2022).

text: In the case of implicit relations between EDUs without clear connectives, it is crucial to introduce sequential information from the context to resolve ambiguity. Feng and Hirst (2014) rely on linearchain CRF with traditional feature engineering to make use of the sequential characteristics of the context for discourse constituent parsing. However, they greedily build up the discourse structure and relations from bottom up. At each timestep, they apply the CRF to obtain the locally optimized structure and relation. In this way, the model assigns relation gradually along with the construction of the parsing tree from bottom up, but only limited contextual information from the top level of the partially constructed tree can be used to predict relations. Besides, at each time-step, they sequentially assign relations to top nodes of the partial tree, without being aware that those nodes might represent different levels of discourse units (e.g. EDUs, sentences, or even paragraphs). In contrast, we explicitly train our sequence labeling models on both intra-and inter-sentential levels after a complete discourse tree is constructed so that we can infer from the whole context with a clear intention of capturing different writing patterns occurring at intra-and inter-sentential levels. Alongside this line of work, recent neural discourse parsers have explored multi-task setups with rhetorical structure prediction, sentiment cues, and topic segmentation, and multiple studies have cataloged genre-specific signals in newswire, forums, and scientific writing without harmonizing these signals into a unified modeling hypothesis (Li et al., 2016; Braud et al., 2017; Liu and Lapata, 2018; Peters et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2021). These efforts span diverse supervision regimes and annotation guidelines, and they include investigations of cross-lingual transfer, domain adaptation, and representation sharing across layers of a transformer encoder; at the same time, related work on hierarchical attention for long documents examines scaling issues and positional encodings (Guzmán et al., 2019; Koto et al., 2020; Zaheer et al., 2020). A survey of discourse signals in dialogue and multi-party conversation emphasizes turn-taking features and pragmatic markers in task-oriented interactions, while studies on summarization leverage nucleus-satellite cues to improve content selection (Asher et al., 2016; Li et al., 2019; Xu et al., 2020). Prior work also evaluates cue scarcity in low-resource genres and investigates curriculum learning for relation labeling, and several datasets revise relation taxonomies while expanding span boundary conventions in multilingual corpora (Biran and McKeown, 2015; Morey et al., 2018; Kobayashi et al., 2019).

text: Text Knowledge enhanced methods. Recently, huge efforts on integrating knowledge into PTLMs have been made. One typical form of knowledge is a knowledge graph. There have been efforts of using knowledge graph to inject entity and relation representations, which are pre-computed from external source, into PTLMs (Zhang et al., 2019;Peters et al., 2019;He et al., 2020). Some other works try to retrieve or generate the sub-graph from the graph to solve the problem (Lin et al., 2019;Wang et al., 2020). Another existing form of knowledge is extra large-scale corpus. Works that use such corpus present knowledge-related pre-training objectives such as concept order recovering (Zhou et al., 2021), entity category prediction (Yu et al., 2020) and source of knowledge prediction (Wang et al., 2021). They are mostly focused on injecting world knowledge presented in text, rather than physical and visual commonsense knowledge that can be found in images. Recent studies on multi-modal transformers investigate cross-attention fusion between textual and visual streams (Chen et al., 2020; Kim et al., 2021; Li et al., 2022) without clarifying the dependency on prior textual pre-training. To situate our work, we review methods that augment PTLMs with structured and unstructured knowledge. Early approaches relied on fixed knowledge graph embeddings that were concatenated or gated into encoder layers. Subsequent methods developed retrieval components that identify task-relevant subgraphs at inference time. A parallel line of research introduced continual pretraining on domain corpora with entity-aware masking and span objectives. These strategies improved factual recall and entity disambiguation in information extraction and question answering. Nevertheless, they often neglect perceptual or procedural knowledge that is not well captured by text alone. Our study targets this gap by aligning textual representations with signals grounded in images and physical interactions. We consider knowledge graphs that encode affordances and spatial relations derived from annotated visual datasets. We also examine corpora that describe manipulation steps and outcomes in instructional narratives. By combining discrete relational structure with sequence-level descriptions, we aim to enhance reasoning about actions and object states. We prioritize approaches that remain parameter-efficient and compatible with standard PTLM checkpoints. Concretely, we extend a retrieval-augmented encoder with a lightweight adapter for multi-source evidence. The adapter conditions token representations on graph neighborhoods and visual descriptors. We then train with a curriculum that alternates between factual queries and commonsense action queries. This design encourages the model to unify symbolic links with perceptual cues. For evaluation, we adopt tasks that require grounded inference, including procedural QA and visual commonsense QA. We report metrics on answer accuracy, calibration, and retrieval faithfulness. In ablations, we isolate the contribution of graph structure, visual features, and corpus pretraining. Results indicate that joint conditioning yields consistent gains over text-only baselines. Error analysis shows improvements in object state transitions and tool-usage predictions. We also observe better robustness to distractor entities during retrieval. Finally, we discuss implications for scalable knowledge integration without exhaustive end-to-end finetuning. We release code, resources, and templates for reproducible experiments. Our contributions include a unified adapter, a mixed-source curriculum, and a benchmark protocol for grounded knowledge assessment. We anticipate broader impact in applications that require safe and reliable reasoning about the physical world. Future work will explore automatic curation of affordance graphs from weakly labeled images. We also plan to investigate continual updating mechanisms that preserve alignment across modalities. By advancing coherence between structured knowledge and perceptual signals, our approach moves PTLMs toward richer commonsense understanding.

A parallel line of research introduced continual pretraining on domain corpora with entity-aware masking and span objectives. These strategies improved factual recall and entity disambiguation in information extraction and question answering. Nevertheless, they often neglect perceptual or procedural knowledge that is not well captured by text alone. Our study targets this gap by aligning textual representations with signals grounded in images and physical interactions. We consider knowledge graphs that encode affordances and spatial relations derived from annotated visual datasets. We also examine corpora that describe manipulation steps and outcomes in instructional narratives. By combining discrete relational structure with sequence-level descriptions, we aim to enhance reasoning about actions and object states. We prioritize approaches that remain parameter-efficient and compatible with standard PTLM checkpoints. Concretely, we extend a retrieval-augmented encoder with a lightweight adapter for multi-source evidence. The adapter conditions token representations on graph neighborhoods and visual descriptors. We then train with a curriculum that alternates between factual queries and commonsense action queries. This design encourages the model to unify symbolic links with perceptual cues. For evaluation, we adopt tasks that require grounded inference, including procedural QA and visual commonsense QA. We report metrics on answer accuracy, calibration, and retrieval faithfulness. In ablations, we isolate the contribution of graph structure, visual features, and corpus pretraining. Results indicate that joint conditioning yields consistent gains over text-only baselines. Error analysis shows improvements in object state transitions and tool-usage predictions. We also observe better robustness to distractor entities during retrieval. Finally, we discuss implications for scalable knowledge integration without exhaustive end-to-end finetuning. We release code, resources, and templates for reproducible experiments. Our contributions include a unified adapter, a mixed-source curriculum, and a benchmark protocol for grounded knowledge assessment. We anticipate broader impact in applications that require safe and reliable reasoning about the physical world. Future work will explore automatic curation of affordance graphs from weakly labeled images. We also plan to investigate continual updating mechanisms that preserve alignment across modalities. By advancing coherence between structured knowledge and perceptual signals, our approach moves PTLMs toward richer commonsense understanding.
span_text: Works that use such corpus present knowledge-related pre-training objectives such as concept order recovering (Zhou et al., 2021), entity category prediction (Yu et al., 2020) and source of knowledge prediction (Wang et al., 2021). They are mostly focused on injecting world knowledge presented in text, rather than physical and visual commonsense knowledge that can be found in images. Recent studies on multi-modal transformers investigate cross-attention fusion between textual and visual streams (Chen et al., 2020; Kim et al., 2021; Li et al., 2022) without clarifying the dependency on prior textual pre-training.

text: Disentangled Representation Learning Recently, there has been a growing amount of work on learning disentangled latent representations in NLP tasks (Zhang et al., 2019;Hu et al., 2017;Yin et al., 2018). In this aspect, the most related work to our syntax-semantics decoupling method is the vMF-Gaussian Variational Autoencoder (VGVAE) model proposed by Chen et al. (2019). It is a generative model using two latent variables to represent semantics and syntax of the sentence, developed for monolingual setting and trained with paraphrases. It uses paraphrase reconstruction loss and a discriminative paraphrase loss to learn semantic representations and word order information for syntactic representations. We adapt this model to multilingual syntax-semantics disentanglement. We use bilingual sentence pairs to train our model with a cross-lingual reconstruction loss and semantic discrimination loss. To better disentangle semantics from complex and diverse syntax in multilingual PLMs, we introduce two additional syntax-related losses for incorporating POS tags and syntax trees. Recent advances in question answering under multilingual settings emphasize that pretrained encoders capture cross-lingual signals that can be surfaced with probing tasks (Artetxe et al., 2020; Pires et al., 2019; Conneau and Lample, 2019). Large-scale parallel corpora have been curated for evaluation in machine reading comprehension, often contrasting extractive and generative answer formats (Lewis et al., 2020; Clark et al., 2020). Tokenization granularity has also been examined as a factor in transfer efficiency, with subword regularization reported to increase robustness across scripts (Kudo, 2018). We position our work at the intersection of cross-lingual MRC and disentangled representation learning. Our primary goal is to assess whether separating syntactic and semantic factors improves zero-shot transfer on extractive MRC benchmarks. We contribute a training framework that jointly optimizes cross-lingual reconstruction and semantic discrimination while regularizing syntax via POS and dependency cues. We further analyze the role of syntactic distance between language pairs in modulating transfer gains. Our experiments span typologically diverse languages to stress-test the decoupling assumptions. We report detailed ablations over loss components, bilingual data sources, and alignment strategies. The results indicate that explicit syntax-guided disentanglement yields consistent improvements over strong mBERT and XLM-R baselines. We also find that the approach remains robust when parallel data is noisy or partially misaligned. Error analysis suggests that improvements are concentrated on questions involving long-distance dependencies and head-final structures. We release code, pretrained checkpoints, and training scripts to facilitate reproducibility. We additionally provide a multilingual diagnostic set targeting syntactic phenomena relevant to MRC. The diagnostic probes include control for answer length and clause embedding depth. We discuss limitations related to reliance on external taggers and parsers in low-resource settings. Future work may reduce supervision by leveraging unsupervised or weakly supervised syntax induction. This line of research could help unify cross-lingual transfer with structure-aware pretraining. Ultimately, our findings underscore the value of decoupling syntax from semantics for robust multilingual comprehension. By clarifying the contribution of structure, we aim to inform the design of next-generation multilingual PLMs. We conclude with open questions on scaling disentanglement to generative MRC and dialogue-oriented reading tasks.

text: Recent work examines bias in pretrained language models via counterfactual data augmentation (Zmigrod et al., 2019), intrinsic association tests (May et al., 2019), and causal probing frameworks (Vig et al., 2020). Hovy and Spruit (2016) discuss social impacts of NLP technology and dataset documentation practices. Bender and Friedman (2018) introduce a data statement protocol for transparency, and Buolamwini and Gebru (2018) study demographic disparities in face recognition performance.

text: Recent studies span diverse transfer paradigms in multilingual NLP, including prompt-based adaptation that conditions pretrained decoders on language tags (Winata et al., 2021), curriculum strategies that schedule target-language exposure during fine-tuning (Conneau et al., 2020), and lexicon-grounded objectives that inject bilingual signal through dictionary constraints (Artetxe et al., 2018). Multilingual pretraining scales to hundreds of languages with shared subword vocabularies and masked language modeling, and encoder-decoder models can be distilled into compact student networks for on-device use (Sanh et al., 2019). Signal alignment in speech-to-text pipelines also benefits from shared bottleneck representations between acoustic and textual modalities (Pratap et al., 2021).

text: This section reports a survey on closely related popular QA datasets that 1) focus on narratives and/or 2) are designed for educational purposes (dataset features in Table ) 1 . We begin by delineating narrative-centric QA from sentence-level reading comprehension, emphasizing discourse structure and event temporality. Narrative datasets often evaluate cross-sentence reasoning, coreference, and character modeling across long contexts. Educational QA corpora, by contrast, foreground curricular alignment, cognitive skill coverage, and item difficulty calibration. Prior work has cataloged these dimensions to clarify where datasets stress comprehension versus retrieval. Prior work on narrative QA is often contrasted with reading comprehension benchmarks that emphasize sentence-level fact retrieval; Hermann et al. (2015) introduced cloze-style queries over news, Rajpurkar et al. (2016) framed extractive QA on Wikipedia, and Kwiatkowski et al. (2019) scaled open-domain answering with heterogeneous sources. Hill et al. (2016) collected the bAbI tasks to test reasoning with synthetic stories. Most narrative QA settings instead require modeling long-range dependencies across events and characters in realistic texts. The BookTest corpus (Bajgar et al., 2016) extended cloze queries to novels. NarrativeQA (Kocisky et al., 2018) paired plot summaries and full books/films with abstractive questions and answers. DREAM (Sun et al., 2019) targeted multi-turn dialogue comprehension in exams. SQuAD-style adversarial sets have also probed robustness. Educational question generation has investigated didactic scaffolding and item difficulty. TextbookQA (Kembhavi et al., 2017) aligned multimodal textbook content with questions. RACE (Lai et al., 2017) aggregated English examination questions from Chinese middle and high schools. DuoRC (Saha et al., 2018) introduced paraphrase-heavy movie synopses requiring inference. To ground the survey, we first group datasets by narrative source: literary fiction, film synopses, dialogues, and news-derived cloze narratives. We then articulate educational orientation along three axes: assessment context, annotation policy, and cognitive taxonomy. For literary fiction, NarrativeQA stands out for its dual setting (summary versus full text) that separates surface recall from deep narrative understanding. For film materials, MovieQA (Tapaswi et al., 2016) integrates plots, subtitles, and videos, enabling multimodal narrative reasoning. DuoRC focuses on paraphrastic divergence between documents and questions, intentionally reducing lexical overlaps. For dialogues, DREAM tests pragmatic inference and speaker attribution in multi-turn contexts. For news-style narratives, CNN/DailyMail variants remain useful for cloze-based sequence modeling. Turning to education-focused corpora, RACE provides multiple-choice items spanning literal, inferential, and summarization skills. TextbookQA layers diagrams and figures with explanatory passages to reflect classroom resources. ARC (Clark et al., 2018) contrasts easy versus challenge science questions to probe knowledge integration. SciQ (Welbl et al., 2017) offers crowdsourced science items with distractors designed to mimic classroom exams. MCTest (Richardson et al., 2013) supplies small, carefully controlled stories for elementary-level comprehension. TOEFL Listening Comprehension sets introduce authentic academic discourse for non-native learners. Several datasets bridge both aims: QuAC (Choi et al., 2018) and CoQA (Reddy et al., 2019) model conversational comprehension while retaining educational relevance. TREC-style QA remains a reference point for taxonomy and evaluation protocol design. MultiRC (Khashabi et al., 2018) emphasizes multi-sentence reasoning with calibrated distractors. As evaluation practices evolve, leaderboard saturation has motivated adversarial splits and length-controlled partitions. We synthesize these developments by highlighting implications for modeling architectures, annotation design, and curricular validity. The survey concludes with recommendations for dataset selection aligned to narrative complexity and instructional goals.

text: Most modern language models are based on the transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017) architecture. The PLMs generally use only the encoder block of the transformer, which consists of two sublayers, a self-attention layer, and a feed-forward layer. BERT (Devlin et al., 2019) is the most widely used PLM, which consists of several layers of transformer encoders. The BERT was pre-trained for 4 days with a large amount of text data, which consists of 3.3 billion words, using masked language modeling and the next sentence prediction task in an unsupervised manner. This pre-trained parameter can be easily used in various downstream tasks by fine-tuning the model using the labeled dataset. Since the success of BERT, a variety of similar PLMs have emerged.

text: Explainability evaluation methods (Doshi-Velez and Kim, 2017) include measuring faithfulness (Jacovi and Goldberg, 2020), enabling model simulatability (Hase et al., 2019), behavioral testing (Ribeiro et al., 2020, and evaluating intelligent user interactions (Nguyen et al., 2018). Recent advances in prompt-based tuning highlight the role of instruction-following models for controllable explanations (Wei et al., 2022; Ouyang et al., 2022). Large language model alignment techniques prioritize helpfulness and harmlessness, often measured with preference data (Bai et al., 2022). Self-consistency decoding has been used to improve reasoning reliability on chain-of-thought benchmarks (Wang et al., 2023). Benchmark suites for interpretability stress-test saliency robustness and sparsity. Practical deployments report mixed evidence about whether explanations improve decision quality in high-stakes domains. Some studies frame explanation as a communication problem rather than a transparency problem. There is also growing interest in hybrid symbolic-neural rationalizers that combine program sketches with natural language. Foundational work on causal abstraction formalizes relationships between mechanistic and functional models (Beckers and Halpern, 2019). Crowdsourcing protocols calibrate user trust through contrastive and counterfactual exemplars. Study designs vary in task complexity, time pressure, and prior knowledge. Quality metrics often combine subjective ratings with objective task performance and error detection. Several datasets include rationale annotations that may bias models toward extractive patterns. User mental models evolve over repeated interactions, complicating between-subject comparisons.

text: There has been the argument revolving deep learning models that they are black-box and hard to offer explainable and interpretable predictions, which has led to a considerable amount of research work regarding their interpretability (Linardatos et al., 2021). The pre-trained BERT language model has been extensively analyzed since its launch, in particular, feature-based probes have been proposed to show how a particular layer, head, or neuron of BERT works on a downstream NLP task. Usually with a small set of additional parameters, a probe is trained in a supervised manner using feature representations from the pre-trained BERT, e.g. contextualized embeddings, to solve a particular task (Wu et al., 2020). Attention and structural probes have been invented to investigate different aspects of BERT and linguistics (Lin et al., 2019;Jawahar et al., 2019;Clark et al., 2019;Hewitt and Manning, 2019;Manning et al., 2020;Tenney et al., 2019a,b;Pruksachatkun et al., 2020). Latent ontology of contextual embeddings has also been investigated via cluster analysis (Michael et al., 2020). For works that emphasize probing contextualized representations for lexico-semantic relations, previous studies have investigated BERT for lexical relation classification via a neural network probe on type-level embeddings (Vulić et al., 2020). Researchers have also examined multilingual adaptations of BERT, domain-specific pretraining for biomedical and legal text, and prompt-based steering of masked language models to elicit latent relational knowledge (Devlin et al., 2019; Beltagy et al., 2019; Chalkidis et al., 2020; Petroni et al., 2019); knowledge graph completion methods compare structured inference against distributional cues, and few-shot prompting can expose parametric facts without fine-tuning (Bosselut et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2021; Brown et al., 2020). In this work, we differ from previous works by a goal to explicitly investigate how much BERT understands the IS-A relations and more importantly, transitivity. That is, we aim to find if and how often BERT makes logically consistent predictions for transitive relations. Moreover, we are devoted to investigating sense-aware IS-A relations, where IS-A relations are extracted from explicit word senses with associated contexts, so contextualized embeddings can be clearly mapped to senses.

text: Pretrained LM in Open-domain dialogue has received sustained attention as models have scaled and datasets diversified for multi-turn interaction research. Many prior works tried to pretrain the models on large-scale social comment chains data like Reddit to model conversational behavior (Zhang et al., 2020; Adiwardana et al., 2020), followed by finetuning on the diverse target dialogue dataset to improve engagingness and humanness. Prior work emphasizes large-scale pretraining on conversational corpora to endow models with discourse competence and pragmatic flexibility (Roller et al., 2021). Smith et al. (2019) introduced a multi-turn response selection benchmark focusing on context coherence and persona consistency. Zhang et al. (2018) proposed PersonalDialog to capture speaker traits and long-range dependencies across sessions. While these efforts improved surface-level fluency, recent studies highlight shortcomings in factual grounding and safety in open-domain settings (Shuster et al., 2021; Dinan et al., 2021). Retrieval-augmented dialogue aims to mitigate hallucination by conditioning generation on retrieved passages from web or curated knowledge sources (Lewis et al., 2020; Komeili et al., 2022). Safety-aware data construction further introduces refusal behaviors and sensitive-topic handling to reduce harmful outputs during both pretraining and finetuning (Baheti et al., 2021; Sun et al., 2022). Instruction tuning with preference optimization has emerged to better align chat models with conversational goals while maintaining factuality and politeness (Ouyang et al., 2022; Bai et al., 2022). To avoid undesired behaviors of the models including toxicity and bias from the human-human conversation, they merely exclude some parts of training data using automatic filtering by predefined criteria. Despite measurable gains, our work argues that filtering alone is insufficient without explicit safety objectives and grounding signals integrated throughout the training pipeline.

(2018) proposed PersonalDialog to capture speaker traits and long-range dependencies across sessions.

text: Introduction Synthetic translations are widely leveraged in neural machine translation to expand training signals when genuine bitext is scarce. Early efforts explored monolingual exploitation through forward translation of source-side corpora (Zhang and Zong, 2016) and through backtranslation of target-side texts (Sennrich et al., 2016a; Marie et al., 2020). These strategies increased domain coverage and lexical diversity without costly human annotation. Subsequent work refined sampling, decoding, and filtering to control noise in generated pairs. Backtranslation quality has been tied to iterative retraining cycles and domain-adaptive language modeling. Data curation pipelines now routinely balance synthetic and human-authored segments for stability. Synthetic augmentation has also been used to target low-resource transfer by pivoting through related languages. Researchers study how noise temperature and sampling affect rare token recall. Some approaches interleave synthetic and authentic batches to mitigate distribution drift. Adaptive schedules adjust the ratio of synthetic sentences across training epochs. Nguyen et al. (2020) diversify the parallel data via translating both sides using multiple models and then merge them with the original to train a final NMT model; Jiao et al. (2020) employ a similar approach to rejuvenate inactive examples that contribute the least to the model performance. Sequence-level knowledge distillation (Kim and Rush, 2016) can also be viewed as replacing original bitext with synthetic translations. Distillation reframes the objective by training on model-generated hypotheses that smooth the target distribution. This perspective aligns with findings that simpler targets can reduce exposure bias during decoding. Non-autoregressive systems are a prominent case where distilled corpora stabilize optimization. Gu et al. (2017) report substantial speedups with competitive accuracy under distilled supervision. Later studies integrate iterative refinement and latent alignment to close quality gaps. Researchers analyze how teacher decoding strategies shape student generalization. Empirical evidence indicates that beam size and length penalties modulate calibration. Studies also examine the interaction between backtranslation and distillation in joint curricula. Mixed regimes alternate synthetic targets from different teachers for robustness. While its original goal was to guide the training of a student model of small capacity with the output of a teacher of high capacity, distillation is also necessary to effectively train some categories of MT architectures such as non-autoregressive models (Gu et al., 2017). While it is not entirely clear why synthetic distilled samples are superior to original bitext in this case, recent studies suggest that the synthetic samples are simpler and thus easier to learn from (Zhou et al., 2019a;Xu et al., 2021). Despite gains, concerns remain about domain narrowing and stylistic homogenization. Recent work proposes round-trip filtering to retain only meaning-preserving synthetic pairs. Others advocate uncertainty-aware selection to downweight brittle hypotheses. Evaluation protocols now emphasize robustness across domains and noise profiles. Our study situates these threads by comparing curriculum schedules that interleave backtranslation and distillation under fixed compute budgets. We focus on practical settings where monolingual data are abundant but parallel data are limited. The proposed analysis isolates the effect of teacher diversity, decoding temperature, and synthetic ratio. We report results on standard benchmarks spanning high- and low-resource pairs. Findings highlight when additional synthetic volume saturates and when diversity continues to help. We release code and preprocessing scripts to support reproducibility. Finally, we discuss open questions around measuring semantic adequacy in heavily distilled corpora.

(2020) employ a similar approach to rejuvenate inactive examples that contribute the least to the model performance. Sequence-level knowledge distillation (Kim and Rush, 2016) can also be viewed as replacing original bitext with synthetic translations.

Sequence-level knowledge distillation (Kim and Rush, 2016) can also be viewed as replacing original bitext with synthetic translations. Distillation reframes the objective by training on model-generated hypotheses that smooth the target distribution. This perspective aligns with findings that simpler targets can reduce exposure bias during decoding. Non-autoregressive systems are a prominent case where distilled corpora stabilize optimization. Gu et al. (2017) report substantial speedups with competitive accuracy under distilled supervision. Later studies integrate iterative refinement and latent alignment to close quality gaps. Researchers analyze how teacher decoding strategies shape student generalization. Empirical evidence indicates that beam size and length penalties modulate calibration. Studies also examine the interaction between backtranslation and distillation in joint curricula. Mixed regimes alternate synthetic targets from different teachers for robustness. While its original goal was to guide the training of a student model of small capacity with the output of a teacher of high capacity, distillation is also necessary to effectively train some categories of MT architectures such as non-autoregressive models (Gu et al., 2017). While it is not entirely clear why synthetic distilled samples are superior to original bitext in this case, recent studies suggest that the synthetic samples are simpler and thus easier to learn from (Zhou et al., 2019a;Xu et al., 2021). Despite gains, concerns remain about domain narrowing and stylistic homogenization. Recent work proposes round-trip filtering to retain only meaning-preserving synthetic pairs. Others advocate uncertainty-aware selection to downweight brittle hypotheses. Evaluation protocols now emphasize robustness across domains and noise profiles. Our study situates these threads by comparing curriculum schedules that interleave backtranslation and distillation under fixed compute budgets. We focus on practical settings where monolingual data are abundant but parallel data are limited. The proposed analysis isolates the effect of teacher diversity, decoding temperature, and synthetic ratio. We report results on standard benchmarks spanning high- and low-resource pairs. Findings highlight when additional synthetic volume saturates and when diversity continues to help. We release code and preprocessing scripts to support reproducibility. Finally, we discuss open questions around measuring semantic adequacy in heavily distilled corpora.

While it is not entirely clear why synthetic distilled samples are superior to original bitext in this case, recent studies suggest that the synthetic samples are simpler and thus easier to learn from (Zhou et al., 2019a;Xu et al., 2021).

text: Evaluation with predicted history. Only recently has it been noticed that the current method of evaluating CQA models is flawed. Mandya et al. (2020); Siblini et al. (2021) point out that using gold answers in history is not consistent with the realworld scenario and propose to use predicted history for evaluation. Chen et al. (2020) analyze pretraining strategies for CQA encoders, and Qu et al. (2019) investigate dialog-level attention mechanisms for context tracking, while Kim et al. (2021) explore turn-level reformulation for error robustness. Different from prior work, in this paper, we conduct a large scale human evaluation to support our claims, identify the issues with predicted history, and propose rewriting questions to further mitigate the gap to human evaluation.

text: [Prior work on natural language inference often presupposes that extracted rationales directly support model predictions without clarifying how complementary evidence is reconciled across heterogeneous sources (Ribeiro et al., 2016; Chen et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2018). Camburu et al. (2018) study natural language explanations accompanying decisions. Talmor et al. (2018) examine multi-hop question answering over knowledge, and Kumar and Talukdar (2020) investigate graph-structured supervision for explainability.]

text: To avoid the direct computation on plain-text data, recent works like TextHide (Huang et al., 2020) and DP-finetuning (Kerrigan et al., 2020) introduce the classical federated learning and differential privacy (DP) to protect the sensitive data. However, TextHide (Huang et al., 2020) can only be applied to sentence-level tasks. Due to the mixup operation, TextHide fails to model token-level tasks like named entity recognition or semantic role labelling. DP-finetuning would greatly sacrifice the performance of fine-tuned model by 20% perplexity for a generation model like GPT-2. Recent studies explore privacy-preserving objectives for pre-training such as PATE-style aggregation for language tasks (Papernot et al., 2018), cryptographic approaches for secure aggregation in federated optimization (Bonawitz et al., 2017), and model auditing via membership inference risk estimators that quantify exposure in generative models (Carlini et al., 2022). We focus on practical configurations that maintain utility while offering quantifiable privacy guarantees. Our analysis considers the trade-off between privacy budgets and downstream accuracy under realistic fine-tuning regimes. We compare task families that stress token-level labeling against sequence-level generation. Prior work highlights that token-level gradients tend to leak more information than sequence-level gradients. We examine privacy amplification effects arising from subsampling in large-batch training. Our setting includes common pre-training corpora with PII redaction strategies. We evaluate attack surfaces for text reconstruction during inference. We study the interaction between clipping norms and optimizer dynamics in DP-SGD. Our experiments include ablations on gradient noise schedules across epochs. We additionally report robustness under distribution shift in the presence of privacy noise. We provide guidance on choosing hyperparameters that balance utility and risk. Our findings suggest that moderate noise can preserve NER performance. We also observe task-dependent sensitivity to μ and σ in privacy accounting. These observations inform deployment recommendations for sensitive domains. We release reproducible pipelines for DP-aware fine-tuning. We further discuss compliance implications for healthcare and finance. Overall, our work clarifies practical privacy baselines for pre-trained models.

text: Pretrained Language Models Contextual representations such as ELMo (Peters et al., 2018) and BERT (Devlin et al., 2019) are not just useful for monolingual representations. Multilingual BERT (Devlin et al., 2019, mBERT), XLM (Lample and Conneau, 2019), and XLM-RoBERTa (Conneau et al., 2020, XLM-R) have surprisingly high cross-lingual transfer performance compared to the previous best practice: static cross-lingual word embeddings (Pires et al., 2019;Wu and Dredze, 2019). Multilingual models are also practicalwhy have hundreds of separate models for each language when you could do better with just one? However, there's a catch. Conneau et al. (2020) reports the "curse of multilinguality": as the number of pretraining languages increases, the better the cross-lingual transfer is up to some point, after which the performance in zero-shot transfer starts to decrease. Our work complements Conneau et al. (2020) by explicitly dividing up between seen and unseen languages during pretraining and testing whether the "curse of multilinguality" is also an issue for unseen languages too. To ground our investigation, we consider standardized benchmarks in cross-lingual natural language understanding and structured prediction. We emphasize evaluation protocols that separate zero-shot transfer from in-language fine-tuning to avoid conflating adaptation with generalization. We further analyze scaling trends with respect to corpus size, language coverage, and tokenization granularity. Recent prompting-based alignment methods focus on steering model behavior without modifying parameters (Lester et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2023). Adapter-based transfer introduces lightweight modules to specialize shared backbones (Houlsby et al., 2019; Pfeiffer et al., 2020). Task-balanced sampling and vocabulary sharing have been examined for data efficiency in multilingual pretraining (Chi et al., 2022). We build on these observations by isolating the impact of language exposure during pretraining while holding architecture and optimization constant. Our design contrasts a family of models with identical capacity but varying partitions of seen and unseen languages. We measure transfer both to typologically related and distant languages to probe whether overlap in subword statistics mediates the effect. We also ablate data temperature, per-language mixing ratios, and domain diversity to assess sensitivity to distributional skew. For robustness, we report results across multiple random seeds and training lengths to mitigate instability in low-resource regimes. We complement accuracy with calibration and consistency metrics to capture overconfidence and representational drift. To interpret transfer pathways, we analyze layer-wise probing results and lexical alignment scores derived from bilingual dictionaries. We include controlled ablations on tokenizer vocab size and share rate to disentangle segmentation from representation learning. We additionally evaluate catastrophic forgetting by fine-tuning on a subset of high-resource languages and re-testing zero-shot performance. Finally, we discuss practical implications for multilingual deployment, including model selection under compute budgets and the trade-off between language coverage and per-language performance.

text: Recent advancements in language models, such as recurrent neural networks (Sundermeyer et al., 2012;Asri et al., 2016;Su et al., 2016;Zhao et al., 2019;Wang et al., 2020a;Zhang et al., 2020) and attention-based architectures (Vaswani et al., 2017;Liu et al., 2019;Devlin et al., 2018;Brown et al., 2020), have spurred increasing interest in such endto-end dialogue systems (Hosseini-Asl et al., 2020;Peng et al., 2020;Adiwardana et al., 2020). Modelbased approaches, in which a learned agent is substituted for a human, allow learning to be done entirely within simulation without human intervention (Li et al., 2016;He et al., 2018;Kang et al., 2019;Lewis et al., 2017;Liu et al., 2018). In contrast to these approaches, CALM augments the traditional language modeling objective with taskspecific rewards in order to finetune a model that is more aware of task goals, which significantly improves performance over a naïve language model without the need for simulating human responses in an interactive training loop. Jaques et al. (2019) recently proposed a model-free, offline approach to undirected dialogue, or dialogue without a specific task goal. Our method differs in that we aim to solve goal-oriented dialogue which allows us to optimize task-specific objectives, and that we take a model-based RL approach which enables us to leverage fine-tuned language models.

text:
We review prior work on visual semantic AI, on the geometry and semantic properties of representations formed by language models, and on semantic intrinsic evaluation tasks.
Foundational studies in visual semantic AI combine recognition, grounding, and reasoning, advancing image–text alignment and relational queries with contrastive pretraining and compositional decoders (Frome et al., 2013; Antol et al., 2015; Lu et al., 2019; Radford et al., 2021).
Recent analyses of learned semantics in LMs examine probing-based structure recovery from hidden states (Hewitt and Manning, 2019), disentanglement of syntax and semantics (Tenney et al., 2019), typological and factual attribution (Ravfogel et al., 2020; Petroni et al., 2019), and clustering properties under linear maps (Ethayarajh, 2019). Liu et al. (2019) survey task sensitivity to fine-tuning and contextualization depth across GLUE. Brown et al. (2020) show scaling laws enable in-context induction of latent features in few-shot prompts. Bommasani et al. (2021) catalog emergent behaviors in foundation models across modalities. Geva et al. (2021) identify neurons acting as key–value memories that correlate with lexical facts.
On the geometric side, theoretical and empirical work characterizes anisotropy, representational collapse, and linear separability, linking curvature of embedding manifolds to retrieval and calibration (Mikolov et al., 2013; Mu and Viswanath, 2018; Gao et al., 2019; Aghajanyan et al., 2020).
Intrinsic semantic evaluation spans similarity, relatedness, and compositional generalization, with benchmarks such as SimLex-999, STS, and SCAN illustrating distinct validity and reliability profiles (Hill et al., 2015; Cer et al., 2017; Lake and Baroni, 2018).

text: In this section, we will briefly introduce the background of adaptive kNN-MT (Zheng et al., 2021a). Adaptive kNN-MT is derived from kNN-MT (Khandelwal et al., 2021) by inserting a lightweight Meta-k Network that fuses kNN retrievals with various k to alleviate the possible noise induced by a single k. Recent work on retrieval-augmented translation has examined cache-based adaptation (Kuang et al., 2018), lexical-constrained decoding strategies (Post and Vilar, 2018), and memory-augmented transformers for domain shift (Bapna and Firat, 2019). Studies on bilingual lexicon induction report complementary benefits from nearest-neighbor search in embedding spaces (Artetxe et al., 2018; Lample et al., 2018). Investigations into example-based machine translation predate neural approaches and document phrase memory effects (Koehn and Senellart, 2010). Formally, it is formulated as two steps: target-side datastore creation and Metak Network predictions. Target-side Datastore Creation. The datastore constists of a set of key-value pairs. Given a bilingual sentence pair (s, t) in a corpus (S, T ), a pretrained general domain NMT model autoregressively extracts the context representation h i of the i-th target token conditioned on both source and target context (s, t <i ), denoted as

text: Unsupervised Revision-Based Methods such as Narayan and Gardent (2016) may apply a pipeline of edit operations in a fixed order. Kumar et al. (2020) then presented a general unsupervised revision-based approach by modelling text simplification as an unsupervised search problem. Prior controllable decoding methods for lexical simplification were explored by Zhang et al. (2022) with frequency-aware constraints. Xu et al. (2016) introduced evaluation metrics emphasizing simplicity and adequacy for system comparison. Pretrained masked LMs have also been applied to lexical substitution (Zhou et al., 2019). These strands have examined curriculum-based fine-tuning for readability (Laban et al., 2021). While GRS also relies on a revision-based framework and an unsupervised search strategy, we integrate a generative paraphrasing model into the framework to leverage the strengths of both text generation and text revision approaches.

text: The fact that few-shot learning is sensitive to many factors and thus is extremely unstable (Liu et al., 2021a;Lu et al., 2021;Zhang et al., 2020;Dodge et al., 2020) increases the difficulty of fewshot evaluation. Several works address evaluation protocols to mitigate the effects of instability: Gao et al. (2020) and Liu et al. (2021b) adopt a held-out set to select models. Perez et al. (2021) proposed K-fold cross-validation and minimum description length evaluation strategies. Bragg et al. (2021) andYe et al. (2021) proposed few-shot NLP benchmarks FLEX and Cross-Fit respectively. Our work differs from these works on few-shot evaluation in several aspects: (1) we propose three metrics to evaluate data split strategies; (2) while most prior work proposed evaluation protocols without justification, we conduct comprehensive experiments to support our key design choice; (3) we formulate a general evaluation framework; (4) our re-evaluation under the proposed framework leads to several key findings. Both FLEX and CrossFit study principles of designing tasks, datasets, and metrics, while we focus on addressing inherent problems of the evaluation procedure.

text: The idea of sequence modeling via segmentation has attracted widespread attention in several natural language processing tasks. In text segmentation task,  propose a probabilistic model for sequence modeling via their segmentation and a "Sleep-WAke Network"(SWAN) method. In machine translation,  propose a neural phrase-based machine translation system that models phrase structures in the target language using SWAN. In data-to-text generation, Wiseman et al. (2018) develop a neural template-like generation model with an HSMM decoder, which is learned tractably by backpropagating through a dynamic program; to tackle the problem of weak Markov assumption for the segment transition probability, Shen et al. (2020) propose to explicitly segment target text into fragments and align them with their data correspondences, and jointly learn the segmentation and correspondence via dynamic programming.

text: Toxicity detection systems (Schmidt and Wiegand, 2017;Pavlopoulos et al., 2017c;Zampieri et al., 2019) are typically trained on datasets annotated at the post level (a text is annotated as toxic or not) (Wulczyn et al., 2017;Borkan et al., 2019). Our work differs from general toxicity detection in that we detect toxic spans, instead of assigning toxicity labels to entire texts. Recent studies on interpretability emphasize instance-level explanations across vision and language (Sundararajan et al., 2017; Lundberg and Lee, 2017; Wiegreffe and Pinter, 2019; Jain and Wallace, 2019). Several works refine span-level supervision to reduce annotation costs and improve model faithfulness (Zaidan et al., 2007; DeYoung et al., 2020; Carton et al., 2020). Prompt-based labeling schemes have also been explored for sensitivity analysis and style transfer (Reif et al., 2019; Prabhumoye et al., 2018; Schick and Schütze, 2021). Multi-task frameworks jointly learn token salience and sequence classification (Hu et al., 2018; Narang et al., 2020). Datasets for offensive language identification cover multiple domains including social media and news comments (Founta et al., 2018; Davidson et al., 2017; Waseem and Hovy, 2016). Toxic spans detection can be seen as a case of attribution or rationale extraction (Li et al., 2016;Ribeiro et al., 2016), but specifically for toxic posts, a task that has never been the challenge to preserve anonymity. The full dataset and the code of this work will be released with a CC0 licence.

text: In this section, we summarize existing works on syntactically and lexically CPG. Syntactically CPG generates a paraphrase constrained by a prespecified sentence of a certain syntax structure namely exemplar. However, the exemplar is only available during inference, resulting in a key challenge: obtaining manual exemplars for existing paraphrasing training datasets is prohibitively expensive. To address this, some of the previous works construct syntactical features from target sentences during training, such as POS Tagging, Constituent Tree, mask template as illustrated in Table . For instance, SCPN (Iyyer et al., 2018) makes the first attempt to introduce Linearised Constituent Tree (LCT) of target sentence into paraphrasing, where LCT is predicted based on predefined parse templates. Similarly, GuiG (Li et al., 2020) proposes two models to expand a partial template LCT and generate paraphrasing, respectively. Different from using LCT, SGCP (Kumar et al., 2020) introduces a graph encoder to encode the Constituent Tree of exemplar as the condition. Besides, masked template replaces several words of the exemplar with a special token to form a template as the condition. For example, BCPG (Liu et al., 2020b) follows BERT (Devlin et al., 2019) to randomly mask exemplar words, ParafraGPT (Bui  2019) make the first attempt to integrate keywords with copy mechanism. Despite their progress, existing works only focus on a special condition under either lexically or syntactically CPG. In comparison, GCPG jointly includes lexically and syntactically CPG, flexibly combining conditions in a unified circumstance.

text: Lifelong Learning for PLMs. Lifelong learning aims at incrementally acquiring new knowledge, and in the meantime, mitigating the catastrophic forgetting issue. Numerous efforts have been spent towards this goal, including (1) memory-based methods (Rebuffi et al., 2017;Rolnick et al., 2019), which perform experience replay with authentic data (de Masson d'Autume et al., 2019), automatically generated data (Sun et al., 2020), or previously computed gradients (Lopez-Paz and Ranzato, 2017) conserved in the memory, (2) consolidationbased methods (Kirkpatrick et al., 2017;Aljundi et al., 2018), which introduce additional regularization terms to consolidate the model parameters that are important to previous tasks, and (3) dynamic architecture methods (Rusu et al., 2016;Yoon et al., 2018), which fix trained network architectures in old tasks and dynamically grow branches for new tasks. Lifelong learning is also a hot topic for PLMs. Some target at domain adaptation through continual pre-training (Gururangan et al., 2020), parameter-efficient adapters (He et al., 2021) and sparse expert models (Gururangan et al., 2021). Others focus on the incremental acquisition of factual knowledge that changes over time (Dhingra et al., 2021;Jang et al., 2021). Recent work also explores retrieval-augmented language modeling for continual adaptation (Lewis et al., 2020), curriculum scheduling for domain drift (Xie et al., 2021), and evaluation protocols for evolving knowledge graphs (Kassner et al., 2021). Lee et al. (2020) study prompt-based continual tuning for task streams. Sun et al. (2021) propose gradient orthogonalization for sequence of tasks. Kumar et al. (2022) benchmark adapter fusion for evolving domains. Nguyen et al. (2019) examine uncertainty estimation in lifelong classifiers. However, the existing works seldom consider our lifelong learning setting where streaming data from multiple sources is sequentially gathered. A concurrent work  conducts empirical studies on conventional continual learning algorithms for PLM adaptation. However, they do not focus on PLM's training efficiency, which is different from our setting. More detailed comparisons are left in appendix F.

text: Compositional Generalization in Semantic Parsing Compositional generalization is an essential problem in semantic parsing because formal languages are internally compositional. Generally, one way to improve compositional generalizability is to incorporate inductive biases directly to models through moduler models (Dong and Lapata, 2018), symbolic-neural machines , latent variables/intermediate representations (Zheng and Lapata, 2020;  learning (Lake, 2019) etc. Another way is to first do data augmentation and then train a model with augmented data (Andreas, 2019;Zhong et al., 2020;Akyürek et al., 2020). Pretrained models has also been shown useful for compositional semantic parsing (Oren et al., 2020;Furrer et al., 2020). Recent advances in compositional data augmentation emphasize controlled recombination of primitives (Keysers et al., 2020; Kim and Linzen, 2020). Lake and Baroni (2018) introduced SCAN to benchmark primitive and compositional splits. Oren et al. (2020) and Furrer et al. (2020) assessed pretrained encoders on compositional splits. Andreas (2019) explored recompositional data augmentation pipelines. Akyürek et al. (2020) proposed reversible transformations for schema-guided tasks. Zhong et al. (2020) studied generalization under structure-preserving perturbations of utterances. None of prior work used decomposition and prompt filling, or zero-shot models to improve compositional generalizability.

text: Prompt and Pivot task based learning has emerged as a candidate solution for data-scarcity problems (Le Scao and Rush, 2021;Min et al., 2021;Liu et al., 2021a). The use of discrete Schick and Schütze, 2021a,b,c) or continuous (Liu et al., 2021b) prompts allowed language models to perform significantly better on many text classification tasks. Closely related to our approach, several works make use of a highresource supervised task such as Question Answering or entailment as pivot tasks (Yin et al., 2019(Yin et al., , 2020Wang et al., 2021;Sainz and Rigau, 2021;McCann et al., 2018). In the case of entailment, Dagan et al. (2006) converted QA data to entailment manually and Demszky et al. (2018) did it automatically. Other semantic tasks such as Named Entity Recognition, Relation Extraction and Semantic Role Labelling have also been reformulated as entailment by automatically converting data into the entailment format (White et al., 2017;Poliak et al., 2018a;Levy et al., 2017;.

text: There have been a series of attempts to utilize phonetic representations of language to improve or extend automatic speech recognition (ASR) models. Some of these jointly model text and audio data using sequences of phonemes combined with sequences of text characters. Sundararaman et al. (2021), for example, uses a joint transformer architecture that encodes sequences of phonemes and sequences of text simultaneously. However, this joint model is utilized to learn representations that are more robust to transcription errors. The architecture still requires text inputs (from ASR transcriptions) and generates outputs in both text and phoneme representations. In contrast, our approach allows for text input, audio input, or text plus audio input to language models. Baevski et al. (2021) transforms unlabeled text (i.e., not aligned with corresponding audio files) into phonemes in a scheme to train speech recognition models without any labeled data. This scheme involves a generator model trained jointly with a discriminator model. The generator model converts audio, segmented into phonetic units into predicted phonemes, and the discriminator model attempts to discriminate between these predicted phonemes and the phonemes transliterated from unlabeled text. Although both text and audio are utilized in this work, they are not input to the same model and the primary output of the training scheme is a model that creates good phonetic speech representations from input audio. Outside of speech recognition focused work, Shen et al. (2020) (and other researchers cited therein) attempt to "fuse" audio and text at the word level for emotion recognition. They introduce another architecture that internally represents both audio and text. However, the so-called WISE framework relies on speech recognition to generate the text corresponding to audio frames in real-time. The current work explicitly avoids reliance on speech recognition. The 2021 Multimodal Sentiment Analysis (MuSe) challenge continues this vein of research integrating audio, video, text, and physiology data in an emotion recognition task (Stappen et al., 2021). Contributions to this challenge, such as Vlasenko et al. (2021), introduce a variety of ways to "fuse" audio and text inputs. However, these contributions are squarely focused on emotion/sentiment analysis and do not propose methods for flexible, phonetic language models. Lakhotia et al. ( 2021) introduced functionality for "textless" NLP. They explored possibility of creating a dialogue system from only audio inputs (i.e., without text). As part of this system, language models are directly trained on audio units without any text. This advances the state-of-the-art with regard to self-supervised speech methods, but it does not provide the flexibility in audio and/or text language modeling introduced here.

Although both text and audio are utilized in this work, they are not input to the same model and the primary output of the training scheme is a model that creates good phonetic speech representations from input audio

text: In the former group, FILM (Verga et al., 2021) extend BERT  with additional memory for storing facts. REALM (Guu et al., 2020) is pre-trained to perform reasoning over a large textual knowledge corpus on-the-fly during inference. In the latter group, ERNIE  trains a knowledge-enhanced model on a corpus combining of triples and their aligned sentence, with relation in triple or words in a sentence randomly masked. KnowBert  incorporate knowledge from Wikipedia and Word-Net into a BERT model through entity embeddings with knowledge-attention and re-contextualization mechanism. BERT-MK (He et al., 2020) is a BERTbased model that integrates graph contextual knowledge of a medical KG, which demonstrates the utility of graph-level knowledge. COMET (Bosselut et al., 2019) trains a commonsense-aware Transformer by learning to generate loosely structured commonsense descriptions in natural language given the structure knowledge. KELM (Agarwal et al., 2021) fine-tunes a T5 model to convert KGs to synthetic natural language sentences in order to augment existing pre-training corpora.

text: In terms of optimization, unlike the heuristicbased previous work that uses greedy (Jin et al., 2020) or genetic algorithms (Zang et al., 2020) to search for the optimal perturbations or other gradient-based methods (Wang et al., 2020) searching for perturbation on a tree-autoencoder with only syntactic constraints, we use an optimizationbased method to efficiently and effectively search for the optimal adversarial perturbation in the semantic preserving spaces to ensure the validity and naturalness of perturbed sentences. Zang et al. (2020) introduces a population-based search that balances semantic preservation and success rate. Alzantot et al. (2018) propose a genetic algorithm for crafting adversarial examples in sentence classification. Morris et al. (2020) survey text attack libraries and standardize evaluation protocols across tasks and datasets.

(2018) propose a genetic algorithm for crafting adversarial examples in sentence classification. Morris et al. (2020) survey text attack libraries and standardize evaluation protocols across tasks and datasets.

text: Few-shot methods are a recent development in English style transfer, with prior work using variational autoencoders (Xu et al., 2020), or prompting large pretrained language models at inference (Reif et al., 2021). Most related is the state-of-the-art TextSETTR model from Riley et al. (2021), who use a neural style encoder to map exemplar sentences to a vector used to guide generation. To train this encoder, they use the idea that adjacent sentences in a document have a similar style. Recently, the Universal Rewriter (Garcia et al., 2021) extended TextSETTR to 101 languages, developing a joint model for translation, few-shot style transfer and stylized translation. This model is the only prior few-shot system we found outside English, and our main baseline. We discuss its shortcomings in Section 3.1, and propose fixes in Section 4. Multilingual style transfer is mostly unexplored in prior work: a 35 paper survey by Briakou et al. (2021b) found only one work in Chinese, Russian, Latvian, Estonian, French. They further introduced XFORMAL, the first formality transfer evaluation dataset in French, Brazilian Portugese and Italian. 4 To the best of our knowledge, we are the first to study style transfer for the languages we consider. More related work from Hindi linguistics and on style transfer control is provided in Appendix B.

text: Evaluation protocols for style transfer vary widely across datasets and metrics, complicating comparisons. We adopt human judgments and automatic measures for style strength, adequacy, and fluency, along with calibration to reduce prompt sensitivity. We also report cross-lingual generalization by training on one language and testing on others using consistent tokenization. Kim et al. (2020) explored controllable paraphrasing with attribute tags. Zhao and Wang (2021) proposed a reinforcement learning framework to balance style strength and content preservation. Empirical findings on content faithfulness show trade-offs when increasing guidance signals (Li et al., 2018). Prior analyses of multilingual tokenization highlight the role of subword granularity in transfer (Kudugunta et al., 2019). Large-scale instruction tuning has been shown to improve cross-lingual generalization in generative tasks (Wang et al., 2023). Benchmarks for politeness and toxicity provide complementary signals for stylistic control (Danescu-Niculescu-Mizil et al., 2013; Sap et al., 2020). We release code, models, and a multilingual evaluation suite to facilitate future work.
