text: Automatic ICD coding is an important task in the medical NLP community. Earlier works use machine learning models to map clinical narratives to standardized diagnosis and procedure codes under multi-label settings. Prior studies emphasize hierarchical label dependencies in multi-level ICD taxonomies and leverage attention over clinical sections to improve code assignment accuracy (Mullenbach et al., 2018; Xu et al., 2019; Vu et al., 2020). Convolutional and recurrent encoders with per-code attention have been shown to capture local and sequential cues in discharge summaries (Baumel et al., 2018; Shi et al., 2017). Multi-label calibration techniques address prevalence skew across rare codes (Choi et al., 2016). Pre-training on large-scale clinical corpora such as MIMIC-III enhances domain adaptation for biomedical terminology (Johnson et al., 2016; Alsentzer et al., 2019). Beyond local encoders, long-document transformers and hierarchical pooling allow models to scale to full-length notes while preserving context. Recent transformer-based models fine-tune clinical variants of BERT or Longformer to handle long notes and sparse code distributions (Li et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2022). Weak supervision with heuristic labeling functions augments limited annotations in specialty domains (Ratner et al., 2017). External knowledge integration aligns mentions to UMLS concepts via dictionary matching and neural linking (Aronson and Lang, 2010; Tutubalina et al., 2020). Graph neural architectures propagate evidence across code hierarchies and note sections. In this work, we unify long-context modeling, label graph propagation, and ontology-aware normalization into a single training objective and demonstrate consistent gains on MIMIC-III and MIMIC-IV benchmarks.

text: Our approach, on the other hand, takes a fresh look at the issue and aims to define the underlying patterns of events for learning. Recent works on contextualized encoders have also reported gains for ED by leveraging pre-trained language models and multi-task signals (Devlin et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2019; Lin et al., 2020). Smith and Lee (2018) explored adversarial training for robust event triggers. Zhao et al. (2020) proposed curriculum learning to balance easy and hard event types. Xu and Chen (2021) examined domain adaptation from newswire to social media. Brown et al. (2020) presented large-scale pretraining for few-shot learning. Gao et al. (2021) investigated prompt-based tuning in classification settings. Liu et al. (2021) surveyed prompt design strategies. Wei et al. (2022) introduced instruction tuning for generalization. Mishra et al. (2022) compiled a collection of task instructions. Schick and Schütze (2021) studied pattern-exploiting training. Logan et al. (2021) analyzed template sensitivity in prompts.

text: We focus primarily on the task of syntactically controlled paraphrase generation, which has recently attracted increasing attention (Iyyer et al., 2018;Chen et al., 2019;Liu et al., 2020;Kumar et al., 2020;Li et al., 2020). According to the control element, previous works can be divided into two categories. Prior work includes parse-conditioned decoders that enforce constituency patterns (Iyyer et al., 2018), neural editors that rewrite to match POS tag sequences (Chen et al., 2019), and variational formulations that disentangle syntax and semantics (Liu et al., 2020). Kumar et al. (2020) introduce exemplar-based control with copy mechanisms to preserve key content. Li et al. (2020) explore discrete control codes to steer formality and style. The first strand of research uses sentential exemplar as syntactic control. We formalize exemplar control as conditioning on a parse-derived scaffold that specifies constituent order while allowing lexical freedom. Differing from previous approaches, we propose a novel tree-transformer to model parent-child and sibling relation for better capturing the tree structure of the parse tree. Our encoder represents nodes with type, span, and depth embeddings, while relation-aware attention composes hierarchical context. To learn the alignment relation between words and nodes, we also propose an attention regularization objective, which makes the decoder accurately select corresponding syntax nodes to guide the generation of words. The objective penalizes diffuse alignments by maximizing mass on gold node anchors extracted from word-to-node heuristics. Additionally, we propose a syntactic template retriever that can help to retrieve compatible syntactic structures for any input sentences. The retriever indexes a large bank of parsed sentences and returns templates that match coarse attributes such as clause counts and dependency heads. Retrieved templates are fused with the exemplar scaffold to reduce brittleness when the exemplar conflicts with input semantics. We evaluate on SCPN, ParaNMT, and PAWS, reporting automatic metrics and human judgments. Results show consistent gains in syntactic accuracy under matched and mismatched control settings. Ablations confirm that relation-aware attention and alignment regularization contribute complementary improvements.
span_text: Prior work includes parse-conditioned decoders that enforce constituency patterns (Iyyer et al., 2018), neural editors that rewrite to match POS tag sequences (Chen et al., 2019), and variational formulations that disentangle syntax and semantics (Liu et al., 2020). Kumar et al. (2020) introduce exemplar-based control with copy mechanisms to preserve key content. Li et al. (2020) explore discrete control codes to steer formality and style.

text: Recent work further examines the interplay between compression and invariance in supervised settings (Achille and Soatto, 2018), studies phase transitions in representation learning under noisy labels (Saxe et al., 2019), and explores causal mechanisms that shape feature selection in deep models (Schölkopf et al., 2021). Smith and Weston (2020) analyze domain shift in multilingual NER with cross-lingual projections. Zhao et al. (2021) introduce prototype-driven tagging for few-shot entity recognition, and Peters et al. (2018) propose deep contextualized embeddings for sequence labeling.

text: This paper discussed the arms-race between models and datasets. Previous works criticized one side of this arms race-the increasing size of pretrained models-due to ethical and environmental concerns (Schwartz et al., 2020;Bender et al., 2021), or questioning its ability to learn meaningful abstractions from raw text (Bender and Koller, 2020;. This work studies the second part of this arms race, regarding the efforts to mitigate spurious correlations through dataset balancing. The release of such datasets is often motivated by their potential to spur progress in modeling, and to help tease apart qualitative differences between models. Prior work on robustness has also examined calibration under distribution shift (Guo et al., 2017), domain generalization via invariant risk minimization (Arjovsky et al., 2020), and adversarial training for perturbation resilience (Madry et al., 2018). These studies report improvements on specific robustness metrics across vision and language tasks, often with trade-offs in accuracy and compute budget, and they raise questions about evaluation fidelity. Pretraining objectives that emphasize next-token prediction have likewise been scrutinized for their inductive biases (Kaplan et al., 2020; Hoffmann et al., 2022).  showed that this is not necessarily the case, by observing that the ranking of reading comprehension models on small and synthetic benchmarks is similar to that of the SQuAD dataset (Rajpurkar et al., 2016).

text: Low-resource event extraction. It has been a rising interest in event extraction under less data scenario. Recent efforts also explore data augmentation with paraphrasing or back-translation (Feng et al., 2021; Anaby-Tavor et al., 2020; Lee et al., 2021), knowledge injection via external ontologies (Huang et al., 2022; Li et al., 2022), and schema induction for unseen event types (Hsu et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2022). These approaches are evaluated on different benchmarks with heterogeneous setups and reporting metrics. Prior work on prompt-based extraction shows benefits from templated queries and constrained decoding (Li et al., 2021; Ebner et al., 2023; Ma et al., 2023). Text2Event (Lu et al., 2021), a sequence-to-structure generation paradigm, first presents events in a linearized format, and then trains a generative model to generate the linearized event sequence. Text2Event's unnatural output format hinders the model from fully leveraging pre-trained knowledge. Hence, their model falls short on the cases with only extremely low data being available (as shown in Section 3).

text: Traditional methods treat NER as a sequence labeling task, where each word or character in the sentence is assigned to a special label. As a representative, Huang et al. (2015) utilized the BiLSTM as an encoder to learn the contextual representation, and then exploited Conditional Random Field (CRF) as a decoder to label the tokens. The BiLSTM-CRF architecture achieved superior performance on various datasets, hence many following works (Lample et al., 2016;Ma and Hovy, 2016) adopt such architecture. More recently, strong pre-trained language models such as ELMo (Peters et al., 2018) and BERT (Devlin et al., 2019) introduce deep contextualization for token representations, and Dai et al. (2019) present Transformer-XL to model longer dependencies across segments. Clark et al. (2020) investigate self-attention interpretability with attention patterns, and Radford et al. (2019) scale unsupervised pretraining on web text. Liu et al. (2019) study multi-task training of transformers, and Lan et al. (2020) propose a lighter pretraining recipe. Recently, for Chinese NER, researchers proposed various lexicon-based models that incorporate the external lexicon information and obtained better results. Zhang and Yang (2018) investigated Lattice-LSTM for incorporating word lexicons into the character-based NER model. However, the lattice structure fails to compute in parallel. To address this problem, Gui et al. (2019) introduced a lexicon-based graph neural network that recasts Chinese NER as a node classification task. There are also several works that focus on incorporating all matched words from the lexicon into the character embeddings (Ma et al., 2020;Liu et al., 2021). Different from the aforementioned lexicon-based works that incorporate external resources, in this paper, we focus on exploring the internal regularity information of spans.

text: Active Learning. Active learning has been widely applied to various NLP tasks (Yuan et al., 2020;Zhao et al., 2020;Shelmanov et al., 2021;Karamcheti et al., 2021). So far, AL methods can be categorized into uncertainty-based methods (Gal et al., 2017;Margatina et al., 2021a,b), diversitybased methods (Ru et al., 2020;Sener and Savarese, 2018) and hybrid methods (Yuan et al., 2020;Ash et al., 2020;Kirsch et al., 2019). Ein-Dor et al. (2020) offer an empirical study of active learning with PLMs. In our study, we leverage the power of unlabeled instances via self-training to further promote the performance of AL. Semi-supervised Active Learning (SSAL). Gao et al. (2020); Song et al. (2019); Guo et al. (2021) design query strategies for specific semi-supervised methods, Tomanek and Hahn (2009); Rottmann et al. (2018); Siméoni et al. (2020) exploit the mostcertain samples from the unlabeled with pseudolabeling to augment the training set. So far, most of the SSAL approaches are designed for CV domain and it remains unknown how this paradigm performs with PLMs on NLP tasks. In contrast, we propose ACTUNE to effectively leverage unlabeled data during finetuing PLMs for NLP tasks. Self-training. Self-training first generates pseudo labels for high-confidence samples, then fits a new model on pseudo labeled data to improve the generalization ability (Rosenberg et al., 2005;Lee, 2013). However, it is known to be vulnerable to error propagation (Arazo et al., 2020;Rizve et al., 2021). To alleviate this, we adopt a simple momentumbased method to select high confidence samples, effectively reducing the pseudo labels noise for active learning. Note that although Mukherjee and Awadallah (2020); Rizve et al. ( 2021) also leverage uncertainty information for self-training, their focus is on developing better self-training methods, while we aim to jointly query high-uncertainty samples and generate pseudo-labels for low-uncertainty samples. The experiments in Sec. 3 show that with appropriate querying methods, ACTUNE can further improve the performance of self-training.

text: Interventions on neural networks were originally used as a structural analysis method aimed at illuminating neural representations and their role in network behavior Pryzant et al., 2021;Vig et al., 2020;Elazar et al., 2020;Giulianelli et al., 2020;Geiger et al., 2020Geiger et al., , 2021a. Geiger et al. (2021b) extend these methods to network optimization. We contribute to this existing research by adapting intervention-based optimization to the task of language model distillation. Sanh et al. (2019) and Jiao et al. (2019) emphasize compressing large transformers with task-aware objectives while Sun et al. (2019) investigates layer-wise mimicry in multilingual settings; Brown et al. (2020) introduce in-context learning with GPT-3 without distillation, and Raffel et al. (2020) unify transfer paradigms under sequence-to-sequence pretraining; Lewis et al. (2020) propose retrieval-augmented generation to ground outputs in external knowledge. Building on these insights, we hypothesize that aligning causal mediators across teacher and student can outperform purely loss-based distillation objectives. We formalize causal mediators as internal directions whose ablation modulates target behaviors while preserving unrelated functionality. To estimate such directions, we adopt interventional gradients that couple a do-operator with representation-level perturbations. Our training objective encourages the student to reproduce the teacher’s interventional response profile on shared probes. We evaluate on domain adaptation for question answering and sentiment classification, where structural alignment should mitigate spurious correlations. Experiments compare to probability matching, feature regression, and contrastive representation alignment baselines. Results show consistent gains in out-of-domain generalization while maintaining or improving in-domain accuracy. Ablations confirm that interventional probes targeted at hypothesized mediators yield the largest improvements. Finally, we discuss limitations regarding probe coverage and potential brittleness under distributional shift.

(2019) investigates layer-wise mimicry in multilingual settings; Brown et al. (2020) introduce in-context learning with GPT-3 without distillation, and Raffel et al. (2020) unify transfer paradigms under sequence-to-sequence pretraining; Lewis et al. (2020) propose retrieval-augmented generation to ground outputs in external knowledge.

text: Fact Aggregation The compact nature of the target text description results in aggregating multiple facts in a single sentence. Previous works (Wiseman et al., 2018;Shao et al., 2019;Shen et al., 2020;Xu et al., 2021) capture the segments which correspond to individual parts of the input as latent variables. Unlike these works, we adopt a simpler scenario using an already ordered sequence of facts, in which we selectively insert delimiters marking sentence boundaries. Recent studies on controllable text generation report that conditioning on structured signals can improve factual accuracy while maintaining fluency (Keskar et al., 2019; Dathathri et al., 2020; Krause et al., 2021). Prompt-based adaptations for structured data inputs have also been explored for data-to-text under constrained decoding regimes (Schick and Schütze, 2021; Li and Liang, 2021; Lester et al., 2021). Large-scale instruction tuning highlights the benefits of broad coverage in task formats and output styles (Wei et al., 2022; Ouyang et al., 2022). Paragraph Compression We introduce paragraph compression as a new task in our D2T generation pipeline. As the last step in the pipeline, it is closely related to linguistic realisation, howeversince we already work with natural language in this step-the focus of our task is on sentence fusion, rephrasing, and coreference resolution. Unlike text summarization or simplification Jiang et al., 2020), we aim to convey the complete semantics of the text without omitting any facts. In contrast to sentence fusion (Geva et al., 2019;Barzilay and McKeown, 2005) or sentence compression (Filippova and Altun, 2013), we operate in the context of multiple sentences in a paragraph. The task is the central focus of our WIKIFLUENT corpus ( §4), which we synthesize using a model for the reverse task, split-andrephrase, i.e. splitting a complex sentence into simpler ones while preserving semantics (Botha et al., 2018;.

text: Language-informed video understanding is a complex task that has been extensively addressed in the multimodal (natural language and computer vision) machine learning research, by proposing diverse tasks and benchmarks. Foundational datasets such as MSR-VTT (Xu et al., 2016), ActivityNet Captions (Krishna et al., 2017), and YouCook2 (Zhou et al., 2018) have catalyzed progress by standardizing evaluation for retrieval, captioning, and grounding. Early approaches leveraged recurrent encoders and attention to fuse frame sequences with sentence features for caption generation (Venugopalan et al., 2015; Yao et al., 2015). Transformer-based models subsequently improved long-range reasoning by modeling temporal structure and cross-modal alignment (Sun et al., 2019; Gabeur et al., 2020). Prior work on video-language grounding has explored temporal localization from captions (Hendricks et al., 2017), dense event captioning (Krishna et al., 2017), and instructional video understanding using weak supervision (Alayrac et al., 2016). Clip-level pretraining with contrastive objectives aligns video and text embeddings at scale (Radford et al., 2021; Jain et al., 2020). Procedural knowledge graphs for videos capture state transitions and object interactions (Chang et al., 2020). Recent large-scale pretraining frameworks fuse visual backbones with text encoders using masked modeling and contrastive losses to learn transferable representations (Lei et al., 2021; Li et al., 2021). These models benefit from curated subtitles or ASR transcripts, which provide weak supervision for event segmentation and narrative structure (Miech et al., 2019). Beyond alignment, methods for video question answering introduce compositional reasoning and multi-step attention to handle temporal dependencies (Jang et al., 2017; Lei et al., 2018). Fine-grained retrieval systems incorporate query-dependent temporal attention to match short snippets to natural language queries (Zheng et al., 2020). Knowledge-augmented models condition on external ontologies or commonsense graphs to resolve implicit references and missing entities in video narratives (Zellers et al., 2021). Cross-domain transfer remains challenging due to domain shift in motion statistics, narration style, and object distributions, motivating domain-adaptive pretraining and test-time adaptation (Alayrac et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2022). Recent work also studies open-vocabulary recognition by pairing region proposals with text embeddings to support zero-shot object and action retrieval (Gu et al., 2021). While progress is significant, most benchmarks emphasize short clips or curated domains, leaving long-form, multi-episode narratives underexplored. We address this gap by proposing a setting that couples hierarchical temporal abstraction with discourse-aware language modeling over entire videos. Our approach integrates structured prompts that bind narrative roles, entities, and temporal anchors to visual evidence extracted at multiple scales. We evaluate on both short- and long-form datasets, reporting gains in retrieval, temporal grounding, and question answering. Ablations isolate the contributions of hierarchical attention, discourse constraints, and weakly supervised temporal labeling within our framework.

Procedural knowledge graphs for videos capture state transitions and object interactions (Chang et al., 2020).

text: Determining which facts contradict claims in the existing article is a central topic of work on fact extraction and verification (Thorne et al., 2018). Recently, Schuster et al. (2021) introduced the VITAMIN-C dataset of factual revisions to Wikipedia articles and the task of factually consistent generation. This work differs from FRUIT in that it only focuses on sentences and does not require adding new facts or content selection. Prior work on controlled text editing encompasses lexical substitution, sentence fusion, and style transfer (Zhang et al., 2018; Nisioi et al., 2017; Rao and Tetreault, 2018). Lee et al. (2021) explored constrained decoding to maintain meaning while enforcing target attributes. Research on retrieval-augmented generation investigates how external documents improve grounding fidelity (Lewis et al., 2020; Guu et al., 2020). Multi-hop retrieval has been used to gather dispersed evidence across the web (Xiong et al., 2021). Human-in-the-loop editing pipelines study interactive revision tools that collect rationales and edits (Stiennon et al., 2020; Kreutzer et al., 2021). Methods for counterfactual data augmentation aim to stress test factuality and robustness (Kaushik et al., 2020). Entity-centric summarization models track mentions and coreference for improved salience (Liu and Lapata, 2019). Prompt-based control with pretrained language models conditions on structured control codes (Keskar et al., 2019).

text: The MFT is a well-established theory of moral values developed by social and cultural psychologists. It argues that human morality is composed of a finite set of innate moral foundations, similar to how the five taste receptors (for sweet, sour, salt, bitter, and umami) combine to yield the tastes we experience. The MFT includes five foundations, each composed of a vice-virtue duality, resulting in the 10 moral values shown in Table . Recent advances in prompt-based learning demonstrate that instruction-tuned language models can recover latent task structure from minimal supervision (Brown et al., 2020; Gao et al., 2021; Wei et al., 2022). Large-scale pretraining has also been linked to emergent abilities on reasoning and classification benchmarks (Kaplan et al., 2020; Srivastava et al., 2022). Several works examine domain adaptation techniques for imbalanced classification (Long et al., 2015; Buda et al., 2018). vided into seven datasets, each corresponding to a topic: All Lives Matter (ALM), Baltimore protests (BLT), Black Lives Matter (BLM), hate speech and offensive language (DAV) (Davidson et al., 2017), 2016 presidential election (ELE), MeToo movement (MT), and hurricane Sandy (SND). These datasets from complex and diverse socio-political issues allow us to evaluate the transferability by treating each dataset as belonging to a domain.

text: The tweets were annotated by multiple annotators with the MFT taxonomy. Hoover et al. (2020) provide additional details on the annotation process. They recognize that the vice and the virtue constituting one moral foundation are expressed differently in natural language. For example, an utterance describing a care concern (e.g., taking care of one's offspring) does not necessarily also contain harm expressions. For this reason, each tweet was annotated with all 10 individual moral values plus an additional nonmoral label, resulting in 11 possible labels per tweet. Due to the subjective nature of moral values, different annotators may label the same tweet differently. For this reason, Hoover et al. (2020) apply a majority vote to select the definitive label(s) of each tweet. Tweets with no majority label are labeled as nonmoral. Table  show three examples of annotated tweets. Table  shows the distribution of labels. Prior research on moral lexica constructs category-specific vocabularies to map text to moral dimensions (Haidt and Graham, 2007; Graham et al., 2009). Topic modeling approaches identify latent themes in social media debates (Blei et al., 2003; Roberts et al., 2014). Representation learning with contrastive objectives has been shown to improve label efficiency and robustness (Chen et al., 2020; Khosla et al., 2020). The MeanIR is a measure of imbalance in a dataset (Charte et al., 2015). MeanIR is the mean of IR l for each label l, where IR l is the ratio of the number of instances having the majority (i.e., nonmoral) label and the number of instances having label l. The degree of imbalance varies largely across datasets, which is realistic since different domains are likely to have different distributions of moral content.

text: In the news domain, Luo et al. (2021) automatically generate out-of-context images. Here, we curate social media posts that are topical and multimodal, and demonstrate an application to misinformation detection of human-generated fakes. There also exist general approaches for multimodal fact checking, e.g., Jaiswal et al. (2017) and Müller-Budack et al. (2020) that query an external knowledge base. Instead, we use a large pretrained model that does not require an external reference set. Prior work on social media misinformation spans diverse formulations: Castillo et al. (2011) model credibility signals in microblogs, Shu et al. (2017) survey content-, user-, and propagation-based features, and Zubiaga et al. (2018) analyze rumour resolution timelines across events. Vosoughi et al. (2018) report differential diffusion patterns between false and true news on Twitter that emphasize speed and breadth of spread. Multimedia datasets targeting cross-modal verification incorporate reverse image search and visual grounding (Boididou et al., 2016; Thorne et al., 2018). Ben-David et al. (2020) formalize covariate shift for domain adaptation while Zellers et al. (2019) introduce adversarially constructed news headlines.

text: Most later works follow the multi-instance multilabel learning framework (Surdeanu et al., 2012) in which there are multiple labels associated with a bag, and the model is trained with at-least-one assumption. Most neural models for the task encode each sentence separately, e.g., using Piecewise CNN (Zeng et al., 2015) To evaluate DS-RE, at test time, the model makes a prediction for an unseen bag. Unfortunately, most popular DS-RE dataset (NYT-10d) has a noisy test set, as it is automatically annotated (Riedel et al., 2010). Recently Gao et al. ( 2021) has released NYT-10m and Wiki-20m, which have manually annotated test sets. We use all three datasets in our work.

text: Multilingual DS-RE: A bilingual DS-RE model named MNRE (tested on English and Mandarin) introduced cross-lingual attention in languagespecific CNN encoders (Lin et al., 2017). Recently, Bhartiya et al. (2021) has released a dataset, DiS-ReX, for four languages -English, Spanish, German and French. We compare mPARE against the state of the art on DiS-ReX, which combines MNRE architecture with mBERT encoder. See Appendix E for details on all DS-RE models.

text:
In this section, we briefly introduce the three directions of translation evaluation.
We first cover reference-based evaluation, which assesses a system output against one or more human translations using surface- and meaning-oriented similarity measures.
Recent corpus-based metrics quantify adequacy with neural predictors (Zhang et al., 2020), reference-free quality estimation leverages multilingual encoders to model sentence-level uncertainty (Kepler et al., 2019), and human-in-the-loop protocols calibrate system ratings via expert calibration curves (Freitag et al., 2021). BLEU and METEOR remain strong baselines for reference-based evaluation (Papineni et al., 2002; Banerjee and Lavie, 2005). Direct Assessment provides crowd-sourced scalar judgments (Graham et al., 2013). BERTScore aligns contextual embeddings to approximate semantic similarity (Zhang et al., 2020). MQM emphasizes fine-grained error categories with inter-annotator reconciliation (Lommel et al., 2014).
We next describe reference-free quality estimation (QE), which predicts quality without access to references by modeling bilingual alignment signals, uncertainty, or calibration.
Finally, we address human evaluation frameworks that seek validity and reliability through task design, rater training, and error taxonomy alignment with downstream use cases.

BLEU and METEOR remain strong baselines for reference-based evaluation (Papineni et al., 2002; Banerjee and Lavie, 2005). Direct Assessment provides crowd-sourced scalar judgments (Graham et al., 2013). BERTScore aligns contextual embeddings to approximate semantic similarity (Zhang et al., 2020). MQM emphasizes fine-grained error categories with inter-annotator reconciliation (Lommel et al., 2014).

text: Compared to these methods, probing tasks (Conneau et al., 2018;Tenney et al., 2019) provide a more direct way to evaluate what a model can and cannot accomplish. While it is possible to probe embeddings or hidden representations directly (Tenney et al., 2019;Liu et al., 2019a), with the adoption of pre-trained language models has made it possible to evaluate such models by framing probing tasks close to the original model objective (Radford et al., 2019;Talmor et al., 2019;Ettinger, 2020;Goldberg, 2019). Clark et al. (2019) analyze attention patterns to explain token-to-token dependencies.

text: However, when a research area moves this quickly, it can be hard to keep up with many new models. Most of the existing research (Garí Soler and Apidianaki, 2020;Zagoury et al., 2021;Kassner et al., 2020) papers compare only one or two model families. Even some of the most recent works only probe BERT or very similar models (Zagoury et al., 2021;Liu et al., 2021). Only a small number of analysis papers have probed multiple (three or more) model families (Zhou et al., 2021;Ilharco et al., 2021). Radford et al. (2019) present a generative pre-training procedure for language understanding tasks that scales with data and compute.

text: Prior work also leverages external document grounding, persona consistency modeling, and retrieval-augmented mechanisms (Zhang et al., 2018; Dinan et al., 2019; Weston et al., 2018). Large-scale pretraining on open-domain corpora has been shown to improve fluency and contextual relevance (Radford et al., 2019; Lewis et al., 2020). Knowledge-grounded dialogue benchmarks report gains when models integrate entity-centric memory modules (Moon et al., 2019; Eric et al., 2017). Studies on evaluation highlight the need for both automatic and human metrics to capture diversity and appropriateness (See et al., 2019; Mehri and Eskénazi, 2020).

text: Document understanding is a critical and challenging task in information processing. There have been many related research works in this direction. Keyword detection (Liu et al., 2009;Tixier et al., 2016) & topic modeling (Blei et al., 2003) works aim is to describe the document by a few important words or topics for concise representation. The first step is to acquire a list of keyword candidates (e.g., n-grams or chunks) with heuristic methods (Hulth, 2003;Shang et al., 2018), then rank them in accordance with their importance to the document (Wu et al., 2005;Gollapalli and Caragea, 2014;Bougouin et al., 2013). Another task is compact and informative headline generation from a document (David and Zajic, 2003;Lopyrev, 2015). Text summarization is the process of generating natural language summaries from an input document retaining the most important information (Rush et al., 2015;See et al., 2017). Recently, an Outline Generation task was introduced by (Zhang et al., 2019) as a hierarchical structured prediction problem. Given a document, their aim is to first predict a sequence of section boundaries and then a sequence of section headings accordingly to come up with a Table of Contents for the same.

text: Further, it has been shown that question-answers play a critical role in scientific inquiry, informationseeking dialogue, and knowledge acquisition (Hintikka and Saarinen, 1979;Stede and Schlangen, 2004). In a dialogue system, question generation is used to obtain specific information from the user or make the conversation more pleasant (Shukla et al., 2019;Saeidi et al., 2018). Hence, we hypothesize that augmenting the default ToC with Questions that give a high level overview of the paragraphs can enhance the reading experience of the users. Question generation can also be seen as a summarization or seq2seq task. Various pre-trained language models like BART (Lewis et al., 2020), PEGASUS (Zhang et al., 2020), etc., have shown excellent results for these tasks. Researchers have worked on top of these language models & proposed various rewards for QGen to optimize these models. (Kumar et al., 2019) has employed BLEUbased rewards, (Zhang and Bansal, 2019) have used answerability rewards, whereas (Xie et al., 2020) has used a combination of fluency, relevance, and answerability rewards. Previous question generation literature has focused on generating questions based on an entity, phrase, or sentence. In this work, we explore longform question generation, i.e., question-based long text. We explore deep reinforcement learning techniques for the same. as they have shown competitive results in various natural language generation tasks such as summarization (Pasunuru and Bansal, 2018), style transfer (Liu et al., 2021;Goyal et al., 2021), question generation (Hosking and Riedel, 2019;Xie et al., 2020) etc. Motivated by this we use BART as our base model. As there is a lack of labeled question datasets in financial domain, to overcome the domain shift problem, we train the model with additional rewards in a reinforcement learning setup to make more suitable for a general domain.

text: Proposing a dependency parsing approach to the full task of SSA, Barnes et al. (2021) show that it leads to strong improvements over state-of-the-art baselines. Peng et al. (2021) propose a sparse fuzzy attention mechanism to deal with the sparseness of dependency arcs in the models from Barnes et al. (2021) and show further improvements. However, in order to apply the parsing algorithm of Dozat and Manning (2018), both of these approaches have to rely on a lossy conversion to bi-lexical dependencies with ad-hoc internal head choices for the nodes of the abstract sentiment graph. This lossy behaviour is caused by nested text spans in the sentiment graphs, as illustrated by Figure , which are ambiguous in their bi-lexical dependency encoding (see Section A in the Appendix). More generally, decoding structured graph information from text has sparked a lot of interest in recent years, especially for parsing meaning representation graphs (Oepen et al., 2020). There has been tremendous progress in developing complex transition-based and graph-based parsers (Hershcovich et al., 2017;McDonald and Pereira, 2006;Dozat and Manning, 2018). Recent work on AMR-to-text generation highlights complementary challenges in aligning graph structure with surface realization (Ribeiro et al., 2021). Several studies investigate joint learning for span-based semantic roles and coreference (He et al., 2018; Lee et al., 2017). Neural approaches to discourse parsing emphasize hierarchical constraints and latent tree induction (Kobayashi et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2019). Studies on cross-sentence relation extraction and event linking report gains from global inference (Jain et al., 2020; Wadden et al., 2019). Multi-task learning with token-level supervision has been shown to be effective in biomedical IE (Beltagy et al., 2019). In this paper, we adopt PERIN (Samuel and Straka, 2020), a state-of-theart graph-based parser capable of modeling a superset of graph features needed for our task. We focus on sentiment structure analysis (SSA) as a graph decoding problem that requires nested span handling and explicit target-opinion linking. Our contributions include a direct graph decoding formulation that avoids lossy bi-lexical conversion and a training objective aligned with labeled subgraph constraints. We compare against dependency-based baselines that apply head-attachment heuristics and show consistent gains on standard SSA benchmarks. We further ablate span boundary modeling, arc factorization, and label set granularity to quantify the impact of nested structures. To test robustness, we evaluate under distribution shift induced by domain and sentence length, reporting calibration and error profiles. Our analysis reveals that nested span ambiguity primarily harms opinion-holder detection when converted to bi-lexical form. We release code, data preprocessing scripts, and trained models to facilitate reproducibility. Finally, we discuss implications for downstream tasks that consume SSA graphs, including explainable sentiment summarization and stance detection. We believe that graph-native parsers provide a principled alternative to conversion-heavy pipelines in sentiment analysis. Looking ahead, we outline extensions to multilingual settings and low-resource domains where span nesting patterns vary substantially across languages.

text:
We now introduce the task of entity set expansion (ESE), existing paradigms and evaluation methods.
ESE aims to infer a ranked list of entities that belong to the same semantic category as a small set of seed exemplars.
The problem is widely used in taxonomy induction, query understanding, and knowledge base completion where coverage and precision must be balanced.
Models typically exploit distributional similarity, pattern-based evidence, and graph connectivity to identify category-consistent candidates from large text corpora.
Prior work spans diverse formulations of ESE: SEISA mines coordination patterns from web lists (Liu et al., 2010); Espresso uses pattern-bootstrapping from seed contexts (Pantel and Pennacchiotti, 2006); SetExpan integrates submodular objective functions with reliability signals (Rong et al., 2016). Neural methods include distributional expansion over contextualized embeddings (Shen et al., 2020). Graph-based approaches propagate label signals over entity co-occurrence graphs (Mamou et al., 2018). Retrieval-oriented systems treat ESE as iterative query expansion (Carpineto and Romano, 2012).
Despite methodological variety, most pipelines share three stages: seed encoding, candidate generation, and scoring with calibration to avoid semantic drift.
Seed encoding may average contextualized token embeddings or construct prototype vectors using attention over seed mentions in corpus contexts.
Candidate generation aggregates signals from mention-level matches, list extractions, and knowledge base neighbors to ensure recall without overwhelming the ranker.
Scoring functions combine local compatibility with global consistency, often via learning-to-rank objectives that incorporate list co-membership and mutual exclusivity cues.
Recent advances investigate semi-supervised training with contrastive losses that pull positives toward seed prototypes while pushing near-miss negatives.
In multilingual settings, shared subword spaces and cross-lingual entity links allow zero-shot ESE by transferring seed semantics across languages.
Evaluation commonly reports AUC, MAP, and Precision@K, along with calibration metrics to assess early precision where user interaction is highest.
Benchmarks include entity typing subsets from Wikipedia, curated business taxonomies, and web list corpora with noisy mentions requiring normalization.
Ablation studies typically separate the contributions of context modeling, list extraction reliability, and graph propagation to diagnose drift sources.
We focus on robust early-precision expansion under limited seeds, providing a principled calibration strategy and reproducible evaluation protocols.
Our approach also emphasizes transparency by releasing seed sets, candidate pools, and ranking scripts to facilitate fair comparison and downstream reuse.

Neural methods include distributional expansion over contextualized embeddings (Shen et al., 2020). Graph-based approaches propagate label signals over entity co-occurrence graphs (Mamou et al., 2018). Retrieval-oriented systems treat ESE as iterative query expansion (Carpineto and Romano, 2012).

text: The works that are most similar to ours strive to gain diversity using controlled-paraphrase generation, by exposing control mechanisms that are manipulated to produce either lexically (Zeng et al., 2019;Thompson and Post, 2020) or syntactically Goyal and Durrett, 2020) diverse paraphrases. One approach is to use an exemplar sentence for guiding the syntax of the generated paraphrase Bao et al., 2019). An alternative is to directly employ constituency tree as the syntax guidance (Iyyer et al., 2018;Li and Choi, 2020). Goyal and Durrett (2020) promote syntactic diversity by conditioning over possible syntactic rearrangements of the input. Zeng et al. (2019) use keywords as lexical guidance for the generation process. Here we introduce a simple model for jointly controlling the lexical, syntactic and semantic aspects of the generated paraphrases.

text: Various benchmarks were proposed to measure models' temporal reasoning abilities. The bAbI suite contains a task that requires reasoning about the order of time expressions (Weston et al., 2015). MC-TACO is a reading comprehension task pertaining to ordering, duration, stationarity, frequency, and typical times of events (Zhou et al., 2019). TI-MEDIAL (Qin et al., 2021) is a dialogue QA task focusing on temporal commonsense. Zhou et al. (2021) and Thukral et al. (2021) both cast the temporal ordering task as an NLI task. In another line of work, tracking state changes in procedural text is also related to temporal ordering (Dalvi et al., 2018;Zhang et al., 2020). Despite the success of pre-trained LMs on language understanding tasks, their performance on these benchmarks is limited, maybe due to the fact that many temporal relations are not explicitly stated in text (Davis and Marcus, 2015). A promising direction is to train LMs explicitly on temporal knowledge (Zhou et al., 2020).

TI-MEDIAL (Qin et al., 2021) is a dialogue QA task focusing on temporal commonsense. Zhou et al. (2021) and Thukral et al. (2021) both cast the temporal ordering task as an NLI task. In another line of work, tracking state changes in procedural text is also related to temporal ordering (Dalvi et al., 2018;Zhang et al., 2020).

text: Cultural Commonsense. There is little focus on cultural differences in NLP in general (Hovy and Yang, 2021) and in research about commonsense reasoning in particular. Recently, Acharya et al. (2021) made a first step in addressing this gap. They surveyed crowdsourcing workers in the US and India regarding rituals that are commonly found across cultures such as birth, marriage, and funerals. In particular, they asked questions pertaining to temporal aspects such as typical time and duration of each event. The paper mentions anecdotal differences such that a wedding lasts a few hours in the US but a few days in India. Although there is no direct mapping between culture and language, one can often teach about the other. For example, in ConceptNet, a multilingual commonsense knowledge base, the English entry for breakfast specifies pancakes as breakfast food, while the Chinese entry mentions noodles (Speer et al., 2017).

text: QG has progressed rapidly due to new datasets and model improvements. Many different QG models have been proposed, starting from simple vanilla Sequence to Sequence Neural Networks models (seq2seq) (Yuan et al., 2017) to more recent transformer-based models (Dong et al., 2019; Chan and Fan, 2019; Varanasi et al., 2020; Narayan et al., 2020; Bao et al., 2020). Foundational encoder-decoder refinements include copy and coverage mechanisms to reduce omissions and repetitions in generated questions (See et al., 2017; Song et al., 2018). Recent studies have examined controllable generation for difficulty and answer style, and they emphasize latent variable conditioning (Zhou et al., 2017; Krishna and Iyyer, 2019). Large-scale pretraining on QA-style corpora has been leveraged to improve QG fluency and factuality (Lewis et al., 2020; Raffel et al., 2020). Multi-task objectives combining answer extraction and question decoding have been explored to align attention with evidence spans (Sachan and Xing, 2018; Dong et al., 2019). Curriculum learning schedules were found to stabilize training on heterogeneous sources (Kumar et al., 2010). Some QG systems use manual linguistic features in their models (Harrison and Walker, 2018; Khullar et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2019a; Dhole and Manning, 2020), some consider how to select question-worthy content (Li et al., 2019; Scialom et al., 2019), and some systems explicitly model question types (Duan et al., 2017; Sun et al., 2018; Kang et al., 2019). The last group focused only on generating questions that start with specific interrogative words such as what and how.

text: QG has been used to solve many real-life problems. For example, QG in conversational dialogue aims to support proactive assistants by generating follow-up questions grounded in dialogue state and retrieved evidence (Gu et al., 2021; Shen et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2021b). Visually grounded QG explores alignment between regions and textual rationales to elicit informative queries about images (Mostafazadeh et al., 2016; Shin et al., 2018; Shukla et al., 2019). In specialized domains, QG has been applied to generate deep questions for mathematics, curiosity-driven tutoring, clinical documentation, and high-stakes examinations, often requiring domain constraints and answer calibration (Liyanage and Ranathunga, 2019; Yue et al., 2020; Jia et al., 2021). Data augmentation using back-translation and paraphrase mining has been reported to mitigate domain shift (Sennrich et al., 2016; Wieting and Gimpel, 2018). Span corruption strategies were adopted to increase robustness to noisy contexts (Guu et al., 2020). Automatic evaluation correlates imperfectly with human judgments in QG, with BLEU and ROUGE missing answerability and specificity (Callison-Burch et al., 2006; Fabbri et al., 2021). Crowd-sourced annotations introduce variability in what constitutes a good question (Nakov et al., 2018). Looking ahead, our work situates these trends by unifying content selection, type control, and multi-domain adaptation under a single training framework.

text: Late-interaction models. The idea of running several transformer layers for the document and the query independently, and then combining them in the last transformer layers, was developed concurrently by multiple teams: PreTTR (MacAvaney et al., 2020), EARL (Gao et al., 2020a), DC-BERT (Nie et al., 2020), DiPair , and the Deformer (Cao et al., 2020). These works show that only a few layers where the query and document interact are sufficient to achieve results close to the performance of a full BERT ranker at a fraction of the runtime cost. For each document, the contextual token vectors are stored in a cache and retrieved during the document ranking phase. This impacts both storage cost as well as latency cost of fetching these vectors during the ranking phase. MORES (Gao et al., 2020b), extends lateinteraction models, where in the last interaction layers only the query attends to the document (and not vice-versa). As document are typically much longer, this results in additional performance improvements with similar storage requirements. Col-BERT (Khattab and Zaharia, 2020) is another variant that runs all transformer layers independently for the query and the document, and the interaction between the final vectors is done through a sumof-max operator. A similar work, the Transformer-Kernel (TK) (Hofstätter et al., 2020b), has an interaction block based on a low-complexity kernel operation. Both ColBERT and TK result in models with lower runtime latency at the expense of a drop in ranking quality. However, the storage requirements for both approaches are still significant. Some of the works above acknowledge the issue of storing the precomputed document representations and proposed partial solutions. In Col-BERT (Khattab and Zaharia, 2020), the authors proposed to reduce the dimension of the final token embedding using a linear layer. However, even moderate compression ratios caused a large drop in ranking quality. In the PreTTR model (MacAvaney et al., 2020), it was proposed to address the storage cost by using a standard auto-encoder architecture and the float16 format instead of float32. Again, the ranking quality drops even with moderate compression ratios (they measured up to 12x).

text: Compressed embeddings. Our work reduces storage requirements by reducing the number of bits per floating-point value. Quantization gained attention and success in reducing the size of neural network parameters (Gupta et al., 2015;Essam et al., 2017;Wang et al., 2018;Wu et al., 2018) and distributed learning communication costs (Suresh et al., 2017;Alistarh et al., 2017;Konečnỳ and Richtárik, 2018;Vargaftik et al., 2021b). Specifically, compressing word embeddings has been studied as an independent goal. May et al. (2019) studied the effect of quantized word embeddings on downstream applications and proposed a metric for quantifying this effect with simple linear models that operate on the word embeddings directly. As our work is concerned with compressing contextual embeddings, these methods do not apply since the set of possible embeddings values is not bounded by the vocabulary size. Nevertheless, as in (May et al., 2019), we also observe that simple quantization schemes are quite effective. Our work uses recent advances in this area to further reduce storage requirements for document representation, which, to the best of our knowledge, were not previously attempted in this context.

text: Our work depends heavily on recent advances in efficient Transformers (Tay et al., 2020) that process long sequences (Rae et al., 2020;Beltagy et al., 2020;Zaheer et al., 2020;Roy et al., 2021). Sparse attention , relative position encoding (Shaw et al., 2018;Raffel et al., 2020;Guo et al., 2021) and other tricks (Dai et al., 2019;Weston et al., 2015;Shen et al., 2020;Stock et al., 2021;Katharopoulos et al., 2020) are commonly adopted by efficient Transformers. Existing long-sequence downstream tasks include long-form QA (Fan et al., 2019;Pang et al., 2021), document-level summarization (Kryściński et al., 2021;Huang et al., 2021), and machine translation (Liu and Zhang, 2020). More recently, Shaham et al. (2022) introduce a new benchmark covering multiple domains and tasks, while Tay et al. (2021) propose multimodal long sequence tasks. We focus on scaling training stability for context lengths beyond 64k tokens by combining attention sparsification with memory-efficient optimizers. A line of work examines blockwise locality patterns and sliding-window kernels that trade quadratic cost for linear or subquadratic behavior (Child et al., 2019;Beltagy et al., 2020;Dao et al., 2022). Our approach builds on these insights by adopting chunked attention with reversible layers to control activation memory (Gomez et al., 2017;Kitaev et al., 2020). We additionally incorporate rotary position embeddings to maintain extrapolative behavior at large contexts (Su et al., 2021;Press et al., 2021). To better support discourse-level reasoning, we integrate hierarchical pooling over segments that feed global tokens (Zhang et al., 2022;Dehghani et al., 2018). Training long-context models demands robust optimization schedules and gradient scaling techniques that avoid divergence under long unrolls (Shazeer and Stern, 2018;Goyal et al., 2017). We further use curriculum context expansion, gradually increasing window sizes while monitoring perplexity plateau thresholds. Our evaluation spans synthetic copy-recall tasks and realistic domains such as legal and scientific corpora with document-level objectives. Recent studies on retrieval-augmented generation investigate how external memory can complement long-context encoders by decoupling retrieval and reasoning, with REALM (Guu et al., 2020) learning latent document indices, DPR (Karpukhin et al., 2020) optimizing bi-encoders with in-batch negatives, and RAG (Lewis et al., 2020) composing retrieval with sequence-to-sequence models; pretraining approaches such as LongT5 (Guo et al., 2022) and LED (Beltagy et al., 2020) scale context windows via efficient attention, while hierarchical modeling explores segment-level aggregation for global coherence (Zhang et al., 2022), and curriculum strategies shape token-to-document objectives for stability (Xiong et al., 2022), prompting methods adapt instruction-tuned models to sparse evidence settings (Wei et al., 2022;Ouyang et al., 2022), task-specific architectures for code understanding extend window sizes using block-sparse patterns (Dao et al., 2022), and speech-language models tie temporal subsampling to attention spans in cross-modal pretraining (Baevski et al., 2020). Prior work on long-range dependency benchmarks emphasizes retrieval-based probes that isolate memory capacity from reasoning accuracy (Hupkes et al., 2020;Tay et al., 2021). We adopt these probes to analyze failure modes under varying sparsity patterns and optimizer states. In addition, we compare sliding-window attention against kernelized approximations such as Performer and Nyströmformer (Choromanski et al., 2021;Xiong et al., 2021). For document summarization, long-range encoders benefit from global tokens and learned segment indices to capture topic shifts (Huang et al., 2021;Zaheer et al., 2020). In machine translation, constrained decoding with extended context improves pronoun resolution and lexical consistency across paragraphs (Voita et al., 2019;Liu and Zhang, 2020). We also examine pretraining objectives that mix span corruption with sentence reordering to encourage cross-sentence coherence (Raffel et al., 2020;Wang et al., 2022). Our contributions include a unified training recipe that scales to 128k tokens on commodity hardware with reproducible settings. We release code and checkpoints to facilitate future research on efficient long-context training and evaluation.

text: Language model prompting. Providing prompts or task descriptions play an vital role in improving pre-trained language models in many tasks Radford et al., 2021;Schick and Schütze, 2020a,b;Brown et al., 2020). Among them, GPT models (Radford et al., 2019;Brown et al., 2020) achieved great success in prompting or task demonstrations in NLP tasks. In light of this direction, prompt-based approaches improve small pre-trained models in few-shot text classification tasks Schick and Schütze, 2020a,b). CLIP (Radford et al., 2021) also explores prompt templates for image classification which affect zero-shot performance. We follow these core ideas so we aim to improve zero-shot and few-shot performance using prompts in visionlanguage tasks. Recent work on instruction-tuned language models emphasizes the importance of structured prompts for cross-task generalization (Sanh et al., 2022; Wei et al., 2022). T0-style mixtures aggregate heterogeneous supervision across datasets to improve transfer, and chain-of-thought prompting yields gains on multi-step reasoning benchmarks. Multimodal instruction datasets have also been curated with synthetic captions aligned to dialogue turns. Concurrently, retrieval-augmented generation integrates external corpora at inference to ground answers in evidence (Lewis et al., 2020). We pretrain FEWVLM with masked language modeling (MaskedLM) and prefix language modeling (Pre-fixLM).

text: Sequence Ordering. Story sequencing test is a popular way of examining children's abilities on sequential reasoning which is shown evident for procedural understanding (Tomkins, 1952;Baron-Cohen et al., 1986;Loucks et al., 2017). In NLP, existing works attempt the sequencing task as sorting a series of unordered sentences Cui et al., 2018;Logeswaran et al., 2018;Oh et al., 2019;Lee et al., 2020;Calizzano et al., 2021) from paper abstracts or short paragraphs. While certain prior work also attempts to extend it to incorporate multimodality , the dataset used, Visual StoryTelling , features album images that were not intended to be procedural nor supply unstated details to complement the texts. In computer vision, existing work leverages shuffle frame prediction for learning video representations (Lee et al., 2017;Xu et al., 2019;Wang et al., 2020;Li et al., 2020) as well as cycle consistency constraints for learning temporal dynamics (Epstein et al., 2021). (Zellers et al., 2021) also features a pairwise relative frame reordering objective to learn temporal common sense from scripted videos, however, as their downstream   We again only list the categories with total instance count >10.

text: We focus on (large-scale) multi-label document classification and study a fundamental component of the learning process leading to performance disparities across labels, i.e., the underlying optimization algorithm used for training. We consider group-robust optimization algorithms initially proposed to mitigate group disparities given specific protected attributes (e.g., gender, race), but re-frame these algorithms to optimize for good performance across labels rather than across groups. Recent works also investigate temporal robustness in pretrained representations (Gururangan et al., 2020; Lazaridou et al., 2021; Zhao et al., 2022). Studies on adaptive vocabularies for streaming text propose dynamic token inventories to accommodate lexical innovation (Bostrom and Durrett, 2020). Large-scale continual learning benchmarks evaluate forgetting across sequential corpora (Delange et al., 2021). Methods for change-point detection in time-stamped corpora range from Bayesian online changepoint models to embedding drift metrics (Adams and MacKay, 2007; Rosin et al., 2022). Analyses of temporally localized events consider shifts in topical salience and sentiment in newswire (Lazer et al., 2018). We operationalize this perspective by defining a per-label objective that equalizes effective sample sizes across time. Our approach integrates per-epoch reweighting with worst-group risk minimization where groups are labels. Concretely, we modify distributionally robust optimization to construct label-indexed uncertainty sets. This yields a training criterion that regularizes toward uniform label-wise margins. We further adapt calibration-aware weighting to stabilize gradients for minority labels. The training pipeline is compatible with transformer encoders and sparse output layers. We evaluate on temporally partitioned multi-label legal and biomedical corpora. Our metrics include macro-F1, label-wise AUC, and time-sliced excess risk. We assess robustness to synthetic drift by permuting label prevalences across epochs. We report ablations isolating the effects of reweighting, uncertainty set radius, and optimizer choice. We additionally analyze fairness spillovers from label-wise balancing to protected groups. Our results indicate significant improvements for rare and emerging labels under drift. The proposed method maintains competitive overall accuracy while reducing volatility. We release code and data splits to facilitate reproducibility. We also discuss limitations related to label taxonomy shifts that our method does not address. Future work will incorporate hierarchical constraints and semi-supervised updates. We provide guidelines for deployment in dynamic domains such as compliance monitoring. Finally, we summarize the contribution as a simple optimization layer for drift-resilient multi-label learning. 20k, 1975-2002), development (8.5k, 2002-2008), test (8.5k, 2008-2018)

text: OTD in Text Classification Early approaches to OTD relied primarily upon dictionaries like hatebase 3 to lookup offensive words and phrases. The creation of OTD datasets enabled the development of ML-based approaches utilizing simple features, such as bag-of-word representations. With the advent of social media platforms, many resources have been developed for identifying toxic comments in web text (Waseem and Hovy, 2016), including a number of deep learning-based methods (Pitsilis et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2018b; Casula et al., 2020; Yasaswini et al., 2021; Djandji et al., 2020). Notably, all of these methods can be described as building a contextual representation of a sentence and making a classification based on this representation. Recent neural architectures for offensive and toxic discourse (OTD) detection include hierarchical attention networks that capture context across comment threads (Pitsilis et al., 2018), convolutional-recurrent hybrids optimized for noisy user-generated text (Zhang et al., 2018b), and transformer-based encoders fine-tuned on multi-platform corpora (Casula et al., 2020; Djandji et al., 2020; Yasaswini et al., 2021). Several corpora emphasize multilingual toxicity with annotation schemes tailored to implicit abuse (Waseem and Hovy, 2016), and dataset curation practices vary in annotator expertise and label granularity. Pre-trained language models have been adapted through task-specific prompts and lightweight adapters to improve few-shot generalization. Evaluation protocols report macro-F1 and AUROC on long-tail identity terms. Data augmentation strategies range from paraphrasing to counterfactual rewrites. Label smoothing and focal losses mitigate class imbalance. Despite these advances, models remain sensitive to distribution shift across platforms and time, which limits deployment in dynamic moderation settings. Studies on robustness highlight that spurious lexical cues and community-specific slang can drive predictions, motivating approaches that integrate context beyond single comments. Recent work leverages user- or thread-level signals to capture pragmatic intent without overfitting to surface forms. Complementary research explores detoxification and style transfer to reduce harmful content while preserving semantics, enabling semi-automated moderation workflows. We build on these insights by focusing on cross-domain generalization with constrained supervision. Specifically, we examine representation learning strategies that decouple toxicity from topical content. Our approach integrates semantically controlled perturbations and domain-adaptive regularization to improve calibration and fairness. We provide a comprehensive evaluation across multiple public benchmarks and a newly curated temporal shift set. The findings indicate consistent gains in out-of-domain F1 and reduced false positives on reclaimed slurs. Finally, we release code and resources to encourage reproducible research and facilitate deployment in real-world moderation pipelines.

text: Fair machine learning The literature on inducing approximately fair models from biased data is rapidly growing. See Mehrabi et al. (2021) for a recent survey. We rely on this literature in how we define fairness, and for the algorithms that we compare in our experiments below. As already discussed, we adopt a capability-centered approach to fairness and define fairness in terms of performance parity (Hashimoto et al., 2018) or equal risk (Donini et al., 2018). The fairness-promoting learning algorithms we evaluate are discussed in detail in §4. Some of these -Group Distributionally Robust Optimization (Sagawa et al., 2020) and Invariant Risk Minimization (Arjovsky et al., 2020) -have previously been evaluated for fairness in the context of hate speech (Koh et al., 2021). has a limited history. In a classic study, Angwin et al. (2016) analyzed the performance of the Correctional Offender Management Profiling for Alternative Sanctions (COMPAS) system, which was used for parole risk assessment (recidivism prediction) in the US. The system relied on 137 features from questionnaires and criminal records. Angwin et al. (2016) found that blacks were almost twice as likely as whites to be mislabeled as high risk (of re-offending), revealing a severe racial bias in the system. The system was later compared to crowdworkers in Dressel and Farid (2018). We extend this line of inquiry by focusing on group-level robustness criteria that align with distributional shifts across demographics in modern web-scale datasets. Recent work has highlighted tensions between individual calibration and group parity, suggesting task-dependent trade-offs that must be made explicit (Kleinberg et al., 2017; Chouldechova, 2017). In applied settings, practitioners often rely on post-processing to meet legal thresholds, yet post-processing can undermine predictive consistency across subpopulations (Hardt et al., 2016). Our study complements these findings by comparing in-processing methods that alter the learning objective to prioritize worst-group performance. We specifically evaluate how the choice of fairness definition affects model selection under covariate shift and label imbalance. To ensure reproducibility, we report all hyperparameters and data preprocessing steps, and we release code to facilitate benchmarking on common fairness datasets. While our primary interest is classification, the framework naturally extends to ranking and survival analysis with minimal modifications.

(2016) analyzed the performance of the Correctional Offender Management Profiling for Alternative Sanctions (COMPAS) system, which was used for parole risk assessment (recidivism prediction) in the US.

text: A series of work (SentenceBERT (Reimers and Gurevych, 2019), BERT-flow (Li et al., 2020), Sim-CSE (Gao et al., 2021)) explored intermediate training to improve STS tasks. All of them applied pre-fine tuning on NLI datasets. More recently, EFL (Wang et al., 2021) proposed a task transformation paradigm, improving single sentence tasks with less labels using rich sentence-pair datasets. STILTs (Phang et al., 2018) reported substantial gains from intermediate NLI training on multiple GLUE tasks. In-context learning with large language models can also reframe classification as instruction following (Brown et al., 2020). Calibration strategies adjust confidence estimates without modifying task heads (Guo et al., 2017). Data augmentation via back-translation expands paraphrase diversity (Edunov et al., 2018). Contrastive pre-training with hard negatives increases embedding uniformity (Khosla et al., 2020). Multi-task encoders share cross-task structure for regularization (Liu et al., 2019). Pre-training on question–answer pairs enriches relational semantics (Kwiatkowski et al., 2019). Sentence-level distillation compresses teacher representations into smaller student models (Sanh et al., 2019).

text: Prior studies (Young et al., 2018;Zhou et al., 2018a; have demonstrated the feasibility of including commonsense knowledge into the dialogue systems. Further, in ConKADI (Wu et al., 2020a), felicitous facts highly relevant to the context were selected and effectively integrated in the generated response by means of fusion mechanisms. Recently, co-reference resolution has been utilized for obtaining coref-informed pre-trained models (Ye et al., 2020), task-oriented dialogue generation (Jun Quan and Hu, 2019), and dialogue understanding . Further, (Huang et al., 2021) demonstrated the improvement upon explicitly incorporating co-reference information to enhance the attention mechanism for the reading comprehension task.

text: While other foci of formal linguistics have attracted the attention of NLP researchers-including coreference (Ogrodniczuk et al., 2019(Ogrodniczuk et al., , 2020, nega-  tion (Hossain et al., 2020;Hartmann et al., 2021), and consistency (Li et al., 2019;Ribeiro et al., 2019;Asai and Hajishirzi, 2020)-there has been little work on generalized quantifiers as a source of error in NLU, let alone in multilingual NLU. Recent computational work on quantification in vision-language grounding highlights data biases and compositional failures (Hudson and Manning, 2019;Miller et al., 2021). The role of monotonicity and conservativity in model behavior has been discussed alongside inference benchmarks (Icard and Moss, 2014;Yanaka et al., 2019). Prior studies on numeracy examine neural sensitivity to approximate number representations and counting proxies (Wallace et al., 2019;Geva et al., 2020). Neural machine translation has also been evaluated for scope preservation (Marvin and Linzen, 2018). It remains an open problem whether LMs represent the semantics of quantifiers words adequately, or if they provide a basis for resolving scopal ambiguities. 1

text: To cope with this problem, approximate calibration methods using a set of interesting events and feature based calibration are proposed in (Kuleshov and Liang, 2015;Jagannatha and Yu, 2020) and an alternative calibration error estimator was proposed using sequence precision scoring function BLEU in (Kumar and Sarawagi, 2019). Recent work has investigated temperature scaling for neural QA systems (Desai and Durrett, 2020), confidence-aware decoding for sequence generation (Kumar and Sarawagi, 2019), and class-conditional calibration for long-tailed recognition (Hong et al., 2021). Calibration under distribution shift has been studied with focal losses and post-hoc mappings (Mukhoti et al., 2020; Ovadia et al., 2019). Label smoothing has also been connected to improved confidence estimates (Müller et al., 2019). Multi-label settings raise additional issues around marginal-vs-joint consistency (Kull et al., 2017).

text: We begin by a background on two span selection tasks considered in our work: coreference resolution and SRL. We describe some previous works on these tasks and contrast them to our work. Coreference resolution seeks to cluster mentions that refer to the same entity across a discourse. SRL aims to identify predicate-argument structures that explain who did what to whom, when, and how. Benchmarks such as OntoNotes and CoNLL 2012 provide standard splits and evaluation protocols for coreference. For SRL, CoNLL 2005 and 2012 datasets remain widely used, while recent work also evaluates on OntoNotes-style annotations. Neural span encoders have become the dominant approach in both tasks due to their flexibility in representing variable-length text spans. The interplay between span scoring, pruning, and global inference is a central design axis in modern systems. Recent neural architectures for coreference resolution emphasize span-based modeling with coarse-to-fine inference (Lee et al., 2017), contextualized encoders for mention scoring (Joshi et al., 2020), and higher-order inference through iterative refinement (Xia et al., 2020). Early feature-engineered systems relied on mention-pair classifiers with hand-crafted compatibility features (Ng and Cardie, 2002; Bengtson and Roth, 2008). End-to-end models integrate mention detection and linking into a single objective that optimizes over latent spans (Lee et al., 2018). Span pruning strategies reduce computational cost by learning salience-aware thresholds (Peters et al., 2018). Biaffine scorers have been adapted to capture antecedent relations in span space (Wu et al., 2020). Attention mechanisms align mentions with discourse cues and speaker roles (Clark and Manning, 2016). For SRL, predicate-argument structures are modeled with span proposals and span-label decoders (He et al., 2018; Tan et al., 2018). Multi-criteria training unifies PropBank-style and FrameNet-style supervision (Marcheggiani and Titov, 2020). Argument span boundary detection benefits from span attention and boundary-sensitive losses (Shi and Lin, 2019). Joint learning with syntactic parses adds structural biases that improve role consistency (Strubell et al., 2018). Despite these advances, cross-task transfer between coreference and SRL remains underexplored. A shared span encoder could, in principle, amortize learning across both tasks without task-specific decoders. Prior multi-task studies often focus on syntactic auxiliaries rather than discourse-level clustering or role labeling. We hypothesize that span-level regularization can improve generalization in low-resource domains. To test this, we design a framework that shares span representations while decoupling task-specific inference. The training objective includes calibration terms that align span length distributions across tasks. Our experiments compare single-task baselines to shared-encoder variants under fixed parameter budgets. We evaluate on English OntoNotes for coreference and CoNLL 2012-style SRL with matched tokenization. The primary metrics are MUC, B^3, and CEAF_phi4 for coreference, and F1 for SRL labeled arguments. We also report calibration error on span length buckets to assess distributional alignment. Ablations remove either the shared encoder or the calibration terms to isolate their contributions. Finally, we analyze errors by span type and discourse position to understand residual brittleness. The results indicate that shared span encoders yield consistent improvements while preserving inference modularity. This suggests practical benefits for joint deployment in information extraction pipelines. We release code and pretrained checkpoints to facilitate reproducibility and fair comparison.

text: Another direction of research has shown impressive results using extremely large models (Radford et al., 2019;Devlin et al., 2019). Using these as a basis for experiments could be an interesting direction, but fine-tuning models in low data settings is known to be difficult and highly variable (Dodge et al., 2020). Similar transformer models have been used for controlled generation. Zellers et al. (2019) developed a model for news generation that conditioned on meta-data including domain, date, authors, and headline. No ablation is performed, and though it would be interesting to compare to a transformer method that conditions on authors alone, we opted for a model that is faster and cheaper to train (Grover-Mega from Zellers et al. (2019) was trained for two weeks and cost around 25k USD). Additionally, when fine-tuning models for new users, little data is available. Contextualized embedding models often require a large amount of data to train effectively, though this type of comparison would be an interesting future direction to explore. Variations of the LSTM have consistently achieved state-of-the-art performance without massive compute resources and thus we chose this architecture for our experiments (Merity et al., 2018a;Melis et al., 2019;Merity, 2019;Li et al., 2020).

text: Shared feature space based models generally train a language-independent encoder using source and target language data (Tsai et al., 2016). Recently, the pre-trained multilingual language models mBERT is effective to address the challenge (Devlin et al., 2019). Moreover, some research introduces new components on top of the mBERT by directly transferring the model learned from labeled source language to that of target languages (Keung et al., 2019). The performance is still weak due to the lack of annotations of target languages. Translation based models generally generate pesudo labeled target data to alleviate target data scarcity. For example, (Wu et al., 2020b;Zhang et al., 2021) gain a improvement by translating the labeled source language to the target language word-by-word. Our model achieves considerable improvement by learning entity similarity in target language data without translation.

text: Knowledge Distillation for PLMs. Knowledge Distillation (KD) (Hinton et al., 2015) aims to compress a large model into a fast-to-execute one. KD has renewed a surge of interest in PLMs recently. Some explore KD at different training phases, e.g., pre-training (Sanh et al., 2019), downstream finetuning (Sun et al., 2019;Krishna et al., 2020), or both of them (Jiao et al., 2020); others explore distilling not only the final logits output by the large PLM, but also the intermediate hidden representations (Sanh et al., 2019;Jiao et al., 2020;Sun et al., 2020). Conventional KD presumes that teacher models play pivotal roles in mastering knowledge, and student models generally cannot match their teachers in performance. Sanh et al. (2019) introduced DistilBERT for efficient pre-training with reduced parameters, Sun et al. (2020) examined layer-wise feature distillation for transformer encoders, Jiao et al. (2020) proposed TinyBERT with task-specific distillation, and Krishna et al. (2020) studied KD during fine-tuning of large-scale PLMs without aligning architectural depth. When it comes to the scenario of KI, since student models have larger capacities, the performance of teacher models is no longer an "upper bound" of student models. Outside NLP, researchers recently demonstrate that a student model could also benefit from a poor teacher for a specific downstream task (Yuan et al., 2020) (reversed KD). Based on the prior explorations, in this paper, we investigate the application of reversed KD in pre-training and conduct a series of empirical studies under various settings.

(2020) studied KD during fine-tuning of large-scale PLMs without aligning architectural depth.

text: For convenience, we work with the formatted versions of these corpora in the GLUE (Wang et al., 2018) and SuperGLUE  benchmarks. The only exception is CommonsenseQA, which is not part of these benchmarks. Related Work Previous work has shown that SNLI (Bowman et al., 2015) and MNLI (Williams et al., 2018) have annotation artifacts (e.g., negation is a strong indicator of contradictions) (Gururangan et al., 2018). The literature has also shown that simple adversarial attacks including negation cues are very effective (Naik et al., 2018;Wallace et al., 2019). Kovatchev et al. (2019) analyze 11 paraphrasing systems and show that they obtain substantially worse results when negation is present. Ribeiro et al. (2020) demonstrate failures on robustness tests that use function words and lexical perturbations. Ettinger (2020) examine masked-token predictions in controlled contexts with negation. Geiger et al. (2020) study monotonicity and report systematic generalization gaps. Yanaka et al. (2019) introduce a diagnostics suite targeting upward and downward entailment with negative contexts. Poliak et al. (2018) discuss hypothesis-only artifacts in entailment datasets. Glockner et al. (2018) expand NLI test sets with lexical substitutions to probe sensitivity to simple changes. Jia and Liang (2017) propose distraction-based adversaries for reading comprehension. Adversarial NLI (Nie et al., 2020) collects harder examples through annotation-game procedures. Williams et al. (2018) provide a broad-coverage NLI benchmark. RTE datasets aggregate multiple sources with varied phenomena (Bar-Haim et al., 2006; Giampiccolo et al., 2007; Bentivogli et al., 2009).
