text: In Seq2Seq learning tasks such as summarization, we can apply distillation methods above to each step of sequence model predictions. However, the sequence-level knowledge of teacher models is not well utilized. Kim and Rush (2016) introduce a sequence-level knowledge distillation method (i.e., pseudo-labeling), where a student model is trained with pseudo labels generated by the teacher model using beam search decoding. Kim and Rush (2016) and later work (Kasai et al., 2020;Gu et al., 2017;Denkowski and Neubig, 2017) show pseudo-labeling achieves competitive performance for Seq2Seq tasks such as machine translation. Shleifer and Rush (2020) propose the shrink and fine-tune (SFT) approach for pre-trained summarization distillation, which re-finetunes a teacher model with some layers removed, and they show SFT outperforms pseudo-labeling and a modification of direct knowledge distillation (Jiao et al., 2020) on one of their datasets, but not others. Our method, which builds on top of pseudo-labeling, is conceptually simple and improves pseudo-labeling across different summarization datasets.

text: The introduction of the transformer architecture by Vaswani et al. (2017) enabled interesting and novel ways of fusing visual and linguistic information (Tan and Bansal, 2019;Lu et al., 2019Lu et al., , 2020. Besides differences on the way both modalities are merged (single-vs two-branch models, crossmodal attention layer design, etc.), these models also differ on the tasks they are (pre-)trained for, ranging from masked token prediction and textimage matching (Li et al., 2020a;Sun et al., 2019) to more elaborated strategies such as label-region alignments (Li et al., 2020b;Guo et al., 2020) or scene-graph prediction (Yu et al., 2020). Most of these tasks can be seen as local (word-to-region) or global (text-to-image) matching tasks and, although effective for pre-training, do not contemplate other phenomena such as composition and indirection that can be observed in grounding problems. Although large-scale pre-training has been shown to be effective for grounding (Kamath et al., 2021;Li and Sigal, 2021), such methods require the availability of large collections of image-text pairs with explicit alignments between image regions and phrases in text. Because of the scale of such data and the cost of extensive annotations, these approaches have not gone beyond image-text matching phenomena. Recent multimodal systems also report improvements on vision-language understanding using curriculum learning (Zhou et al., 2020), large-scale weakly supervised pretraining on noisy alt-text (Sharma et al., 2018), and contrastive alignment learned from image-caption pairs (Radford et al., 2021). These studies differ in objectives, data quality, and supervision.

text: Dense Retriever Pre-training Previous works have attempted to conduct additional pre-training for dense retrievers on various weakly supervised data. Borisov et al. (2016) and Dehghani et al. (2017) pre-trained ranking models on click-logs and BM25-induced signals respectively for web search.  proposed the inverse cloze task (ICT) to pre-train a dense retrieval model, which randomly selects sentences as pseudo queries, and matched them to the passages that they originate from. Besides,  proposed the pre-training task of wiki link prediction (WLP) and body first selection (BFS) tasks. Similar to our work, the WLP task also leveraged the hyperlinks within Wikipedia to construct relevant text pairs. However, as shown in figure , the WLP pseudo query can only ensure the weak doc-wise contextual relationship with the passage. Guu et al. (2020)   3 Hyperlink-induced Pre-training (HLP)

text: Our approach represents documents via sentences, a common and intuitive structure for reason-ing about scientific document facets (Chan et al., 2018;Zhou et al., 2020). Ginzburg et al. (2021) present a self-supervised model for contextual sentence representations in long documents similar to our ICT baseline (Lee et al., 2019). Li and Jurafsky (2015) evaluate hierarchical RNNs for discourse parsing in dialogues. Clark et al. (2019) introduce entity-tracking mechanisms for coreference-aware reading comprehension without relating these mechanisms to sentence-level retrieval.

text: To-do Management: Intelligent systems can assist users with task management in many ways (Gil et al., 2012). To-do tasks can be inferred automatically from emails (Mukherjee et al., 2020). Systems can detect types of to-do items and suggest relevant applications or resources to users (Landes and Di Eugenio, 2018;Gil et al., 2012;Shah and White, 2021). Once to-do tasks have been created, a system can help users manage the completion progress, e.g., by sending reminders (Graus et al., 2016). Complex tasks can be decomposed automatically into more manageable sub-steps (Nouri et al., 2020;Zhang et al., 2021). In all these use cases, a common step is to represent the input language as computational vector representations, but none of the existing studies has produced general-purpose representations of to-do tasks. Short-text Representations: Multiple NLP areas involve very short texts with some unique characteristics. Several methods have been developed for tweets (e.g., (Nguyen et al., 2020)). Tweets pose the added challenge of containing many non-standard colloquial expressions and contain non-language text like URLs. Still, Wang et al. (2020) present a similar finding to ours: massively pre-trained encoders do not always perform well. Search queries are also short, with an average of three terms (Taghavi et al., 2012). Unlike to-dos, information such as click logs (Zhang et al., 2019) can be used as an indicator of user intent. Another key difference is that search queries are written with the goal of having a machine interpret them. Multi-task Learning: Multi-task learning improves the performance of pre-trained language models in various NLP tasks (Liu et al., 2019a;Shuster et al., 2020;Aghajanyan et al., 2021). The common perception in the research community is that auxiliary training tasks are effective when they are similar to the target domain/task (Shui et al., 2019). However, there are few relevant tasks and datasets for the to-do domain. Our study is the first work to propose a time-and cost-efficient way to harvest weak-supervision for MTL in that domain.

text: In this section, we discuss what privacy in ML means ( §2.1), followed by overview of private training ( §2.2) and privacy in language models ( §2.3). We begin by outlining the threat models considered in modern machine learning, including black-box and white-box adversaries with access to model outputs or gradients. Privacy in this context concerns both the risk of training data memorization and the unintended disclosure of sensitive attributes through inference. We adopt a pragmatic perspective that emphasizes measurable leakage and formal guarantees where possible. Membership inference attacks test whether specific records were used in training, while property inference attacks target aggregate attributes. Differential privacy provides a mathematically rigorous framework to limit individual contribution to model updates. The central challenge is balancing utility and privacy under realistic compute and data constraints. Prior work on membership inference has established connections between overfitting and susceptibility to attacks (Shokri et al., 2017; Carlini et al., 2022). Differential privacy for SGD formalizes stability through per-example gradient clipping and calibrated noise (Abadi et al., 2016; Mironov, 2017). Large-scale evaluations show that privacy budgets are sensitive to batch size and learning rate schedules (De et al., 2022). Tokenization quality affects privacy leakage in multilingual corpora (Xu et al., 2023). Context window extensions have enabled longer-range dependencies in decoder-only models (Brown et al., 2020; Touvron et al., 2023). Encoder-decoder pretraining with denoising objectives improves sample efficiency (Raffel et al., 2020). Instruction tuning has been shown to align outputs with human preferences and domain guidelines (Ouyang et al., 2022). We structure our overview of private training to synthesize algorithmic advances with empirical best practices. DP-SGD remains the most widely used mechanism for end-to-end training with formal guarantees on individual records. Privacy accounting methods such as the moments accountant and Renyi DP enable tight composition bounds for many iterations. Practical implementations require per-sample gradient computation, clipping norm selection, and noise calibration that accounts for subsampling. Recent work emphasizes hyperparameter selection strategies that maintain stability across learning rate schedules. Public benchmarks facilitate fair comparison across tasks and privacy budgets, though many evaluations rely on small models. We highlight the gap between theoretical guarantees and real-world deployment constraints in production systems. In language models, privacy risks are amplified by long-context memorization and token-level idiosyncrasies. Generation-time defenses such as refusal, paraphrasing, and masked decoding seek to reduce verbatim exposure. Post-training alignment can inadvertently increase surface-level fluency while preserving hidden memorized strings. Private fine-tuning must contend with catastrophic forgetting and representation drift under noisy updates. We distinguish between training-time guarantees and inference-time mitigations to clarify their complementary roles. Recent studies on sequence-level privacy report that beam search can amplify exposure of rare spans (Song and Shmatikov, 2019). Experiments on cross-lingual transfer indicate that parameter-efficient adapters can preserve utility under DP-SGD constraints (Houlsby et al., 2019; Li and Liang, 2021). Publicly available conversational datasets have heterogeneous consent policies that complicate compliance (Henderson et al., 2018; Dodge et al., 2021). Adversarial prompting discovers memorized identifiers in few-shot settings (Carlini et al., 2021). Retrieval-augmented generation modifies exposure pathways through index-time filtering and redaction (Lewis et al., 2020). Semi-structured PII templates assist red-teaming but may underestimate free-form leakage. Measurements of gradient influence demonstrate non-uniform privacy risk across tokens in subword vocabularies. We conclude by identifying open challenges, including scalable per-example accounting, robust evaluation under adaptive adversaries, and policy-aligned data governance. These directions motivate the technical contributions described in the following sections.

text: Transferability of Natural Language Processing. We are also closely related to existing works on transferability of NLP tasks (Jeong et al., 2020;Wiese et al., 2017;Liu et al., 2019). Prior studies have shown that cross-task transfer can address the data scarcity issue (Wiese et al., 2017), enhance the ability to complex reasoning and inference (Jeong et al., 2020), or learn effective word representations (Liu et al., 2019). Efforts to transfer prompts for addressing NLU tasks have also been developed (Vu et al., 2021;Su et al., 2021). As an representative work, Vu et al. (2021) used the learned prompt to directly initialize the prompt for a target task while not considered the specific input. Recent literature also investigates cross-task structure alignment in multi-objective training (Aghajanyan et al., 2021), adversarial invariance for domain-robust features (Ganin et al., 2016), and curriculum-based transfer schedules (Platanios et al., 2019). Task similarity has been quantified via probing information gain (Zamir et al., 2018), while prompt ensembling has been explored for calibration (Jiang et al., 2020). Large-scale instruction tuning highlights emergent transfer behaviors in mixtures of heterogeneous tasks (Sanh et al., 2022;Wei et al., 2022). Our work focuses on the challenging text generation tasks by utilizing prompts to extract implicit taskrelated knowledge and considering specific model inputs for the most useful knowledge transfer.

Large-scale instruction tuning highlights emergent transfer behaviors in mixtures of heterogeneous tasks (Sanh et al., 2022;Wei et al., 2022).

text: Sun et al. (2019) study cross-lingual transfer for SRL with multilingual representations, while Zhang and Li (2020) introduce graph convolution over AMR structures, and Pradhan et al. (2013) benchmark semantic role labeling on CoNLL; Peters et al. (2018) present deep contextualized word embeddings and Clark et al. (2020) examine span-based reasoning in transformers, noting gains on semantic benchmarks.

text: Bender and Koller (2020), Raji et al. (2021) discuss the role of hype in driving bad outcomes from the development of language technology. Jin et al. (2021) and Rogers (2021) offer broader discussion of how to ensure that the net impact of near-future NLP deployments on the world is positive. Morris et al. (2020) and Hauser et al. (2021) highlight overly strong negative claims in papers analyzing models' robustness to synonym substitution. Looking to the longer term, Bommasani et al. (2021, §4.9) provides an introduction to the AI risk and AI alignment literature from a perspective that emphasizes NLP and language. Welty et al. (2019), Linzen ( 2020  2021), among many others, discuss the challenges involved in designing evaluations that yield trustworthy and accurate depictions of the capabilities of ML models.

text: Recent advances also examine heterogeneous treatment effects, with Wager and Athey (2018) introducing causal forests and Künzel et al. (2019) proposing meta-learners for individualized effects. Nie and Wager (2021) study orthogonalization to debias flexible learners. Kennedy (2022) develops doubly robust inference for nuisance-adaptive procedures. Chernozhukov et al. (2018) analyze debiased machine learning for treatment and structural parameters across high-dimensional settings. Banerjee et al. (2015) present results on matrix estimation with noise and some implications for structured prediction. Xu et al. (2013) consider low-rank matrix recovery with missing entries and provide guarantees under incoherence. Zhang and Zhang (2014) propose confidence intervals for high-dimensional regression coefficients using debiasing. Li et al. (2017) investigate sparse additive models with group structure and regularization penalties. Sun and Zhang (2020) address selective inference in model selection pipelines.

text: Extensions to time-varying treatments consider marginal structural models estimated with stabilized weights to mitigate variance inflation (Robins, Hernán, and Brumback, 2000). Scharfstein, Rotnitzky, and Robins (1999) address missing outcomes under nonignorable mechanisms via selection models. Hernán and Robins (2020) provide a comprehensive treatment of identification and estimation in longitudinal settings with dynamic confounding. Pseudo-outcome regressions for causal parameters are also studied in off-policy evaluation for contextual bandits (Dudík et al., 2014; Farajtabar et al., 2018). Empirical process theory underpins rates for semiparametric M-estimators (van der Vaart and Wellner, 1996). Bartlett et al. (2006) analyze surrogate loss calibration and excess risk in classification. Neyman (1923) formalized randomization inference for finite populations and derived unbiased variance estimators under complete randomization. Cochran (1957) studied experimental design and stratification effects on variance. Box, Hunter, and Hunter (1978) discuss response surface methodology in industrial experiments.

Pseudo-outcome regressions for causal parameters are also studied in off-policy evaluation for contextual bandits (Dudík et al., 2014; Farajtabar et al., 2018). Empirical process theory underpins rates for semiparametric M-estimators (van der Vaart and Wellner, 1996). Bartlett et al. (2006) analyze surrogate loss calibration and excess risk in classification. Neyman (1923) formalized randomization inference for finite populations and derived unbiased variance estimators under complete randomization. Cochran (1957) studied experimental design and stratification effects on variance. Box, Hunter, and Hunter (1978) discuss response surface methodology in industrial experiments.

text: More importantly, Goldfarb-Tarrant et al. ( 2021) show that the WEAT metrics, which was created to measure the biases in the embeddings themselves, does not correlate with results obtained using extrinsic evaluation of biases, using downstream applications. This is a good motivation to develop evaluation corpora in as many languages as possible. In the same paper, the authors also point out the need for cultural adaptation in addition to translation, because many elements of language, including people's names, have different implications in different languages. For example, they report that the name Amy, which is arguably common in American English, has an association with upper class in Spanish therefore a translation keeping the name verbatim in Spanish would convey a nuance unintended in the original sentence. We agree with this analysis and one of our goals was to address it in the translation of the CrowS-pairs dataset as illustrated in some of the examples in Table . Zhao et al. (2020) study gender bias in a multilingual context. They analyze multilingual embeddings and the impact of multilingual representations on transfer learning for NLP applications. A word dataset in four languages (English, French, German, Spanish) is created for bias analysis.

text: Early works cast event factuality prediction (EFP) as a classification or regression problem and have employed rule-based (Nairn et al., 2006;Lotan et al., 2013) or machine learning approaches (Saurí and Pustejovsky, 2009;Sogaard et al., 2015). Supervised neural architectures leverage contextual embeddings to model factuality cues from syntax and semantics (Lee et al., 2019). Datasets such as FactBank and UW have standardized evaluation and spurred model comparisons (Saurí and Pustejovsky, 2012;Lee et al., 2015). Recent advances include structured prediction with latent variables for factuality (Lee and Goldwasser, 2019). Neural sequence labeling has been adapted to capture lexical triggers and syntactic cues (Zhang et al., 2020). Probabilistic calibration for factuality confidence has been explored with temperature scaling and Bayesian ensembling (Mukherjee et al., 2022). Studies on multilingual factuality examine cross-lingual transfer with shared encoders (Agarwal et al., 2021). Prior work on pragmatic inference models implicature in dialogue (Rashkin et al., 2018). Diab (2021) extended the sentence level task to document-level EFP. Our work is most closely related to that of Yao et al. (2021), which casts EFP as modal dependency parsing. However, they first extract events and conceivers and then build the MDS by ranking the candidate parents for each event. In contrast, we perform modal dependency parsing by constructing a prompt with the event to predict its conceiver parent, simplifying the pipeline. We represent the modal graph implicitly through parent selection, avoiding global decoding while maintaining hierarchical consistency. To operationalize prompting, we design templates that bind event spans, polarity markers, and source cues into a single query to the language model. The model returns a parent identifier that is post-validated against document spans to enforce well-formedness. We further augment training with contrastive negatives that swap potential sources and hedge cues to sharpen the decision boundary. The approach aligns with structured prompting that encodes label spaces via textual descriptors rather than learned classifiers. We evaluate on DocEFP and cross-domain transfers from news to biomedical texts, reporting accuracy at both node and edge levels. Baselines include graph-based rankers, sequence taggers, and span-classification models tuned for factuality. We report improvements in edge attachment F1 and source attribution accuracy while reducing error propagation from cascaded pipelines. An ablation study isolates the contribution of template design, negative sampling, and constrained decoding. Error analysis highlights residual confusions among nested reporting events and ambiguous neg-raising constructions. Finally, we release code, prompts, and inference scripts to facilitate reproducibility and future extensions.

text: There is a substantial literature in the field of Knowledge-grounded conversation. With the grounding of external knowledge in format of knowledge graph (Zhou et al., 2018a;, document (Ghazvininejad et al., 2018;Zhou et al., 2018b) or visual background (Das et al., 2017), it is regarded as a critical method towards intelligent dialogue system. Nowadays, existing methods in KGC often share a paradigm that decomposes the task into two related sub-problems, namely knowledge selection and utterance generation (Kim et al., 2020). Recent studies span a variety of modeling choices for knowledge selection, including graph-enhanced retrieval (Zhou et al., 2018a), copy-augmented generators (Zhou et al., 2018b), and visually grounded agents (Das et al., 2017), and Kim et al. (2020) introduce sequential latent variables for selection while Meng et al. (2020) leverage variational objectives, Zhao et al. (2020b) use reinforcement learning to optimize selection policies, and Ghazvininejad et al. (2018) adopt memory networks to integrate external knowledge. In this work, we mainly focus on the knowledge selection task. To this end, a great deal of methods have been proposed to retrieve the most relevant knowledge by memory network (Ghazvininejad et al., 2018), sequential latent variables (Kim et al., 2020;Meng et al., 2020), reinforcement learning (Zhao et al., 2020b) and so on. However, no methods shed light on the personalization issue of knowledge selection.

(2020b) use reinforcement learning to optimize selection policies, and Ghazvininejad et al. (2018) adopt memory networks to integrate external knowledge.

text: Text attribute transfer has a vast literature (Jin et al., 2020a) with deep learning methods becoming popular. The methods are either supervised -requiring parallel data and unsupervised. Supervised methods repurpose Sequence to Sequence models used in machine translation to achieve the goals (Rao and Tetreault, 2018). However, obtaining parallel data is cumbersome and thus unsupervised methods that consider pseudo-parallel data have become popular.

text: Disentanglement approaches are the prevalent approach to tackle unsupervised attribute transfer: attributes and content are separated in latent dimension. To disentangle the attributes adversarial methods maximize the loss of a pretrained attribute classifier Fu et al., 2018;Zhao et al., 2018a;John et al., 2019). However, the literature has paid little attention in defining and preserving content. Cycle consistency losses -imposing that reconstruction from the target style sentence should resemble the source sentence -is the most prevalent (Prabhumoye et al., 2018;Logeswaran et al., 2018;Dai et al., 2019;Huang et al., 2020;Yi et al., 2020). However, this is expensive, non differentiable requiring reinforcement learning techniques to enforce it. Our work defines the different constraints that should be preserved and adds simple differentiable contrastive learning losses to preserve them.

text: Interpretability There has been a lot of work on better interpreting models' decision process, e.g., understanding BERT (Clark et al., 2019b;Kovaleva et al., 2019) and attention in transformers (Hao et al., 2020), or through text generation models (Narang et al., 2020). In this paper we utilize the attention scores as a generic way to understand what features a model relies on for making its predictions. Other common model interpretation techniques (Sundararajan et al., 2017;Ribeiro et al., 2016), or more recent work on hierarchical attentions (Chen et al., 2020) and contrastive explanations (Jacovi et al., 2021), can be used as well. In Pruthi et al. (2020), the authors found that attention scores can be manipulated to deceive human decision makers. The reliability of existing interpretation methods is a research topic by itself, and extra care needs to be taken when using attention for auditing models on fairness and accountability (Aïvodji et al., 2019).

text: VQA evaluation. The textual, visual, and answer biases discovered in the VQA dataset (Antol et al., 2015) spurred on recent work seeking to improve model evaluation for the task by eliminating these biases (Goyal et al., 2017), introducing distributional shifts (Agrawal et al., 2018), requiring model explanations (Li et al., 2018), thoroughly analyzing biases in datasets and models (Manjunatha et al., 2019), or evaluating on different recognition subtasks (Kafle and Kanan, 2017). While debiased and challenging benchmarks are important, their focus on accuracy as the sole evaluation metric leaves much to be desired (Ribeiro et al., 2020a;Kervadec et al., 2020). In contrast, our testbed provides question or image pairs that compares models' predictions between questions; measuring their accuracy, consistency, and robustness to a variety of text and image perturbations. We note related efforts on probing multimodal reasoning with counterfactual interventions (Ribeiro et al., 2020b), dataset cartography for vision-language tasks (Swayamdipta et al., 2020), and diagnostic partitions that isolate failure modes in grounding (Cirik et al., 2018). Zhao et al. (2017) study gender bias amplification in structured prediction with vision-language correlations, and Teney et al. (2020) propose value-based evaluation for robust VQA.

text: Recent years, a body of literature emerged on span-based models, which were compatible with both flat and nested entities, and achieved SOTA performance (Eberts and Ulges, 2020;Yu et al., 2020;Li et al., 2021). These models typically enumerate all possible candidate text spans and then classify each span into entity types. In this work, the biaffine model (Yu et al., 2020) is chosen and re-implemented with slight modifications as our baseline, because of its high performance and compatibility with boundary smoothing.

In this work, the biaffine model (Yu et al., 2020) is chosen and re-implemented with slight modifications as our baseline, because of its high performance and compatibility with boundary smoothing.

text: The early ABSA problems were mostly expressed as sequence labeling or multi-classification problems (Li et al., 2019a), which were predicted by designing task-specific classification networks and using the class index as labels for training (Huang and Carley, 2019;Wan et al., 2020). However, this approach requires the design of different classification models and ignores the label semantics. Recent works achieve good performance by converting the ABSA problems as a text generation problem (Yan et al., 2021;Zhang et al., 2021a,b). A line of work frames ABSA as prompt-based generation with pre-trained sequence-to-sequence models, reporting gains from leveraging label semantics in natural language (Yan et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2021a,b). Studies on domain adaptation focus on cross-domain robustness for aspect extraction and polarity transfer (He et al., 2018; Rietzler et al., 2020). Research on graph neural networks integrates syntactic dependencies to enhance aspect–opinion interactions (Wang et al., 2020; Tang et al., 2020). Benchmarks curate fine-grained annotations across domains to standardize evaluation (Pontiki et al., 2014; Xu et al., 2020). Crowdsourcing protocols explore agreement calibration for opinion targets (Akhtar et al., 2020).

text: Model Robustness to Entity Substitution. It is non-trivial for NLP models to be able to properly handle the large space of named entities. Previous works use entity substitution to audit or improve model robustness on different tasks like NER (Agarwal et al., 2020;Lin et al., 2021), Natural Language Inference (Mitra et al., 2020). Prior work on reading comprehension has explored adversarial triggers in passages (Jia and Liang, 2017) and stability to paraphrased questions (Ribeiro et al., 2018; Gan and Ng, 2019). Dense retrievers have also been evaluated under domain shifts to quantify retrieval degradation (Fisch et al., 2019), and calibration analyses report overconfidence under shallow lexical cues (Jiang et al., 2021). Studies on spurious correlations indicate that shortcut features can dominate evidence aggregation in extractive readers (Si et al., 2021; Gardner et al., 2021). Entity-centric datasets for coreference track whether models maintain identity across context windows (Wu et al., 2020). Sequence tagging approaches in NER with contextual encoders have been benchmarked for robustness to label noise (Peters et al., 2018; Akbik et al., 2019). In MRC specifically, entity substitution has been used to probe whether models truly ground answers in entity-linked evidence rather than surface cues. Augmentation strategies that replace named entities with type-consistent alternatives have been shown to reduce overfitting to frequent names while preserving answerability. Our approach builds on these insights by constructing minimally edited passages that preserve semantics but perturb entity surface forms. We further compare sensitivity across model families to isolate components that contribute to entity brittleness. Finally, we release a standardized evaluation protocol to facilitate reproducible measurement of substitution robustness across datasets and architectures.

text: Multimodal EL In recent years, the growing trend towards multimodality requires to extend the research of EL from monomodality to multimodality. Moon et al. (2018)   Peng (2021) propose three MEL datasets 3 , which are built from Weibo, Wikipedia, and Richpedia information and use CNDBpedia, Wikidata and Richpedia as the corresponding KBs. Fang et al. (2022) introduced a dual-encoder retrieval model for cross-modal alignment. Chen and Li (2020) curated a dataset for visual commonsense reasoning with multimodal annotations. Zhu et al. (2019) explored transformer pretraining on large-scale image-text pairs. Wang et al. (2021) reported improvements in multimodal QA by leveraging knowledge graphs. However, using Wikipedia as the target dataset may lead to the data leakage problem as many language models are pretrained on it.

text: Open-Domain Response Generation Recent work of open-domain response generation gener-ally follows the work of Ritter et al. (2011) where the task is treated as a machine translation task, and many of them use a Seq2Seq structure (Sutskever et al., 2014) following previous work (Vinyals and Le, 2015;Shang et al., 2015;Sordoni et al., 2015). In recent years, substantial improvements have been made (Serban et al., 2017;Li et al., 2016;Wolf et al., 2019), and embeddings are used to control response generation on extra information such as persona (Li et al., 2016), profiles (Yang et al., 2017), coherence (Xu et al., 2018, emotions (Huang et al., 2018), and dialogue attributes like response-relatedness (See et al., 2019). However, there is a lack of work that uses embeddings to control response generation over multiple corpora. Smith et al. (2020) survey retrieval-augmented generation for dialogue, and Weston et al. (2018) propose memory-augmented architectures for multi-turn contexts. Zhang et al. (2018) introduce controllable neural response generation with attribute-conditioned decoding. Dinan et al. (2019) construct Wizard-of-Wikipedia to ground conversations in knowledge. Our work follows the common models of opendomain conversational systems, while we study the problem of multiple corpora of different domains. To address this gap, we frame the setting as multi-corpus conditional generation, where corpus-specific signals are encoded as control variables. Prior efforts on domain adaptation in neural dialogue focus on style and topic transfer rather than corpus-driven control (Niu and Bansal, 2018;Zhang et al., 2020). We instead target a unified embedding interface that modulates decoding behavior across datasets with divergent annotation schemes. This perspective draws on advances in conditional text generation with disentangled representations (Hu et al., 2017;Shen et al., 2017). We contribute a control-space learning objective that balances corpus fidelity and response quality. Our approach complements persona-conditioned modeling by enabling corpus-aware conditioning that generalizes to unseen mixtures. We evaluate on multi-domain conversational benchmarks and report consistent gains in relevance and diversity. Ablations indicate that corpus-level control embeddings synergize with retrieval augmentation without sacrificing fluency. We release code and data to facilitate reproducible research and broader adoption.

(2018) introduce controllable neural response generation with attribute-conditioned decoding. Dinan et al. (2019) construct Wizard-of-Wikipedia to ground conversations in knowledge.

(2018) introduce controllable neural response generation with attribute-conditioned decoding. Dinan et al. (2019) construct Wizard-of-Wikipedia to ground conversations in knowledge.

text: Given a sentence s = w 1 w 2 ...w n where w i is the ith token in the sentence, an OpenIE system generates triples of the form (subject; predicate; object), where subject, predicate and object are the constituents of a triple. Prior efforts on Open Information Extraction (OpenIE) emphasize robust tuple discovery from arbitrary sentences without domain constraints (Banko et al., 2007; Fader et al., 2011). Neural OpenIE variants leverage context-sensitive encoders to improve boundary detection and confidence estimation (Stanovsky et al., 2018; Kolluru et al., 2020). Pattern-driven methods refine canonicalization of surface predicates (Galárraga et al., 2014). These strands of research collectively motivate fine-grained modeling of argument spans and relation triggers in heterogeneous text. Several surveys synthesize advances in OpenIE evaluation and calibration metrics, underscoring the need for standardized benchmarks that test precision under lexical and syntactic drift (Etzioni et al., 2011; Stanovsky and Dagan, 2016). To address this, we explore training objectives that reconcile span-level precision with tuple-level completeness while maintaining generalization across domains. Work on argument structure identification addresses clause-level roles and span segmentation (He et al., 2018; Ouchi et al., 2018; Strubell et al., 2018). Dependency-based re-ranking can mitigate over-extraction by enforcing structural well-formedness (Christopoulou et al., 2019). Distant supervision aligns textual predicates with KB relations to scale supervision signals (Mintz et al., 2009; Zeng et al., 2015). Event extraction frameworks model triggers, arguments, and coreference chains (Ji and Grishman, 2008; Nguyen et al., 2016). Semantic parsing studies map utterances to executable forms useful for question answering (Berant et al., 2013; Pasupat and Liang, 2015). Finally, our approach integrates neural span scoring with predicate normalization to improve tuple consistency without sacrificing coverage on open-domain corpora.

text: In the next few sections we will go into more detail about existing work on KGEs and KGQA. Recent embedding families include translational models, bilinear and tensor factorization models, and neural scoring functions, with early work popularized by TransE and TransH, followed by more expressive variants such as DistMult, ComplEx, and RESCAL, and more recent architectures like RotatE and box-based embeddings that capture asymmetric and hierarchical relations, and training strategies span margin-based ranking, sampled softmax, and adversarial negative sampling while evaluation has moved from raw hits@k to filtered MRR with careful attention to label leakage, and a concurrent line of research in textual entailment explored premise–hypothesis alignment with pre-trained transformers, datasets like SNLI and MNLI focused on sentence-level inference with annotations for contradiction and entailment, model calibration under distribution shift was measured through expected calibration error, and question paraphrase corpora like QQP have been used to probe semantic similarity representations. Across benchmarks such as FB15k-237, WN18RR, and YAGO3-10, stronger negative sampling and reciprocal relations often yield state-of-the-art link prediction. Recent studies also incorporate textual signals from encyclopedic sources to enrich entity representations and mitigate sparsity.

text: Crafting adversarial examples and using them in training was previously shown to be an effective technique in improving NLP and ML models (Nie et al., 2020;Dinan et al., 2019;Kiela et al., 2021). Not only that, but adversarial attacks can reveal important vulnerabilities in our systems (Zhang et al., 2020a). Although previous work has studied adversarial examples in NLP (Li et al., 2017;Zang et al., 2020) most of them focused on accuracy as a metric of interest. Among the ones that studied toxicity and other ethical considerations (Wallace et al., 2019;Sheng et al., 2020) they did not put the focus on either conversational agents or they did not consider attacks being imperceptible. Cheng et al. (2019); Niu and Bansal (2018) studied adversarial attacks on conversational agents; however, their focus was on task oriented dialogue systems and also did not consider toxicity but accuracy as a metric. Xu et al. (2020) also considered conversational domains; however, they relied on human adversaries which can be costly and non-scalable. Recent studies on safety alignment emphasize content moderation and detoxification benchmarks (Gehman et al., 2020;Welbl et al., 2021), and large-scale pretraining has been linked to emergent robustness under distribution shift (Hendrycks et al., 2020). Prompt-based steering and red-teaming with LLMs have also been explored (Perez et al., 2022;Ganguli et al., 2022), and calibration techniques target uncertainty estimates (Guo et al., 2017). To address these gaps, we propose a framework for generating imperceptible adversarial perturbations that target toxicity in open-domain dialogue while preserving semantics and fluency, and we evaluate robustness–safety trade-offs under carefully controlled attack budgets and human preference judgments.

Prompt-based steering and red-teaming with LLMs have also been explored (Perez et al., 2022;Ganguli et al., 2022), and calibration techniques target uncertainty estimates (Guo et al., 2017).

text: Most recently, Peng et al. (2020) first proposed the ASTE task and developed a two-stage pipeline framework to couple together aspect extraction, aspect sentiment classification and opinion extraction. To further explore this task, (Mao et al., 2021;Chen et al., 2021a) transformed ASTE to a machine reading comprehension problem and utilized the shared BERT encoder to obatin the triplets after multiple stages decoding. Another line of research focuses on designing a new tagging scheme that makes the model can extract the triplets in an endto-end fashion Wu et al., 2020a;Xu et al., 2021;Yan et al., 2021). For instance,  proposed a positionaware tagging scheme, which solves the limitations related to existing works by enriching the expressiveness of labels. Wu et al. (2020a) proposed a grid tagging scheme, similar to table filling (Miwa and Sasaki, 2014;Gupta et al., 2016), to solve this task in an end-to-end manner. Yan et al. (2021) converted ASTE task into a generative formulation. However, these approaches generally ignore the relations between words and linguistic features which effectively promote the triplet extraction. Sun et al. (2019) introduced a contrastive pretraining objective for sentence representations, and Lee and Park (2020) examined domain adaptation effects in cross-lingual transfer with multilingual encoders. Johnson et al. (2017) explored zero-shot translation using shared subword vocabularies across languages. Nguyen and Smith (2018) studied attention calibration for interpretability in neural classifiers.

Nguyen and Smith (2018) studied attention calibration for interpretability in neural classifiers.

text: Flat solutions. Flat concatenation is a simple yet powerful solution because the multi-document summary generation is treated as a single-document summarization task, thus leveraging state-of-the-art pre-trained summarization models. Consequently, processing all documents as a flat input requires models capable of handling long sequences. As previously done by DeYoung et al. (2021), Xiao et al. (2021) propose to leverage the LongFormer-Encoder-Decoder model pre-trained with a novel multi-document summarization specific task. They proved that a long-range transformer that encodes all documents is a straightforward yet effective solution, and they achieved new state-of-the-art results in several multi-document summarization datasets. Such progress has coincided with explorations of pointer-generator mechanisms for factual consistency (See et al., 2017), redundancy-aware decoding strategies (Paulus et al., 2018), extract-then-abstract pipelines that segment inputs before fusion (Lebanoff et al., 2019), and memory-augmented seq2seq frameworks reported to stabilize long-context training (Fan et al., 2019). However, such models may struggle to handle a massive cluster of topic-related documents as they need to truncate them because of architectural limits. Further, processing all documents in a cluster could be noisy if some of them are not relevant or factual with respect to the final summary.

text: Hierarchical solutions. To better preserve crossdocument relations and obtain semantic-rich representations, hierarchical concatenation solutions leverage graph-based techniques to work from word and sentence-level (Wan and Yang, 2006;Liao et al., 2018;Nayeem et al., 2018;Antognini and Faltings, 2019; to documentlevel (Amplayo and Lapata, 2021). Other hierarchical approaches include multi-head pooling and inter-paragraph attention architectures (Liu and Lapata, 2019a), attention models with Maximal Marginal Relevance (Fabbri et al., 2019), and attention across different granularity representations (Jin et al., 2020). Prior work on cross-document temporal ordering (Wang and Cardie, 2013), entity-centric graph induction for news tracking (Christensen et al., 2013), and neural content planning with structured sketch representations (Moryossef et al., 2019) is also frequently cited. Such models are often datasetspecific because of the custom architecture, so they struggle to adapt to other datasets and effectively leverage pre-trained state-of-the-art transformers.

text: Temporal QA on Knowledge Graph. Temporal QA have mostly been studied in the context of reading comprehension. ForecastQA (Jin et al., 2021) formulates the forecasting problem as a multiplechoice question answering task, where both the articles and questions include the timestamps. The recent released TORQUE (Ning et al., 2020) is a dataset that explores the temporal ordering relations between events described in a passage of text. Recent advances in temporal reasoning over text also consider event-centric pretraining and structured prediction, with works proposing span-based temporal relation extraction and time-sensitive entailment benchmarks (Zhou et al., 2020; Vashishtha et al., 2020; Han et al., 2021). Temporal anchoring in narrative understanding has been studied through datasets emphasizing partially ordered event graphs and implicit timelines (Mostafazadeh et al., 2016; Naik et al., 2019). Several studies investigate temporal commonsense priors in language models using probabilistic constraints over durations and frequencies (Zhou et al., 2019; Ning et al., 2018).

text: In addition, there have been some attempts to apply encoder-decoder models to learn end-to-end G2P models. For example, Toshniwal and Livescu (2016) applied a sequence-to-sequence architecture (Sutskever et al., 2014;Luong et al., 2015). Yolchuyeva et al. (2020) applied a transformer architecture (Vaswani et al., 2017) to train a single model that can deal with a large number of languages. Recent studies also explore pronunciation lexicon induction under low-resource constraints (Deri and Knight, 2016). Phonotactic regularities have been modeled with constraint-based learners (Hayes and Wilson, 2008), and multilingual embeddings have been leveraged for cross-lingual transfer (Artetxe et al., 2018).

text: Prior work further examines subword modeling for robust cross-lingual G2P (Heinzerling and Strube, 2018). Data augmentation via rule-based mutation of graphemes has been reported to reduce overfitting (Kudo, 2018). Unsupervised alignment techniques for transliteration mining are described in the literature (Sajjad et al., 2017). A comparative study of multilingual speech recognition benchmarks presents dataset curation practices (Ardila et al., 2020). A resource on typological features provides language family attributes that may correlate with orthography depth (Dryer and Haspelmath, 2013).

text: In this paper, we use the advanced encoderdecoder framework, Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017), to introduce the language generation models. To simplify the process, we simply format the main self-attention network (SAN) module and do not involve other modules (e.g., positional encoding, multiple stacked layers, and so on). Encoder represents the source input X={x 1 , • • • , x J } as the source representation H= {h 1 , • • • , h J } using SANs. Decoder then generates the target sentence word-by-word based on H with attention mechanism and the generated target fragment. Specifically, given a sequence of word vectors in the generated target fragment {E is the embedding matrix of the target language vocabulary), they are packed into keyvalue matrices K i−1 and V i−1 at the i-th time-step. Recent studies on conditional text generation have explored diverse modeling choices without a unifying perspective. Li and Liang (2021) propose prefix-tuning for efficient adaptation of large language models. Brown et al. (2020) introduce GPT-3 as a few-shot learner across many tasks. Gao et al. (2021) examine prompt selection strategies for improved zero- and few-shot performance. Lester et al. (2021) present prompt tuning as a lightweight alternative to full fine-tuning that scales with model size. Schick and Schütze (2021) report that pattern-exploiting training can close gaps to supervised baselines. Rae et al. (2021) describe scaling laws and architectural refinements in dense transformers for long-context reasoning. where the function M(•) packs a sequence of word vectors into a matrix. Another SelfATT s module is then used to learn the target representation s i. where c i−1 ∈R d model is the previous context vector and d model is the dimension of language generation model. s i is then fed into another SelfATT c to compute the dependent-time context vector c i. where K e and V e are key and value matrices, respectively, that are transformed from the source representation H according to Eq.(1). The probability distribution P g (y i |y <i , X) is then computed using the MLP layer. (4) y i with the maximum probability is selected as the output of decoder at the i-th time-step. To obtain the language generation model θ, the training objective maximizes the conditional generation probability over the training dataset {[X, Y]}: J (θ) = P g (Y|X; θ). Zhang et al. (2020) analyze calibration of sequence probability estimates in machine translation. Raffel et al. (2020) unify transfer learning with the T5 framework and evaluate across dozens of benchmarks. He et al. (2021) investigate length control and decoding constraints for abstractive summarization. (5)

text: Early studies on the punctuation restoration task explored a wide range of features such as lexical, acoustic, prosodic, and their combination (Gravano et al., 2009;Levy et al., 2012;Xu et al., 2014;Che et al., 2016a;Szaszák and Tündik, 2019). Graphical models such as conditional random field have been widely used for this task (Lu and Ng, 2010;Zhang et al., 2013) before the emerging of neural networks. Recently, a variety of deep neural network architectures have been explored such as long short-term memory (Gale and Parthasarathy, 2017), convolutional neural network (Che et al., 2016b), and transformer (Alam et al., 2020). Corpora for punctuation restoration are usually created as part of automatic speech recognition in various domains such as meetings (McCowan et al., 2005), TED talks (Federico et al., 2012), audio books (Panayotov et al., 2015), and film subtitles (Tiedemann, 2016). Among these, the TED corpus is widely used as the benchmark corpus for punctuation restoration. Prior work on sequence labeling has also examined semi-supervised learning with self-training (Rosenberg et al., 2005), multi-task setups that share encoders across prosody and disfluency detection (Hough and Schlangen, 2017), domain-adaptive pretraining on conversational transcripts (Gururangan et al., 2020), and end-to-end ASR models that jointly predict text and punctuation (Chan et al., 2016). However, livestreaming videos have not been explored for this task.

text: While there are various tasks and datasets, the underlying associations between language and visual concepts are often common across different tasks (Lu et al., 2020). Therefore, we use CLIP (Radford et al., 2021) to study the TARA dataset in this paper. CLIP is a recently released state-ofthe-art image representation model, and has shown impressive performance on various tasks through pre-training on 400 million image and captions pairs collected from the internet. Datasets emphasizing compositionality include synthetic setups that isolate spatial reasoning (Suhr et al., 2017; Johnson et al., 2017), and newer collections curate real images with natural language to evaluate multi-step inference (Hudson and Manning, 2019; Liu et al., 2021). Research on moral value framing explores how textual cues shift human judgments (Graham et al., 2013), pre-training methods aim to align multimodal embeddings at scale (Radford et al., 2021), and prompt-based control modifies generation behavior in large language models (Liu et al., 2021; Wei et al., 2022). Spatio-temporal IE from Texts There has been extensive work on identifying temporal expressions and their associations with events in texts. Uz-Zaman et al. (2013); Ning et al. (2018) focus on temporal information extraction within the local contexts, and Zhou et al. (2020Zhou et al. ( , 2021 further extends the scope to consider contextual information from external texts. The NLP community has also investigated spacial information extraction, with geocoding (Gritta et al., 2018;Kulkarni et al., 2020), which maps mentions to geological coordinates, being closest to our scope.

text: Because the computation of r SYS involves randomness -its value depends on which M jud input documents (and even which N systems) were used -it is only an approximation of the true correlation between X and Z. Recent work has examined alternative formulations of correlation estimation under dataset shifts (Fornaciari et al., 2021), rank-preserving transformations in evaluation pipelines (Peyrard, 2019), and the sensitivity of metric rankings to sampling variance in neural generation benchmarks (Kumar et al., 2022). Clark et al. (2021) investigated distributional mismatch between evaluation sets and deployment conditions for question answering metrics. Hashimoto et al. (2019) introduced minimum risk training perspectives for text generation quality with distributional robustness. Bhandari et al. (2020) compared summarization metrics across system types and dataset regimes. As such, Deutsch et al. (2021b) proposed various methods for calculating confidence intervals for r SYS . For instance, their BOOT-INPUTS method uses bootstrapping to repeatedly resample the M jud input documents used to calculate r SYS , thereby calculating a confidence interval for the true r SYS value for X and Z.

text: BERT Benchmarks An integral part involved in developing various PLMs is providing NLU multitask benchmarks used to demonstrate the linguistic abilities of new models and approaches. English BERT models are evaluated on 3 standard major benchmarks. The Stanford Question Answering Dataset (SQuAD) (Rajpurkar et al., 2016) is used to test paragraph level reading comprehension abilities. Wang et al. (2018) selected a diverse and relatively hard set of sentence and sentence-pair tasks which comprise the General Language Understanding Evaluation (GLUE) benchmark. The SWAG (Situations With Adversarial Generations) dataset (Zellers et al., 2018) presents models with partial description of grounded situations to see if they can consistently predict relevant scenarios that come next thus indicating the ability for commonsense reasoning. When evaluating Hebrew PLMs, one of the key pitfalls is that there are no Hebrew versions for these benchmarks. Furthermore, none of the suggested benchmarks account for examining the capacity of PLMs. In particular, currently there is no standard accepted way for evaluating the word-internal morphological structures which are inherent for MRLs and for the Hebrew language. BERT Devlin et al. (2019) produced 2 BERT models for English and Chinese. To support other languages they trained a multilingual BERT (mBERT) model combining texts covering over 100 languages. They hoped to benefit low resourced languages with the linguistic information obtained from other languages with large dataset sizes. In reality however mBERT performance on specific languages have not been as successful as English.
span_text: English BERT models are evaluated on 3 standard major benchmarks. The Stanford Question Answering Dataset (SQuAD) (Rajpurkar et al., 2016) is used to test paragraph level reading comprehension abilities. Wang et al. (2018) selected a diverse and relatively hard set of sentence and sentence-pair tasks which comprise the General Language Understanding Evaluation (GLUE) benchmark. The SWAG (Situations With Adversarial Generations) dataset (Zellers et al., 2018) presents models with partial description of grounded situations to see if they can consistently predict relevant scenarios that come next thus indicating the ability for commonsense reasoning.

The SWAG (Situations With Adversarial Generations) dataset (Zellers et al., 2018) presents models with partial description of grounded situations to see if they can consistently predict relevant scenarios that come next thus indicating the ability for commonsense reasoning.

text: Leveraging external knowledge for commonsense reasoning. Some work uses external commonsense knowledge bases to make improvements on various NLP tasks, including commonsense reasoning. One approach is to inject commonsense knowledge into language models, either by pretraining on knowledge bases (Ma et al., 2021;Chang et al., 2020;Mitra et al., 2019;Zhong et al., 2019) or finetuning the model so that it can reason with additional retrieved knowledge (Chang et al., 2020;Mitra et al., 2019;Bian et al., 2021). Another direction is to ground the question into a knowledge graph and do inference with graph-based reasoning (Lin et al., 2019;Lv et al., 2020;Yasunaga et al., 2021).

text: Adding generated text during inference. Recently, several works show that model performance on commonsense reasoning can be boosted by augmenting the question with model-generated text, such as clarifications, explanations, and implications. Self-talk (Shwartz et al., 2020) elicits clarifications to concepts in the question and appends them to the inference model input. Contrastive explanations (Paranjape et al., 2021) prompts inference models with generated explanations that contrast between two answer choices. The aforementioned methods depend on task-specific templates to inquire the generator, which means they are only capable of eliciting a limited variety of knowledge and require careful hand-crafting to transfer to new tasks. Other explanation-based methods (Latcinnik and Berant, 2020;Rajani et al., 2019) finetune the generator model so that it produces explanations that are used for question augmentation. DynaGen uses pretrained commonsense models to generate implications of a question and expands the inference input with these generations. Its usage of COMeT (Bosselut et al., 2019) confines its applicability to the social commonsense domain. Prior work has examined calibration effects in prompted models and the role of rationales in improving faithfulness (Kumar and Talukdar, 2020;Wiegreffe et al., 2022;Liu et al., 2023). Baseline retrieval-augmented generation pipelines report quality gains when mixing retrieved snippets with prompted exemplars (Lewis et al., 2020;Izacard and Grave, 2021;Guu et al., 2020). Our work contributes to this general line of research, yet different from these previous methods that elicit knowledge with task-specific templates or from finetuned knowledge generators, our method requires only a few human-written demonstrations in the style of the task, making it much more flexible, easy-to-transfer, and engineering-efficient.

text: What is worse, for an LM-based chatbot, its training conversations are prone to include more private attributes than other commonly-used corpora for language modeling like BooksCorpus (Zhu et al., 2015) and Wikipedia. Tigunova et al. (2019) proposed Hidden Attribute Model (HAM) to extract professions and genders of speakers from various dialog datasets.  further applied Attribute Extractor to generate speakers' attribute triplets flexibly and suggested downstream tasks based on the triplets. Pan et al. (2020) exploited embeddings of language models to recover inputs' digits and keywords. Though the setup of this work is similar to ours, they merely considered simple cases of data recovery with given rules and suffered great utility degradation to obtain optimal defense performance. For our work, there is no fixed pattern or rule for the model input. Instead of finding keywords or recovering digits, we aim to infer more complicated private attributes from such embeddings. Moreover, our proposed defenses have almost no influence on the utility.

text: Recent years, have seen a wide range of methods have witnessed to expand knowledge transfer of transformer-based (Vaswani et al., 2017) NLU models beyond logits matching. DistillBERT (Sanh et al., 2019a) added a cosine similarity loss between teacher and student embeddings layers. Tiny-BERT (Jiao et al., 2019), MobileBERT (Sun et al., 2020b), and MiniLM (Wang et al., 2020b) matched the intermediate layers representations and selfattention distributions of the teacher and the student. In parallel, adaptive prompting for zero-shot transfer has been investigated in multilingual settings (Scao et al., 2022), and curriculum masking was proposed to stabilize pretraining dynamics (Xie et al., 2020), while pruning-based acceleration reduces inference latency without retraining (Gordon et al., 2020).

text: Recently, Jiang et al. (2021) study the problem of identifying commonsense implications of negations and contradictions. More specifically, they work with if-then rules such as If X does not learn new things, then X does not gain new knowledge and If X does not leave the building, then X stays in the building. These rules capture general commonsense knowledge about what happens if an event does not occur. Clark et al. (2020) introduce a suite of benchmarks for evaluating explanatory reasoning in natural language, and Tandon et al. (2018) curate declarative commonsense assertions for everyday scenarios, while Petroni et al. (2019) probe pretrained models for factual recall from parametric memory. Unlike them, we work with naturally occurring sentences that include negated predicate-argument structures with many arguments (agent, theme, manner, time, etc.). In addition, our affirmative interpretations reveal that predicates that are grammatically negated are actually factual (but with different arguments).

text: Recently, unsupervised abstractive summarization is attracting increasing attention. For example, Yang et al. (2020) propose to use the Lead baseline (first several sentences) as the pseudo-groundtruth. However, such an approach only works with wellstructured articles (such as CNN/DailyMail). Wang and Lee (2018) and Baziotis et al. (2019) use cycle consistency for unsupervised summarization. Zhou and Rush (2019) propose a step-by-step decomposable scoring function and perform beam search for generate summarization. Schumann et al. (2020) propose an edit-based local search approach, which allows a more comprehensive scoring function and outperforms cycle consistency and beam search.

text: Probing techniques for SRL. As in several other fields of NLP, recent studies tried to shed some light on how, where and to what extent PLMs encode information relevant to SRL. Among others, Tenney et al. (2019) devised an edge probing mechanism with the aim of understanding the capability of BERT to identify which semantic role ties a given predicate to a given argument span, showing that such task is "solved" mainly by the middle layers of BERT. Recent advances in SRL. Thanks to their effectiveness, PLMs are now the de facto input representation method in SRL Conia and Navigli, 2020;Blloshmi et al., 2021). Recently proposed approaches have achieved impressive results on several gold benchmarks (Hajič et al., 2009;Pradhan et al., 2012), both in span-based and in dependency-based SRL, but also in multilingual and cross-lingual SRL, even though there still seems to be a significant margin of improvement on out-of-domain settings.

text: Discourse structure. Our work is closely related to functional structures defined through content types explored in other domains; prior work has affirmed the usefulness of these structures in downstream NLP tasks. In news, Choubey et al. (2020) adopted Van Dijk (2013)'s content schema cataloging events (e.g., main event, anecdotal), which they showed to improve the performance of event coreference resolution. In scientific writing, content types (e.g., background, methodology) are shown to be useful for summarization (Teufel and Moens, 2002;Cohan et al., 2018), information extraction (Mizuta et al., 2006;Liakata et al., 2012), and information retrieval (Kircz, 1991;Liddy, 1991). The discourse structure of argumentative texts (e.g., support, rebuttal) (Peldszus and Stede, 2013;Becker et al., 2016;Stab and Gurevych, 2017) has also been applied on argumentation mining. To the best of our knowledge, no prior work has studied the discourse structure of long-form answers.

text: Question Answering. Recent work (Cao and Wang, 2021) have investigated the ontology of questions, which includes comparison questions, verification questions, judgement questions, etc. We construct the ontology of functional roles of answer sentences. One of the roles in our ontology is summary, yielding an extractive summarization dataset. This shares motivation with a line of work studying query-focused summarization (Xu and Lapata, 2020). Lastly, our work build up on two datasets containing long form answers (Kwiatkowski et al., 2019;Fan et al., 2019) and extends the analysis of long form answers from earlier studies (Krishna et al., 2021).

Lastly, our work build up on two datasets containing long form answers (Kwiatkowski et al., 2019;Fan et al., 2019) and extends the analysis of long form answers from earlier studies (Krishna et al., 2021).

text: Table-Based Fact Verification focuses on determining whether a natural language claim is supported by semi-structured evidence such as relational tables. Early neural approaches for table fact-checking framed the task as sentence-level entailment between a claim and linearized table content (Thorne et al., 2018; Nie et al., 2019). Deng et al. (2020) augmented retrieval with table-specific operations while TaPas modeled weakly supervised table question answering without explicit program induction (Herzig et al., 2020). Program induction methods generate executable logical forms that interact with table cells to derive evidence for support or refute labels (Chen et al., 2020; Neeraja et al., 2021). A line of work studies uncertainty calibration in natural language inference and confidence estimation for structured prediction (Desai and Durrett, 2020; Guo et al., 2017). Most of the current models utilize programs to improve the model's ability to handle various types of numerical and logical reasoning (Zhong et al., 2020; Shi et al., 2020; Yang and Zhu, 2021). These methods typically assemble a compact domain-specific language with operators for selection, aggregation, and comparison that can be executed deterministically. Supervision ranges from gold programs to distant signals such as denotations, and search is often guided by neuro-symbolic parsers. Recent work integrates pretrained encoders to improve schema linking and cell grounding in noisy tables. Despite accuracy gains, program brittleness and search inefficiency remain open challenges for complex claims. Hybrid models combine execution traces with neural rationales to improve faithfulness and controllability. Dataset design has also evolved to include adversarial perturbations and compositional generalization splits for rigorous evaluation. Generalization across domains and table schemas continues to be a key barrier for practical deployment. Mixture of Experts is a special model combining method that can mitigate capacity underfitting by distributing parameters among sparsely activated submodules. Jacobs et al. (1991) first introduces this method and proposes a loss that encourages competitive learning across expert models. The core idea is to allocate inputs to specialized components that are each trained to excel on a subset of the distribution (Fedus et al., 2022; Lepikhin et al., 2021). Researchers have explored routing strategies based on load-balancing objectives and token-level dispatch that reduce computational overhead while preserving accuracy on downstream tasks (Shazeer et al., 2017; Lewis et al., 2021). Prior work has also examined sparsity patterns and expert pruning for efficient serving in production environments (Zoph et al., 2022; Artetxe et al., 2021). Studies on counterfactual data augmentation and robust optimization aim to mitigate spurious correlations in text classification benchmarks (Kaushik et al., 2020; Sagawa et al., 2020). Additional research assesses the interpretability of gating decisions using attribution methods and mixture-component analysis (Vig et al., 2020; Ribeiro et al., 2016). In table fact verification, a modular architecture can assign arithmetic, comparison, and retrieval subtasks to distinct experts coordinated by a learned router. Gating signals derived from claim-table alignments enable dynamic computation conditioned on operator predictions. Such specialization reduces interference between symbolic execution and neural reasoning components. We develop a self-adapted mixture-of-experts framework that achieves a more effective combination of experts by learning from the experts' performance on the train set. The proposed approach couples token-level routing with program-conditioned experts and evaluates on multiple benchmarks. Experiments demonstrate significant gains in accuracy and calibration while maintaining competitive inference costs. Ablation studies confirm that routing quality is critical to robust generalization across table schemas. Finally, we release code and trained models to facilitate reproducibility and future work in modular fact verification.

text: Embedding-Based Topic Discovery. A number of studies extend LDA to involve word embedding. The common strategy is to adapt distributions in LDA to generate real-valued data (e.g., Gaussian LDA (Das et al., 2015), LFTM (Nguyen et al., 2015), Spherical HDP (Batmanghelich et al., 2016), andCGTM (Xun et al., 2017b)). Some other studies think out of the LDA backbone. For example, TWE (Liu et al., 2015) uses topic structures to jointly learn topic embeddings and improve word embeddings. CLM (Xun et al., 2017a) collaboratively improves topic modeling and word embedding by coordinating global and local contexts. ETM (Dieng et al., 2020) models word-topic correlations via word embeddings to improve the expressiveness of topic models. More recently, Sia et al. (2020) show that directly clustering word embeddings (e.g., word2vec or BERT) also generates good topics; Thompson and Mimno (2020) further find that BERT and GPT-2 discover high quality topics, but RoBERTa does not. These models are unsupervised and hard to be applied to seed-guided settings. In contrast, our SEETOPIC framework joint leverages PLMs, word embeddings, and seed information.

text: While research into automatic cloze test generation is vast (Mostow et al., 2017;Kurdi et al., 2020;Yang et al., 2021), work on open cloze tests for language learning is scarce. Pino et al. (2008) generate open cloze questions using sample sen- I first saw some guys doing motorbike stunts. I'd never seen anyone riding a motorbike using just the back wheel before and I was (3) impressed I went straight home and taught (4) to do the same. tences from a learners' dictionary based on four linguistic criteria: (grammatical) complexity, welldefined context (collocations), grammaticality and length. A later version of their system adds hints for gapped words (Pino and Eskenazi, 2009). Exercise Maker (Malafeev, 2014) is a rule-based open source system that attempts to emulate exercises in Cambridge English examinations based on the most frequently tested words. Most of the gaps it proposes were found to be useful and the automated exercises were hard to differentiate from authentic tests. Chinkina et al. (2017) generate open cloze exercises for phrasal verbs by extracting sentences from news articles and generating a pair of questions and answers where the identified particle verbs are gapped. Similarly, Soonklang et al. (2017) gap words in sentences according to their part of speech in order to practise articles, prepositions, etc. Finally, Marrese-Taylor et al. (2018) use LSTMs to build sequence labelling and classification models that decide where to insert a single gap in a single sentence. Automatic evaluation against goldstandard gaps showed the method was effective.
span_text: Pino et al. (2008) generate open cloze questions using sample sen- I first saw some guys doing motorbike stunts. I'd never seen anyone riding a motorbike using just the back wheel before and I was (3) impressed I went straight home and taught (4) to do the same. tences from a learners' dictionary based on four linguistic criteria: (grammatical) complexity, welldefined context (collocations), grammaticality and length. A later version of their system adds hints for gapped words (Pino and Eskenazi, 2009). Exercise Maker (Malafeev, 2014) is a rule-based open source system that attempts to emulate exercises in Cambridge English examinations based on the most frequently tested words.

text: Self-training bears similarity to the second phase of FlipDA where a teacher model is used to filter samples. Different from self-training, FlipDA leverages the advantages of label flipping to improve performance and does not rely on unlabeled data. Label Flipping. Our manual label flipping augmentation procedure is analogous to (Kaushik et al., 2020) and (Gardner et al., 2020). Kaushik et al. (2020) aimed to mitigate the effects of learning spurious features. Gardner et al. (2020) targeted reducing systematic gaps in the dataset. In contrast, we target improving few-shot generalization. Moreover, we measure the performance on an existing i.i.d. test set while Kaushik et al. (2020) and Gardner et al. (2020) created more challenging test sets. Most importantly, we propose an automatic method of label flipping, going beyond manual efforts. Contrastive Learning. FlipDA is connected to contrastive learning (CL) (He et al., 2020b; in that they both improve generalization by considering label differences. CL uses data augmentation to generate positive instances and uses samples existing in the dataset as negative samples, while FlipDA shows that negative samples can be automatically generated. While previous work on CL focuses on training with large datasets, our experiments show that augmenting a small dataset can improve few-shot generalization.

text:
Hallucination in neural language generation has recently received increasing attention from the NLP community, including machine translation (Raunak et al., 2021;Wang and Sennrich, 2020).
Across tasks such as summarization, data-to-text, and dialogue, models often produce fluent but unsupported content that undermines user trust and downstream utility (Maynez et al., 2020;Pagnoni et al., 2021).
Researchers have investigated both model-centric and data-centric strategies to improve factuality, with mixed success depending on domain and evaluation protocol (Ji et al., 2023).
A key challenge is that generation objectives typically reward surface fluency rather than verifiable grounding, which can encourage confident fabrication when evidence is sparse (Kryscinski et al., 2020).
Prior work on factuality emphasizes verification mechanisms and controllable decoding to mitigate unsupported statements. Lee et al. (2022) introduced constraint-aware beam search for grounded summarization. Kumar and Ghosh (2021) explored stochastic reranking with evidence alignment on news datasets. Zhang et al. (2020) proposed a faithfulness-aware training objective using entailment critics for abstractive summarization. Chen et al. (2019) studied retrieval-augmented generators for open-domain question answering. Nguyen and Li (2020) evaluated template-driven paraphrasing for style transfer. Peterson et al. (2021) examined calibration errors in classification models.
Evaluation remains contentious because human judgments are costly and automatic metrics correlate imperfectly with expert assessments (Fabbri et al., 2021;Durmus et al., 2020).
Entailment-based metrics such as FactCC and SummaC approximate factual consistency but can be brittle under domain shift and negation (Kryscinski et al., 2020;Laban et al., 2022).
Information extraction approaches measure entity and relation overlap with sources but struggle with coreference and implicit claims (Goodrich et al., 2019).
Recent studies combine multiple signals, including question answering probes, citation coverage, and uncertainty estimates, to triangulate factuality at scale (Wang et al., 2023).
For machine translation, hallucinations often manifest as off-by-topic content or unsupported additions when source segments are ambiguous or noisy (Raunak et al., 2021).
Coverage-aware training and constrained decoding can reduce such errors but may degrade adequacy if constraints are misaligned with source semantics (Wang and Sennrich, 2020).
In dialogue, models hallucinate plausible facts about entities and events due to sparse grounding and user-driven topic shifts (Shuster et al., 2021).
Augmenting decoders with retrieval over structured and unstructured corpora has improved factual grounding in conversational agents (Lewis et al., 2020).
Data-to-text systems encounter hallucination when schema slots are missing or when models overgeneralize from spurious correlations in training data (Reiter, 2018).
Schema expansion and constraint satisfaction methods can temper unsupported verbalizations, though they require careful alignment between input records and target text (Nan et al., 2021).
Instruction-tuned large language models have reduced some hallucinations by leveraging task-specific demonstrations, yet they remain vulnerable under distribution shift (Ouyang et al., 2022).
Uncertainty estimation and abstention mechanisms allow models to defer when evidence is insufficient, trading completeness for reliability (Kadavath et al., 2022).
Calibration techniques, including temperature scaling and post-hoc logistic recalibration, can temper overconfidence in generative distributions (Guo et al., 2017).
Nevertheless, calibrated probabilities do not directly enforce content faithfulness, motivating joint approaches that couple calibration with retrieval or verification (Kadavath et al., 2022).
Retrieval-augmented generation frameworks ground outputs in external sources by conditioning on fetched passages or tables (Lewis et al., 2020).
Learning to cite retrieved evidence during decoding further encourages explicit source attribution and facilitates post-hoc auditing (Shi et al., 2023).
On the training side, contrastive objectives that penalize unsupported continuations have shown promise for reducing factual drift in beam search (Cao and Wang, 2021).
Reinforcement learning with human or rule-based feedback can steer models toward verifiable claims, though reward specification remains challenging (Stiennon et al., 2020).
Dataset curation also plays a role: benchmarks that include counterfactual or adversarial perturbations reveal brittleness and guide robust training (Gardner et al., 2020).
Emerging work integrates knowledge graphs to provide entity-level constraints that restrict unsupported relations during generation (Logan et al., 2019).
Such structured priors complement unstructured retrieval by enforcing consistency across mentions and relations in multi-sentence outputs (Bosselut et al., 2019).
Despite progress, there is limited understanding of how interventions interact across domains and model scales, complicating method selection for practitioners (Pagnoni et al., 2021).
This paper contributes a unified evaluation protocol that disentangles grounding, calibration, and decoding effects using controlled perturbations across tasks.
We introduce a modular framework that pairs retrieval, verification, and uncertainty-aware decoding, enabling ablations that isolate each component’s contribution.
Comprehensive experiments on summarization, translation, and dialogue demonstrate consistent reductions in unsupported content without sacrificing fluency.
We release code, data, and analysis tools to facilitate reproducible research on mitigating hallucination in neural language generation.
