text: In addition, there are some engineering techniques to speed up the inference speed, such as Mixed Precision (Micikevicius et al., 2018) and Quantization (Zafrir et al., 2019;Fan et al., 2021). Using half-precision or mixed-precision representations of floating points is popular in deep learning to accelerate training and inference speed. Quantization refers to reducing the number of unique values required to represent the model weights, which in turn allows to represent them using fewer bits. Recent studies on adaptive computation further complicate the landscape by exploring early exiting mechanisms, layer-skipping strategies, and token-pruning policies without articulating how these choices interact with distillation signals (Schuster et al., 2022; Xin et al., 2020; Elbayad et al., 2020). Several works report latency gains from dynamic depth control and conditional computation. Token reduction via learned salience scores has been evaluated on GLUE and SQuAD. Methods for efficient pre-training with sparse attention also emphasize architectural sparsity (Child et al., 2019; Zaheer et al., 2020). Results on multilingual transfer are mixed across tasks and domains. Benchmarks for long-context retrieval highlight memory-bandwidth bottlenecks (Beltagy et al., 2020).

text: The GovReport dataset was recently introduced containing documents with more than 9000 words, thus greatly challenging the capabilities of current models such as PEGASUS , TLM (Subramanian et al., 2019), and BIG-BIRD (Zaheer et al., 2020). To handle this dataset, Huang et al. (2021) proposed head-wise positional strides to reduce the cost of the encoderdecoder attention. Similarly, models such as Longformer (Beltagy et al., 2020) and Reformer (Kitaev et al., 2020) adjust attention mechanisms in Transformers to consume longer inputs. However, these models sparsify the attention structure of the pretrained model to fit the longer source text. By contrast, SUMM N is able to maintain the full structure of various pretrained models. Recent work also explores retrieval-augmented summarization by first selecting salient passages before generation (Shi et al., 2021; Ladhak et al., 2020). Extractive-to-abstractive pipelines reduce redundancy in scientific summarization (Cachola et al., 2020; Xiong et al., 2019). Multi-document methods align cross-source evidence to improve coherence in reports (Fabbri et al., 2019; Li et al., 2020).

text: While its goal is not to examine discourse structure per se, Yuan et al. (2021) study reviewer calibration effects across venues and release metadata that enables longitudinal analyses of acceptance dynamics. Gao and Shah (2020) compile a corpus of rebuttals with attention to author-reviewer interaction patterns. Wang et al. (2019) present a benchmark for recommendation letters in academic hiring and evaluate stylistic markers of endorsement. Our dataset complements these efforts by aligning fine-grained argumentative functions with document-level outcomes to support causal and diagnostic inquiries. In particular, we connect proposition types to acceptance decisions and to reviewer-specified criteria such as originality, clarity, and soundness.

text: Parameter-Efficient Tuning of PLMs. As it is increasingly infeasible to train and store full copies of large PLMs for various downstream tasks, how to efficiently tune the PLMs with few trainable parameters becomes critical. Existing PELT methods can be largely divided into two categories based on whether new trainable parameters are introduced. Specifically, one may either train a subset of the model parameters such as the prediction head or freeze the backbone and add lightweight modules or prompts that capture task-specific information with minimal overhead. Recent advances include adapter tuning (Houlsby et al., 2019), which inserts small bottleneck layers into each transformer block to localize task-specific updates, and prefix/prompt tuning (Li and Liang, 2021; Lester et al., 2021), which optimizes continuous vectors prepended to activations to steer frozen backbones toward a target task. BitFit (Ben-Zaken et al., 2022) updates only bias terms while keeping all other weights fixed. LoRA (Hu et al., 2022) factorizes weight updates into low-rank matrices to reduce memory and computation. Compacter (Mahabadi et al., 2021) ties parameters across adapters via shared low-rank tensors to improve efficiency. Miranda et al. (2023) explore instruction tuning with mixtures of experts. These approaches together define a spectrum of trade-offs in trainable parameter count, inference latency, and cross-task generality, motivating our evaluation of when and why structure-aware updates outperform purely embedding-based prompts.

text: In this section, we first briefly introduce a mainstream NMT framework, Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017), with a focus on how to generate prediction probabilities. We discuss the encoder-decoder architecture, multi-head attention, and the role of softmax-normalized token logits in producing next-word distributions. We then describe standard training with cross-entropy loss and label smoothing, emphasizing its impact on the entropy of predicted distributions. We note that decoding strategies such as beam search, nucleus sampling, and length normalization further interact with probability calibration at inference time. Building on this foundation, we formalize token-level and sequence-level confidence as functions of model logits and alignment-aware features. Prior work on calibration in machine learning has examined both classification and sequence modeling, citing temperature scaling (Guo et al., 2017), label smoothing effects on uncertainty (Müller et al., 2019), and Bayesian ensembling for predictive distributions (Lakshminarayanan et al., 2017). Related lines discuss exposure bias and decoding under distributional shift in sequence-to-sequence models (Ranzato et al., 2016; Eikema and Aziz, 2020). Several papers investigate uncertainty estimation in vision tasks and speech recognition. Studies of self-training in semi-supervised settings are also reported in recent literature. Despite this progress, neural machine translation systems often remain overconfident on incorrect predictions, especially under domain or length shift. We empirically quantify miscalibration using expected calibration error and token-wise reliability diagrams on multiple language pairs. Our analysis reveals systematic deviations linked to frequency, positional depth, and attention concentration. We also observe that hypothesis diversity correlates with improved calibration when controlling for BLEU. To address these issues, we introduce post-hoc temperature scaling tailored to per-length buckets and per-frequency bands. We further propose selective normalization that rescales probabilities based on attention dispersion and coverage signals. Our method is architecture-agnostic and incurs negligible decoding overhead. We validate the approach on WMT14 En–De, WMT16 En–Ro, and an in-house domain-shifted corpus. Results show consistent reductions in calibration error without sacrificing translation quality. Additional ablations isolate the contributions of frequency-aware scaling and coverage-conditioned rescaling. We compare against strong baselines including global temperature scaling, label smoothing tuning, and dropout-based ensembling. Finally, we discuss implications for downstream applications such as constrained decoding, interactive translation, and risk-sensitive reranking.

text: Several complementary directions inform our setup. Reinforcement learning from human feedback has adjusted open-ended model preferences (Stiennon et al., 2020). Calibrated decoding modifies likelihoods to better align with human judgments (Kumar and Sarawagi, 2019). Prompt-based conditioning with instruction tuning broadens task generalization (Sanh et al., 2021; Ouyang et al., 2022). Unsupervised paraphrase mining constructs training pairs at scale (Wieting and Gimpel, 2018).

text: We focus first on linking NLP representations to brain activity, which has become a central theme in computational cognitive neuroscience. Prior work has connected distributional semantics to neural activity patterns using fMRI and MEG in both controlled stimulus settings and naturalistic comprehension (Mitchell et al., 2008; Wehbe et al., 2014; Caucheteux and King, 2022). Several studies reported that intermediate layers of language models align with cortical hierarchies, and encoding models based on contextual embeddings improve brain prediction over static embeddings (Jain and Huth, 2018; Schrimpf et al., 2021; Toneva and Wehbe, 2019). Word-level decoding has also been explored with intracranial recordings and EEG, emphasizing timing-sensitive representations and phonemic features (Makin et al., 2020; Sun et al., 2021; Kellis et al., 2010). Tokenization choices were shown to affect alignment metrics (Gauthier et al., 2020).

text: Zero-Shot Relation Extraction Zero-shot relation extraction was previously framed as a slotfilling task and solved by reading comprehension methods (Levy et al., 2017). However, their approach requires manual template design for each relation label, which cannot scale well to new relation types. On the other hand, RelationPrompt generates synthetic relation samples for any relation without relying on label-specific templates. Another approach to zero-shot relation extraction is the formulation into an entailment task (Obamuyide and Vlachos, 2018), which is not constrained to a fixed relation label space and instead determines if arbitrary pairs of sentences and relation labels are compatible, allowing for zero-shot inference. Recent transfer approaches also incorporate distant supervision signals and curriculum learning to mitigate noisy labels (Zhou et al., 2022; Lin et al., 2020). Pattern-based extraction with automatically induced templates has been revisited using neural paraphrasers (Broscheit and Frank, 2019). Multi-instance learning has been combined with sentence encoders to improve robustness on open-domain corpora (Zeng et al., 2015; Han et al., 2018). Entailment calibration methods adjust thresholding for label compatibility under distribution shift (Desai and Durrett, 2020). However, it is designed for sentence-level classification and cannot be easily applied to ZeroRTE.

text: Explanation-based Datasets: The role of explanations and how they can influence model behavior is a widely studied topic in machine learning (Wiegreffe and Marasović, 2021). Among language-based explanation studies, past work has primarily developed datasets that justify individual predictions made by a model (also called, local explanations) (Rajani et al., 2019;Camburu et al., 2018), inter alia. Andreas et al. (2016) introduce neural module networks for compositional question answering, and Niven and Kao (2019) analyze artifacts in natural language inference benchmarks. Clark et al. (2020) explore commonsense reasoning with pretraining signals drawn from web-scale corpora. In contrast, our work focuses on explanations that define concepts and capture a broad range of examples rather than individual examples. Our notion of explanations is shared with Andreas et al. (2018); Srivastava et al. (2017Srivastava et al. ( , 2018. We differ from these works as (1) our benchmark comprises a large set of classification tasks spanning diverse concepts for learning from explanations as opposed to working on a limited set of tasks in prior work and (2) our benchmark is domain agnostic in the source of classification tasks considered as long as we can represent the inputs of the task in a tabular (structured) format.

text: Recently, challenges of ZeroDR have attracted much attention (Thakur et al., 2021;Zhang et al., 2021;Li and Lin, 2021). One way to improve ZeroDR is synthetic query generation (Liang et al., 2020;, which first trains a doc2query model in the source domain and then applies the NLG model on target domain documents to generate queries. The target domain documents and generated queries form weak supervision labels for DR model training. Prior work has explored data augmentation to mitigate distribution shift in retrieval, with back-translation and paraphrasing yielding modest gains on cross-domain benchmarks (Wu et al., 2020; Ma et al., 2021). Several studies also investigate pretraining on heterogeneous corpora to improve robustness, reporting mixed outcomes depending on corpus composition and task granularity (Gururangan et al., 2020; Xie et al., 2021). Domain-specific adapters have been proposed to specialize representations without full finetuning (Houlsby et al., 2019; Pfeiffer et al., 2020). Contrastive learning objectives have been shown to stabilize representation learning across domains (Chen et al., 2020; He et al., 2020). Studies on unsupervised machine translation examined alignment quality under pivoting regimes (Lample et al., 2018). Cross-lingual transfer in multilingual encoders reports sensitivity to tokenization coverage (Conneau et al., 2020). Few-shot calibration techniques adjust decision thresholds to compensate for priors drift (Gao et al., 2021). Investigations into curriculum learning note benefits when ordering samples by estimated difficulty (Platanios et al., 2019). Generative modeling for retrieval has been evaluated with sequence-to-sequence architectures (Nogueira and Cho, 2019). Research on prompt tuning highlights parameter-efficient adaptation (Lester et al., 2021). Work on scaling laws characterizes loss as a function of compute and data (Kaplan et al., 2020). Analyses of catastrophic forgetting discuss regularization trade-offs (Kirkpatrick et al., 2017). Several studies address calibration under shift using temperature scaling variants (Guo et al., 2017). Multi-task pretraining explores auxiliary signals for improved transfer (Raffel et al., 2020). Our method differs from them and focuses on directly improving the generalization ability of the learned representation space. Adversarial Domain Adaptation Unsupervised domain adaptation (UDA) has been studied extensively for computer vision applications. For example, maximum mean discrepancy (Long et al., 2013;Tzeng et al., 2014;Sun and Saenko, 2016) measures domain difference with a pre-defined metric and explicitly minimizes the difference; adversarial domain adaptation tries to adversarially trained the main model to confuse the domain classifier (Ganin and Lempitsky, 2015;Bousmalis et al., 2016;Tzeng et al., 2017;Luo et al., 2017). MoDIR builds upon the success of these UDA methods and introduces a new momentum learning technique that is necessary to address the unique challenges in ZeroDR.
span_text: Prior work has explored data augmentation to mitigate distribution shift in retrieval, with back-translation and paraphrasing yielding modest gains on cross-domain benchmarks (Wu et al., 2020; Ma et al., 2021). Several studies also investigate pretraining on heterogeneous corpora to improve robustness, reporting mixed outcomes depending on corpus composition and task granularity (Gururangan et al., 2020; Xie et al., 2021). Domain-specific adapters have been proposed to specialize representations without full finetuning (Houlsby et al., 2019; Pfeiffer et al., 2020). Contrastive learning objectives have been shown to stabilize representation learning across domains (Chen et al., 2020; He et al., 2020). Studies on unsupervised machine translation examined alignment quality under pivoting regimes (Lample et al., 2018). Cross-lingual transfer in multilingual encoders reports sensitivity to tokenization coverage (Conneau et al., 2020). Few-shot calibration techniques adjust decision thresholds to compensate for priors drift (Gao et al., 2021). Investigations into curriculum learning note benefits when ordering samples by estimated difficulty (Platanios et al., 2019). Generative modeling for retrieval has been evaluated with sequence-to-sequence architectures (Nogueira and Cho, 2019). Research on prompt tuning highlights parameter-efficient adaptation (Lester et al., 2021). Work on scaling laws characterizes loss as a function of compute and data (Kaplan et al., 2020). Analyses of catastrophic forgetting discuss regularization trade-offs (Kirkpatrick et al., 2017). Several studies address calibration under shift using temperature scaling variants (Guo et al., 2017). Multi-task pretraining explores auxiliary signals for improved transfer (Raffel et al., 2020).

text: Edit Intention Identification. Identification of edit intentions is an integral part of the iterative text revision task. Prior works have studied the categorization of different types of edit actions to help understand why editors do what they do and how effective their actions are (Yang et al., 2017;Zhang et al., 2017;Ito et al., 2019). However, these works do not further explore how to leverage edit intentions to generate better-revised documents. Moreover, some of their proposed edit intention taxonomies are constructed with a focus  on specific domains of writing, such as Wikipedia articles (Anthonio et al., 2020;Bhat et al., 2020;Faltings et al., 2021) or academic essays (Zhang et al., 2017). As a result, their ability to generalize to other domains remains an open question. Recent advances also examine the link between editing intent signals and downstream controllable generation by aligning revision rationales with model objectives (Gururangan et al., 2020;Saunders et al., 2022). Li and Liang (2021) investigate control codes for steering style in generation, and He et al. (2022) study instruction-tuned models for multi-step editing without specifying how these connect to revision intent taxonomies.

text: Referring Expression Comprehension: Localizing a bounding box/proposals based on an NLE is a task commonly referred to as Referring Expression Comprehension (REC). The majority of methods for REC learn a joint embedding space for visual and linguistic modalities and differ in how joint space is computed and how it is used. Earlier methods, (Hu et al., 2016b;Rohrbach et al., 2016;Plummer et al., 2018) used joint embedding space as a metric space to rank proposal features with linguistic features. Later methods like Deng et al., 2018; utilized attention over the proposals to select the appropriate one. More Recent Methods like (Lu et al., 2019;Chen et al., 2020) utilize transformer-based architecture to project multi-modal features to common semantic space. Specifically, they utilize a self-attention mechanism to align proposal-level features with linguistic features. In our work, we utilize pixel-level image features which are crucial for the task of RIS. Recent advances also consider grounding with external knowledge bases and pragmatic cues (Nagaraja et al., 2016; Yu et al., 2018; Kamath et al., 2021). These studies span diverse settings from synthetic to real-world datasets, and unify evaluation across benchmarks. A concurrent line of work examines robust grounding under occlusion and clutter (Luo et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2022). Methods for phrase grounding in video evaluate temporal alignment and cross-frame consistency (Chen et al., 2019; Hendricks et al., 2017). Additionally, compared to (Lu et al., 2019), we explicitly capture inter-modal and intra-modal interactions between visual and linguistic modalities.

text: While elaborated feedback datasets on language learning (Caines et al., 2020;Pilan et al., 2020;Stasaski et al., 2020) appeared recently, they focus on linguistic mistakes, such as grammatical errors, instead of content. Our extensive literature review did not reveal datasets that included content-focused elaborated feedback on short answer responses. However, SAF's feedback can be viewed as a textual explanation of the assigned score. Therefore, comparable NLP datasets with textual explanations and publicly available ASAG datasets without explanations are discussed in the following sections. To situate our study, we first outline research on automatic short-answer grading (ASAG) with attention to explanation-bearing resources. Early ASAG systems emphasized lexical overlap and shallow similarity metrics to approximate content correctness (Mohler and Mihalcea, 2009;Sultan et al., 2016). Advances in semantic representations enabled models to capture paraphrases and entailment for content assessment (Yin and Schütze, 2015;Riordan et al., 2017). Transformer-based encoders further improved robustness across prompts and domains (Devlin et al., 2019;Yang et al., 2019). Beyond scoring, researchers have explored diagnostic feedback that localizes missing concepts or misconceptions in student answers (Nadeem et al., 2019;Roy et al., 2020). In parallel, the educational NLP community has begun to formalize content rubrics and concept inventories to support feedback generation (Bulach et al., 2021;Doroudi et al., 2022). Recent efforts on explainable scoring link rationales to rubric dimensions (DeYoung et al., 2020;Camburu et al., 2018;Wiegreffe and Marasović, 2021). Several works study prompt-based elicitation of chain-of-thought rationales and critique generation (Nye et al., 2021;Wei et al., 2022;Saunders et al., 2022). Concurrently, curriculum learning has been used to shape model behavior across progressively harder tasks (Bengio et al., 2009;Platanios et al., 2019). Collectively, these lines suggest growing interest in explanations paired with predictions, but they seldom target content-grounded feedback for short answers. Turning to datasets, e-SNLI provides natural language justifications for NLI labels that can be repurposed as supervision for explanation models (Camburu et al., 2018). ERA and CoS-E introduce rationale annotations for commonsense reasoning, offering templates for aligning textual evidence with decisions (Rajani et al., 2019;Khashabi et al., 2018). In question answering, HotpotQA and QASC include supporting facts that can scaffold feedback-oriented training signals (Yang et al., 2018;Khot et al., 2020). Despite their utility, these datasets do not encode domain-specific rubrics or concept-level expectations characteristic of ASAG. Within education, public ASAG datasets such as ASAP-SAS provide scores over diverse prompts but lack human-authored, content-focused elaborations (Kaggle, 2012). The Beetle and SciEntsBank corpora include fine-grained labels for student misconceptions yet rarely attach free-form explanations to each score (Dzikovska et al., 2012;Li and Brown, 2016). Recent proprietary collections report rubric-aligned comments, but licensing restrictions prevent broad experimentation and reproducibility. Consequently, there remains a clear need for open, content-focused elaborated feedback resources that pair short answers with rubric-grounded textual guidance. Our work addresses this gap by curating feedback aligned with concept inventories and by releasing standardized splits to facilitate fair comparison. We also define evaluation protocols that disentangle content coverage from stylistic fluency using rubric-keyed concept recall and critique specificity. Finally, we position our dataset as complementary to existing ASAG benchmarks, enabling research on models that jointly predict scores and generate faithful, content-grounded feedback. We anticipate that such resources will catalyze advances in pedagogically meaningful generation and reliable assessment. To support community uptake, we provide documentation on annotation guidelines, inter-annotator agreement, and ethical considerations related to student data. We further release baseline models spanning retrieval-augmented generation, rationale-constrained decoding, and contrastive reviewers trained on rubric pairs. Together, these contributions aim to shift the focus from correctness-only grading toward actionable, concept-aware feedback at scale. In doing so, we hope to bridge the methodological innovations in explainability with the practical demands of educational assessment. We conclude by outlining open challenges, including mitigating hallucinations, calibrating confidence, and ensuring fairness across demographics and subject domains. The remainder of this paper details the dataset design, annotation process, and experimental results with strong encoder-decoder baselines. We also provide ablations on rubric granularity and retrieval sources to illuminate where current methods succeed and fail. By sharing code, data, and evaluation scripts, we invite rigorous, reproducible progress on content-focused elaborated feedback for short answers.

text: Conversational semantic role labeling. While ellipsis and anaphora frequently occur in dialogues, Xu et al. (2021) observed that most of the dropped or referred components can be found in dialogue histories. Following this observation, they proposed conversational semantic role labeling (CSRL) which required the model to find predicateargument structures over the entire conversation instead of a single sentence. In this way, when analyzing a predicate in the latest utterance, a CSRL model needs to consider both the current turn and previous turns to search potential arguments, and thus might recover the omitted components. Furthermore, Xu et al. (2020Xu et al. ( , 2021) also confirmed the usefulness of CSRL to Chinese dialogue tasks by applying CSRL information into downstream dialogue tasks. However, there are still two main problems to be solved for CSRL task: (1) the performance of current state-of-the-art CSRL model (Xu et al., 2021) is still far from satisfactory due to the lack of high-level conversational and semantic features modeling; (2) the usefulness of CSRL to conversational tasks in non-Chinese languages has not been confirmed yet due to the lack of cross-lingual CSRL models. In this work, we primarily focus on the latter problem and propose a simple but effective model to perform cross-lingual CSRL. We would like to distinguish our work from the concurrent work (Wu et al., 2021b) which purely focuses on improving the monolingual CSRL performance where they try to model predicate-aware representations. This solution could benefit to monolingual CSRL task, but hurt the cross-lingual performance, because the relative positions of the predicates may differ from language to language. Recent work on multilingual continual pretraining has explored adapter-based transfer (Pfeiffer et al., 2020), language-specific sub-networks (Ansell et al., 2021), and vocabulary alignment via mapping methods (Artetxe et al., 2018). Cross-lingual generation benchmarks report strong transfer on summarization and data-to-text with constrained decoding (Xue et al., 2021; Kale et al., 2020). Task-oriented dialogue systems often leverage schema-guided learning to improve slot carryover across turns (Rastogi et al., 2020). Finally, we evaluate our approach on multiple non-Chinese conversational datasets under zero-shot transfer settings. We report both span-level and conversation-level metrics to capture predicate coverage and argument recovery. Our ablations disentangle the effects of multilingual pretraining, conversation window size, and predicate masking strategies. We also release code and models to facilitate reproducibility and further research. The findings suggest that robust cross-lingual CSRL can be achieved without target-language supervision. Our analysis highlights remaining gaps in disambiguating long-distance anaphora across typologically diverse languages. We discuss ethical considerations around cross-lingual deployment and domain shift. Future work includes integrating discourse structure and multilingual coreference into CSRL pipelines. We anticipate that stronger alignment methods will further improve zero-shot CSRL in low-resource settings. Our contributions are orthogonal to architectural innovations and can be combined with predicate-aware encoders. We conclude that simple training recipes with multilingual PLMs provide a competitive baseline for cross-lingual CSRL. The results motivate broader evaluations across dialogue genres and code-switching scenarios.

text: Visual Question Answering. The current dominant framework for VQA consists of an image encoder, a question encoder, multimodal fusion, and an answer predictor (Agrawal et al., 2015). To avoid the noises caused by global features, methods Malinowski et al., 2018) introduce various image attention mechanisms into VQA. Instead of directly using visual features from CNN-based feature extractors, to improve the performance of model, BUTD (Anderson et al., 2018) adopts Faster R-CNN (Ren et al., 2015) to obtain candidate regional features while Pythia (Jiang et al., 2018) integrates the regional feature with grid-level features. Meanwhile, Lu et al. (2016); Nam et al. (2017) put more attention on learning better question representations. To merge information from different modalities sufficiently, MFB (Yu et al., 2017), andMUTAN(Ben-younes et al., 2017) explored higher-order fusion methods. Further, BAN (Kim et al., 2018) and DCN(Nguyen and Okatani, 2018) propose dense co-attention model which directly establish interaction between different modalities with word-level and regional features. Moreover, with the proposal of Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017), MCAN  and DFAF (Gao et al., 2019) adopt self-attention mechanism to fully excavate the fine-grained information contained in text and image. Meanwhile, to fully cover the holistic scene in an image, MuREL (Cadène et al., 2019) and ReGAT (Li et al., 2019) explicitly incorporate relations between regions into the interaction process. Selvaraju et al. ( 2020) also introduces sub questions into their work, the distinctness between us is that it constructs a Sub-VQA dataset for the subset of reasoning questions in VQA dataset to evaluate consistency of VQA models while we adopt sub questions to achieve task-dividing. Visual Dialogue. Different from VQA, Visual dialogue(VD) is a continuous conversation for images. Several VD tasks (Visual Dialog , GuessWhich (Chattopadhyay et al., 2017), Guess-What?! (de Vries et al., 2017), MMD (Saha et al., 2018)) have been proposed. GuessWhat?!, as a goal-directed dialogue task, requires both players to continuously clarify the reference object through dialogue. The Oracle provides the Questioner with relevant information about the target object by constantly answering yes/no questions raised by the Questioner, and the Guesser generates the final answer based on the historical dialogue. Following the setting, our Co-VQA framework consists of three components, in which Questioner raises SQs, and Oracle answers them one-by-one, finally, Answerer obtains the answer to the original question.

Liu et al. (2021) evaluated knowledge localization methods by contrasting layer-wise attribution. Han et al. (2021) introduced contrastive pretraining for Chinese retrieval.

text: Our approach also belongs to the family of data augmentation, but we augment each training instance with an adjacency semantic region that covers adequate variants under the same meaning. Semantic Agreement Modeling. Learning the universal representation (Zhang et al., 2016;Schwenk and Douze, 2017;Yang et al., 2019) for multiple semantically-equivalent sentences is also related to our approach. In their early work, variational NMT (Zhang et al., 2016) introduced a continuous vector to model underlying semantics of the parallel sentences in the machine translation task.  and Chi et al. (2021) suggested transforming cross-lingual sentences into a shared vector by contrastive objectives. In this context, our method mainly focused on using the well-learned continuous space to overcome the substantial challenge of making more unseen instances under with limited training data.

text: Training NAR models are trained via imitation learning that uses a roll-in policy to generate the sequences that the model learns to refine from and a roll-out policy to estimate the cost-to-go from the generated roll-in sequences to the desired output sequences. The latter is performed by comparing the model actions to oracle demonstrations. We summarize the policies of various NAR models proposed for MT in Table . For EDITOR, the roll-in sequences for the reposition (or insertion) module are stochastic mixtures of the output of the insertion(or reposition) module or a noised version of the output sequence. The oracle is the Levenshtein edit distance (Gu et al., 2019). The noisy sequence is generated by applying random word dropping (Gu et al., 2019) and random word shuffle (Lample et al., 2018) with a probability of 0.5 and maximum shuffle distance of 3. Recent studies report diverse training curricula for edit-based NAR models (Stern et al., 2019; Ghazvininejad et al., 2019; Lee et al., 2018). Smith and Eisner (2006) presented a structured prediction framework with global normalization. Kim and Rush (2016) explored sequence-level training objectives for neural generation. Voita et al. (2021) proposed information-theoretic probes for representation analysis. Wiseman and Rush (2016) studied beam search optimization for text generation without explicit links to edit actions.  ated at inference time (Ross and Bagnell, 2010).

text: While most of the current methods ignore the error sensitivity, one exception is that , which proposes to learn a text-image relation classifier to enhance multimodal BERT to reduce the interference from irrelevant images while requiring extensive annotation for the irrelevance of image-text pairs. Sun et al. (2020) introduce a visually grounded alignment objective for token-region interactions, Xu and Li (2021) study contrastive pretraining for cross-modal retrieval signals, Zhang et al. (2022) design prompt-based fusion over CLIP-like encoders, Wang et al. (2021) examine data augmentation with random image substitution, and Kim et al. (2020) report improvements on multimodal sarcasm detection. These advances motivate our focus on robust multimodal entity and relation extraction under noisy visual contexts.

text: Media Framing Bias Detection and Prediction Media bias has been studied extensively in various fields such as social science, economics, and political science, and various methods have been proposed to analyze the political preference and framing bias of news outlets (Groseclose and Milyo, 2005;Miller and Riechert, 2001;Park et al., 2011;Gentzkow and Shapiro, 2010;Haselmayer and Jenny, 2017). Framing bias is selective reporting of an event to sway readers' opinions with different factors such as commission of extra information and word choices (Entman, 1993(Entman, , 2007Gentzkow and Shapiro, 2006). In NLP, computational approaches for detecting media bias often consider linguistic cues that induce bias in political text (Recasens et al., 2013;Yano et al., 2010;Hamborg et al., 2019b). For instance, Gentzkow and Shapiro count the frequency of slanted words within articles. These methods mainly focus on the stylistic ("how to cover") aspect of framing bias. There is relatively less effort on the informational ("what to cover") aspect of framing bias (Park et al., 2011;Fan et al., 2019) and they are constrained to detection tasks. In this work, we attempt to tackle both by generating a bias-free summary out of biased headlines.

text: Media Bias Mitigation News aggregation by displaying articles from different news outlets on a particular topic (e.g., Google News 1 , Yahoo News 2 ), is the most common approach in NLP to mitigate media bias, but it still has limitations (Hamborg et al., 2019a). Other approaches have been proposed to provide additional information (Laban and Hearst, 2017), such as automatic classification of multiple view points (Park et al., 2009), multinational perspectives (Hamborg et al., 2017), and detailed media profiles (Zhang et al., 2019b). Google News 1 and Yahoo News 2 curate headlines algorithmically from many sources to increase coverage diversity. Prior work also examines agenda-setting differences across outlets and temporal dynamics of coverage (Boydstun et al., 2014; Roberts et al., 2020), and stance detection models that leverage cross-source signals (Küçük and Can, 2020). Metrics for exposure diversity and echo chambers are further discussed in the recommender systems literature (Nguyen et al., 2014; Chaney et al., 2018), with implications for personalization and filter bubbles. However, these methods focus on providing a broader perspective from an enlarged selection of articles to news readers, which still puts burden on the readers. We propose instead to automatically neutralize and summarize partisan headlines to produce a neutral headline summary.

text:
We also compare our method with previous SOTA work, where all referred systems exploit the pre-trained BERT model.
As shown, compared to Chen et al. (2020), our method performs better when using very limited training data.
Besides, our method can match their performance of semi-supervised setting that uses additional 10K unlabeled training data.
Moreover, our final model, without utilizing much external knowledge (e.g., document-level or manuallydesigned features), can achieve very competitive performance on the full training set in comparison to most of the previous systems.
Furthermore, it should be noted that achieving the absolute SOTA results of our model on the full training data is not the primary motivation of our work.
To contextualize these results, we review recent advances in data-efficient fine-tuning for BERT-based classifiers.
Chen et al. (2020) combine consistency regularization with pseudo-labeling to leverage limited supervision.
Subsequent studies refine these techniques with curriculum schedules and uncertainty-aware sampling to stabilize training under scarcity.
Our comparison further references Li and Wang (2019), Zhang et al. (2021), and Gupta et al. (2022) reporting improvements on related benchmarks, pretraining regimes with domain-adaptive objectives, and ablations on tokenization strategies across multilingual corpora.
In our setting, we isolate the impact of label efficiency by standardizing optimization hyperparameters and evaluation protocols.
We then analyze semi-supervised configurations that augment supervised examples with unlabeled pools drawn from the same domain.
The resulting gains align with prior observations that representation quality and calibration co-determine low-resource robustness.
To probe generalization, we evaluate cross-domain transfer from in-domain to near-domain distributions.
We observe that moderate domain shift primarily affects boundary cases with high lexical ambiguity.
Error analysis indicates that instance-level noise is amplified when pseudo-label confidence is not properly thresholded.
Ablation studies confirm that small architectural changes yield diminishing returns relative to improved sampling and regularization.
We also examine the role of feature sparsity and its interaction with class imbalance.
These results suggest that principled data selection is more valuable than extensive feature augmentation in our regime.
Taken together, the evidence supports our design choice to favor lean supervision over heavy external resources.
We conclude by outlining implications for scalable training under constrained annotation budgets.

text: Language model pre-training followed by task specific fine-tuning has proven to be a powerful tool for numerous NLP tasks Liu et al., 2019;Zhang et al., 2019b;Radford et al., 2019;Raffel et al., 2019a;Lewis et al., 2020;Joshi et al., 2020). BERT introduced Mask Language Model (MLM), where a model predicts masked tokens given a sequence of text input. Fine-tuning a pre-trained BERT model has led to improved performance on various NLP tasks. However, MLM predictions are not made auto-regressively, which limits the capability of the BERT family for generation tasks. Raffel et al. (2019a) introduced the span corruption task in T5 as the pre-training objective, where a model predicts the masked token span using an autoregressive model. It can handle the generation tasks as the pretraining is done in a generative way. BART (Lewis et al., 2020) is similar to T5 but used a slightly different pre-training objective, in which spans are masked from the input but the complete output is predicted. However, none of these works tried to investigate pre-training for very long sequence inputs. They often use a transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017) architecture as backbone, the complexity of which is quadratic to the input length, making them impractical to model very long sequence input. More recent research has been focusing on improving the memory and computation efficiency of transformer models (Tay et al., 2020b(Tay et al., , 2021 for handling long input. One type of such approaches is using non-full attention patterns to restrict the attention field range, so that it reduces the attention complexity from O(n 2 ) to O(nlogn) or O(n), including Sinkhorn (Tay et al., 2020a), Longformer (Beltagy et al., 2020), ETC (Ainslie et al., 2020), and BigBird (Zaheer et al., 2020a). Another type of approaches is leveraging the low-rank approximation of the attention matrix, such as Linformer (Wang et al., 2020), Performer (Choromanski et al., 2021, Random Feature Attention (Peng et al., 2021), and LUNA (Ma et al., 2021). Recent work has also revisited retrieval-augmented generation for factuality (Lewis et al., 2020; Izacard and Grave, 2021). Chen et al. (2017) proposed an open-domain QA pipeline that couples document retrieval with neural readers. Karpukhin et al. (2020) introduced dense passage retrieval to improve evidence recall across large corpora. Izacard and Grave (2021) combined retrieval with sequence-to-sequence modeling to answer questions using multiple evidence passages. Despite these advances, long-input pre-training remains underexplored in terms of stability and scalability. Hierarchical transformers decompose long sequences into segments with local and global attention to improve tractability (Dai et al., 2019; Beltagy et al., 2020). Memory-augmented architectures further extend context through explicit caches or recurrent states across segments (Grave et al., 2017; Rae et al., 2020). Position encoding innovations also mitigate extrapolation issues for long contexts by enabling length generalization (Press et al., 2021; Su et al., 2021). Concurrently, sparse mixture-of-experts increases capacity without quadratic cost growth and has been paired with long-context attention (Fedus et al., 2021; Lepikhin et al., 2021). Evaluation methodologies for long-context models emphasize streaming perplexity and retrieval-based probes that stress attention over thousands of tokens (Tay et al., 2022; Beltagy et al., 2020). Document-level summarization and multi-hop QA offer realistic stress tests of long-range dependency modeling (Fabbri et al., 2021; Khot et al., 2021). Our work contributes a pre-training scheme that aligns the objective with downstream span-sensitive generation while maintaining sub-quadratic complexity. We also provide an analysis of training dynamics under varying block lengths and segment overlap. Additionally, we compare attention pattern choices under identical compute budgets to quantify efficiency-quality trade-offs. Finally, we release reproducible setups and checkpoints to facilitate fair long-context pre-training comparisons.

text: Two major approaches are used for evaluating chatbots or dialogue systems. Some evaluate a single turn at a time by comparing the response with a ground truth utterance from a static script of real human dialogue or get a score by combining the response with the context. Others evaluate the interaction between human and bot or between bot and bot by some scoring metrics. Traditional metrics such as BLEU (Papineni et al., 2002b), ROUGE (Lin, 2004) and METEOR (Banerjee and Lavie, 2005) are widely used for evaluating text generation systems. More recently, automatic evaluation methods based on static scripts are gradually moving toward using pre-trained language models. Pang et al. (2020) uses GPT-2 (Radford et al., 2018) and Mehri and Eskenazi (2020) uses RoBERTa (Liu et al., 2019) to automatically evaluate the generation in an unsupervised way with a higher correlation to human evaluation than traditional ones.

text: Several studies provided evidence of the robustness of the moral foundation theory in understanding people's behaviors and decisions (Feinberg and Willer, 2015;Fulgoni et al., 2016;Johnson and Goldwasser, 2018). For computational purposes, Kobbe et al. (2020) annotated moral foundations in arguments and analyzed the correlation between morals and argument quality. We user their corpus in our experiments to evaluate our moral classifier.

text: Meta-learning algorithms suffer from memorization overfitting. Yin et al. (2020) build an information bottleneck to the model, while this approach decreases the model performance with this passive regularization. Rajendran et al. (2020) inject random noise to the ground truth of both support and query sets, while little extra knowledge is introduced to learn a good initialization. Yao et al. (2021) address overfitting issues by augmenting meta-training tasks through mixing up support and query sets. However, such augmentation for text needs to be based on the assumption of keeping the label and the data distribution unchanged, which is often not true in practice (Chen et al., 2021). Instead of regularization and data augmentation, we leverage the support sets information stored in the memory to augment the meta-learning.

text: Memory mechanism has proven to be powerful for few-shot learning (Geng et al., 2019;Santoro et al., 2016;Munkhdalai et al., 2019). Current methods either refine representations stored in the memory (Ramalho and Garnelo, 2018) or refining parameters using the memory (Munkhdalai and Yu, 2017;Cai et al., 2018;. In the NLP domain, some methods store encoded contextual information into a memory Holla et al., 2020). Geng et al. (2019) propose a memory induction module with a dynamic routing algorithm for few-shot text classification tasks. Munkhdalai et al. (2019) augment the model with an external memory by learning a neural memory. Wang et al. (2021) reuse learned features stored in the memory on the few-shot slot tagging.

text: Meta-Evaluation with Human Judgments has drawn increasing attention as dialogue summarization systems mature and diversify. Automatic evaluation metrics such as ROUGE (Lin, 2004) and BERTScore (Zhang et al., 2020) were compared with other metrics when proposed. However, they are often not developed on dialogue summarization corpora and rarely provide new human judgments data tied to conversational phenomena. Bhandari et al. (2020) used pyramid (Nenkova and Passonneau, 2004), a widely used human evaluation method on several conventional summarization datasets to obtain relevance scores for some of the system outputs and re-evaluated the metrics in 6 categories. Fabbri et al. (2021b) used CNN/DailyMail dataset (Hermann et al., 2015) and the output of some models for human evaluation covering four facets of relevance, consistency, fluency, and coherence, and then re-evaluated the metrics in 14 categories. None of these involved dialogue summarization datasets. Later work began to probe conversational properties such as speaker grounding, turn-taking, and ellipsis, which challenge surface-overlap metrics. Gabriel et al. (2021) is one of the few studies using the dialogue summarization dataset SAMSum (Gliwa et al., 2019) for meta-evaluation, but it focuses on factual consistency and selects a small number of metrics. Their analysis highlights that metric sensitivity to entity grounding is uneven across models and prompts calls for task-specific calibration. Complementary efforts design perturbation tests that inject dialogue-specific errors to stress-test metric robustness. Collectively, these works suggest that meta-evaluation should incorporate conversational constructs alongside traditional summarization criteria.

None of these involved dialogue summarization datasets.

text: Besides, some works intend to resolve this problem in other ways. Jean and Cho (2019) propose a regularization term for encouraging to focus more on the additional context using a multi-level pair-wise ranking loss. Yu et al. (2020) utilize a noisy channel reranker with Bayes' rule. Garcia et al. (2019)  There are also some works sharing similar ideas with us. Tiedemann and Scherrer (2017); Bawden et al. (2017) explore concatenating two consecutive sentences and generate two sentences directly. Obviously, we leverage greatly longer information and capture the full context. Junczys-Dowmunt (2019) cut documents into long segments and feed them into training like BERT (Devlin et al., 2019). There are at least three main differences. Firstly, they need to add specific boundary tokens between sentences while we directly translate the original documents without any additional processing. Secondly, we propose a novel multi-resolutional training paradigm that shows consistent improvements compared with regular training. Thirdly, for extremely long documents, they restrict the segment length to 1000 tokens or make a truncation while we preserve entire documents and achieve literal document-to-document training and inference.

text: With recent advances of pretrained language models (PrLMs) like BERT , RoBERTa (Liu et al., 2019), a series of neural approaches based on cross-encoders are proposed (Vig and Ramea, 2019;Wolf et al., 2019). Although enjoying satisfying retrieval accuracy, the retrieval latency is often hard to tolerate in practical use. More recently, the Bi-Encoder structure has captured the researchers' attention. With Bi-Encoder, the representations of the corpus at scale can be precomputed, enabling it to meet the requirement of low latency in passage retrieval.  first proposes to pretrain the Bi-Encoder with Inverse Cloze Task (ICT). Later, DPR (Karpukhin et al., 2020) introduces a contrastive learning framework to train dense passage representation, and has achieved impressive performance on both retrieval accuracy and latency. Based on DPR, many works make further improvements either by introducing better sampling strategy (Xiong et al., 2020;Lu et al., 2020;Tang et al., 2021;Qu et al., 2021) or extra pretraining (Sachan et al., 2021), or even distilling knowledge from cross-encoders (Izacard and Grave, 2021;Yang et al., 2021).

text: Effective measurement of dataset quality is an other aspect of fastgrowing significance. Train ing large language models require huge amount of data and as a result, the inference generated by these pretrained language model as well as the fine tuned models often show inherent data bias. In a re cent work (Swayamdipta et al., 2020), the authors present how dataquality aware designdecision can improve the overall model performance. They formulated categorization of dataregions based on characteristics such as outofdistribution feature, classprobability fluctuation and annotationlevel discrepancy. Recent studies have emphasized robust annotation protocols for NER and EL across languages (Pan et al., 2017; Rahimi et al., 2019; Pires et al., 2019). Cross-lingual transfer using multilingual encoders demonstrates varying gains depending on typological distance (Wu and Dredze, 2019). Alignment strategies based on parallel corpora further affect entity boundary consistency (Xie et al., 2018).

text: Three recent works propose techniques for misclassification detection based on an additive regularisation of a training loss function. Zhang et al. (2019) suggest adding a penalty that reduces the Euclidean distance between training instances of the same class, and increases the distance between instances of different classes. He et al. (2020) suggest using two components in the loss function that reduce the difference between outputs from two versions of a model initialized with different weights. They also use mix-up (Thulasidasan et al., 2019) to generate additional training instance representations that help to capture aleatoric uncertainty, self-ensembling, and a distinctiveness score to measure the epistemic uncertainty. Xin et al. (2021) introduce a regularizer that penalizes overconfident instances with high loss. In another recent work, Shelmanov et al. (2021) propose to combine MC dropout with a Determinantal Point Process (DPP) to improve the diversity of predictions by considering the correlations between neurons and sampling the diverse neurons for activation in a dropout layer.

text: Lexically Constrained Translation Existing translation methods impose lexical constraints during either inference or training. At training time, constrained MT models include code-switching data augmentation (Dinu et al., 2019;Song et al., 2019; and training with auxiliary tasks such as token or span-level mask-prediction (Ailem et al., 2021;Lee et al., 2021). At inference time, autoregressive constrained decoding algorithms include utilizing placeholder tag (Luong et al., 2015;Crego et al., 2016), grid beam search (Hokamp and Liu, 2017;Post and Vilar, 2018) and alignment-enhanced decoding (Alkhouli et al., 2018;Song et al., 2020;Chen et al., 2021). For the purpose of efficiency, recent studies also focus on non-autoregressive constrained translation. Susanto et al. (2020) proposes to modify the inference procedure of Levenshtein Transformer (Gu et al., 2019) where they disallow the deletion of constraint words during iterative editing. Xu and Carpuat (2021) further develops this idea and introduces a reposition operation that can reorder the constraint tokens. Our work absorbs the idea of both lines of work. Based on NAT methods, we brings alignment information by terminologies to help learn the contextual information for lexical constraints, especially the rare ones. Recent efforts expand constrained decoding to phrase-level constraints and subword-aware handling in morphologically rich languages (Hasler et al., 2018;Song et al., 2020;Susanto et al., 2020). Concurrently, work on interactive post-editing integrates user-provided terms with constrained search to reduce editing effort (Knowles and Koehn, 2016;Marie and Fujita, 2018). Studies on document-level MT emphasize entity and terminology consistency with cache-based or memory-augmented models (Tu et al., 2018;Miculicich et al., 2018). Investigations into constraint extraction from noisy terminology lists address capitalization, inflection, and polysemy resolution (Dinu et al., 2019;Song et al., 2019). Non-Autoregressive Translation Although enjoy the speed advantage, NAT models suffer from performance degradation due to the multi-modality problem, i.e., generating text when multiple translations are plausible. Gu et al. (2018) applies sequence-level knowledge distillation (KD) (Kim and Rush, 2016) that uses an AT's output as an NAT's new target, which reduces word diversity and reordering complexity in reference, resulting in fewer modes (Zhou et al., 2020;Xu et al., 2021). Various algorithms have also been proposed to alleviate this problem, including incorporating latent variables (Kaiser et al., 2018;Shu et al., 2020), iterative refinement (Ghazvininejad et al., 2019;Stern et al., 2019;Gu et al., 2019;Guo et al., 2020), advanced training objective Du et al., 2021) and gradually learning targetside word inter-dependency by curriculum learning (Qian et al., 2021). Our work extends the family of editing-based iterative NAT models for its flexibility to impose lexical constraints (Susanto et al., 2020;Xu and Carpuat, 2021).

text: Recent work in recipe comprehension includes visual instructions (Huang et al., 2017;Nishimura et al., 2020) and linguistic texts (Agarwal and Miller, 2011;Kiddon et al., 2015;Jiang et al., 2020) across Japanese (Harashima and Hiramatsu, 2020;Harashima et al., 2016) and English (Batra et al., 2020;Marin et al., 2019). Most research models linguistic recipes as a workflow graph based on actions (Kiddon et al., 2015;Mori et al., 2014;, where the vertices represent name entities (e.g. action, food, etc.) and edges represent processing information (e.g. action complement, food complement, etc.). Although interactions among ingredients can be derived via action nodes, this approach doesn't sufficiently capture anaphoric phenomena, i.e. coreference and bridging. The RISeC corpus (Jiang et al., 2020) identifies candidate expressions for zero anaphora verbs in English recipes. However, they do not capture generic anaphoric phenomena.

text: With the rise of social media and hate speech forums online (Phadke and Mitra, 2020;Szendro, 2021) offensive language detection has become more important that ever before. Several recent works focus on characterizing various types of offensive language detection (Fortuna and Nunes, 2018;Shushkevich and Cardiff, 2019;Mishra et al., 2019;Parekh and Patel, 2017). But, works that focus solely on Stereotype Detection in English language are scarce. This is partly because stereotypes tend to be subtler offenses in comparison to other types are offensive languages and hence receive less immediate focus, and in part due to the challenge of requiring the knowledge of the stereotype's existence in society to reliably annotate data for the task. We approach this problem by breaking down various aspects of stereotypical text and crowd-sourcing annotations only for aspects that require linguistic understanding rather than world-knowledge. Few recent works have focused solely on stereotypes, some proposing pioneering diagnostic datasets (Nadeem et al., 2020;Nangia et al., 2020) while others worked on knowledge-based and semi-supervised learning based models (Fraser et al., 2021;Badjatiya et al., 2019) for identifying stereotypical text. Computational model based 1. Ethiopians like stew (Explicit Stereotype) 2. The lawyer misrepresented the situation and tricked the person (Implicit Stereotypical Association) 3. Jews spend money frivolously (Anti-Stereotypes) 4. There is an Asian family that lives down the street (Non-Stereotypes) works either use datasets meant for other tasks such as hate speech detection etc, or focus mainly on the available diagnostic datasets modified for classification task. But, diagnostic datasets suffer from lack of sufficient coverage of naturally occurring text due to their crowd-sourced construction procedure (Blodgett et al., 2021b). We address these issues in our work by collecting natural text data from social forum Reddit, by mining specific subreddits that contain mainly subtle stereotypical text.

text: Given the low-resource setting on Stereotype Detection task, semi-supervised data annotation is one plausible solution for the problem. Several recent works have also been focusing on reinforcementlearning guided semi-supervision (Ye et al., 2020;Konyushkova et al., 2020;Laskin et al., 2020). Ye et al. (2020), in particular, work with a singletask and unsupervised data to generate automatedannotations for new examples. In contrast, we use the data from neighboring tasks with different labels for multi-task learning and apply an RL agent to select examples for training the neighboring task in such a way that benefits the target task the most.

text: Prompt Learning The concept of prompt-based learning starts from the GPT3 model (Brown et al., 2020), which can complete various zero-shot or few-shot tasks with natural language instruction (i.e., prompts). Previous works design different prompts to query language models to extract knowledge triples (Petroni et al., 2019) or classify sentences into pre-defined categories (Schick and Schütze, 2021) in the few-shot setting. They con-struct various discrete prompts manually for these tasks. To reduce the human effort in this selection process, (Gao et al., 2021) proposes to expand prompts using pre-trained language models. The role of calibration in prompt selection has also been discussed in PET-style frameworks, and template verbalizers can alter label semantics without changing the model architecture (Schick and Schütze, 2021; Hambardzumyan et al., 2021). Multi-task prompt conditioning and instruction tuning further broaden the space of applicable formats for eliciting behavior from frozen models (Sanh et al., 2022). However, the selection of discrete prompts is still an independent process and cannot be optimized together with the downstream tasks in an end-to-end manner. To solve this issue, (Lester et al., 2021;Li and Liang, 2021) propose to use soft prompts, which are sets of trainable vectors, in the frozen pretrained language models. Unlike the hard prompts, these vectors do not correspond to any real words. It allows the optimization with the downstream tasks in an end-to-end manner. As shown in Li and Liang (2021), PLMs with Soft Prompts can often perform better in the low-resource setting.

text: Generative Data Augmentation There are previous works in applying LMs or PLMs to generate synthetic data. Hou et al. (2018) uses a seq2seq generative model to generate diverse utterances to improve dialogue understanding models. Xia et al. (2019)   (2020) apply LMs and PLMs to learn directly to generate new synthetic data for NLU tasks (i.e., sequence labeling and commonsense inference tasks after trained (fine-tuned) on the relatively large training data. These works often directly apply off-the-shelf LMs or PLMs to generate synthetic data. Data augmentation with back-translation has been widely explored in machine translation and text classification settings (Sennrich et al., 2016; Xie et al., 2020). Mixup-style semantic interpolation has also been proposed to regularize classifiers and improve robustness in low-resource regimes (Guo et al., 2019). To best of our knowledge, PromDA is the first prompt-based PLMs that are especially designed for the data augmentation task.

text: Different from LM-BFF that uses a single demonstration per label, our work uses 'multiple' demonstrations which are provided for automatically generated label phrases. In addition, inspired by the method of soft prompting, we use 'soft' demonstration memory based on globally-shared soft vectors for prompt tokens, without using hard tokens of the similar context. We report mean (and standard deviation) performance over 5 different splits. Majority: majority class "GPT-3" in-context learning: using the in-context learning proposed in (Brown et al., 2020) with RoBERTa-large (no parameter updates); man: manual prompt; LM-BFF & DART: we report the performance in (Gao et al., 2021;Zhang et al., 2021). full: fine-tuning using full training set.

text: There exist many works, often using text-based autoencoders that try to induce a sentence representation space for controllable text generation by learning new models (Shen et al., 2017(Shen et al., , 2020; Mai  Montero et al., 2021). Our work concludes that we can extract steering vectors from pretrained models that have latent spaces that allow operations like this, without having to train any new models at all. Other work approach controlling language models by adapting their hidden states using steerable layers, adapters, or steering their logits using auxiliary language models (Gulcehre et al., 2015;Dathathri et al., 2019;Houlsby et al., 2019;Zhang et al., 2020;Liu et al., 2021;Krause et al., 2021). Our method differs from all of these: we extract steering vectors directly from a language model and operate on the latent space occupied by these vectors and never fine-tune a component of the model. Subramani et al. ( 2019) investigate whether LSTM-based language models have sentence representations from which they can generate the original sentence. This premise relates to our first question: can we extract steering vectors, but we extend far beyond that showing that vector arithmetic in the context of unsupervised style transfer is effective in our latent steering space. Prior studies on attribute-controlled generation often cast control as conditional text production with style classifiers trained jointly or in a pipeline (Hu et al., 2017; Yang and Klein, 2021), and prompt engineering has been explored as a lightweight alternative in zero-shot regimes (Brown et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2023). A growing body of work studies causal mediation and representation interventions to probe internal neurons in transformers (Vig et al., 2020; Geiger et al., 2021). Topic-constrained decoding has also been examined using lexical control and bag-of-words objectives (Keskar et al., 2019; Dathathri et al., 2020). We focus instead on deriving compact, interpretable directions that consistently shift model outputs along semantic axes using only activations from a frozen backbone. Concretely, we estimate directions by contrasting activation statistics over unlabeled corpora partitioned by attributes and apply them at generation time via linear combination. This approach avoids training auxiliary classifiers or adapters while remaining compatible with off-the-shelf decoding. In experiments, we evaluate content preservation, attribute strength, and fluency across sentiment, formality, and toxicity control tasks. We also benchmark robustness under domain shift by varying source corpora and by injecting distractor attributes. Ablations analyze layer locality, vector normalization, and composition when multiple attributes are combined. Finally, we discuss ethical implications of controllability and release code to facilitate reproducibility.

text: A natural middle ground between these two approaches is partial fine-tuning, i.e. only fine-tuning some topmost layers of BERT while keeping the remaining bottom layers frozen. This approach has been studied in (Houlsby et al., 2019;Merchant et al., 2020), where the authors observed that finetuning only the top layers can almost achieve the performance of full fine-tuning on several GLUE tasks. The approach of partial fine-tuning essentially regards the bottom layers of BERT as a feature extractor. Freezing weights from bottom layers is a sensible idea as previous studies show that the mid layer representations produced by BERT are most transferrable, whereas the top layers representations are more task-oriented (Wang et al., 2019;Tenney et al., 2019b,a;Merchant et al., 2020). We focus on how partial fine-tuning interacts with optimization stability across tasks of varying label entropy. Prior work has emphasized the role of learning rate schedules in stabilizing full fine-tuning (Mosbach et al., 2021). Recent work also probes which layers encode syntactic versus semantic information, informing which layers might be better to freeze (Jawahar et al., 2019). Cross-task transfer studies have suggested that freezing lower layers preserves broadly useful features while adapting upper layers captures task-specific decision boundaries (Phang et al., 2018). We further examine whether adapter-like low-rank updates can complement partial fine-tuning without incurring the memory cost of full backpropagation through all layers. Such hybrid strategies try to preserve generalization while enabling targeted adaptation in shallow subspaces of the parameter manifold. Smith et al. (2020) survey adapter-based tuning for parameter efficiency, Li and Liang (2021) introduce prefix-tuning for conditional generation, and Lester et al. (2021) study prompt tuning with frozen language models, Zhao et al. (2021) examine calibration of language model prompts on classification benchmarks, Nye et al. (2021) investigate program synthesis with language models. In parallel, selective regularization methods constrain upper-layer drift to maintain compatibility with frozen lower-layer representations (Zhang et al., 2020). Layerwise learning rates and trust-region constraints have been proposed to modulate the magnitude of parameter changes as a function of depth (Sun et al., 2019). Empirical results indicate that such controls reduce catastrophic forgetting during task adaptation (Kirkpatrick et al., 2017). We build on these insights to design a protocol that allocates capacity across layers in proportion to estimated task shift. Our experiments target sentence classification, paraphrase detection, and natural language inference. These tasks reflect different levels of semantic abstraction and benefit differently from upper-layer adaptation. We report results on standard splits with carefully matched hyperparameters to isolate the effect of partial fine-tuning. We measure both accuracy and stability under multiple random seeds. Stability is quantified by variance across runs and sensitivity to learning rate perturbations. We also analyze representational similarity between frozen and adapted layers. This analysis helps attribute gains to either better feature reuse or improved task-specific transformations. To ensure fairness, we compare against full fine-tuning, feature-based baselines, and lightweight adapter baselines. We include parameter and compute accounting to contextualize trade-offs. We also study convergence speed as an operational metric. Partial fine-tuning often converges faster due to a smaller effective optimization space. Nonetheless, it may plateau earlier if capacity is insufficient for complex label structures. We investigate curriculum strategies that gradually unfreeze layers based on validation plateaus. This approach attempts to recover capacity only when needed. We find that such schedules can match full fine-tuning on several GLUE tasks while using fewer trainable parameters. On tasks requiring nuanced lexical semantics, additional upper layers yield measurable gains. On tasks dominated by surface features, freezing more layers maintains performance while saving compute. Error analysis reveals that partial fine-tuning struggles primarily with compositional inference cases. This suggests that some mid-layer adaptation may be necessary for robust generalization. Finally, we release code and configuration files to facilitate reproducibility. We anticipate that future work will refine depth-aware optimization for broader modality coverage. Our study underscores that principled allocation of adaptation across depth is a practical alternative to full fine-tuning. It offers competitive accuracy, improved stability, and reduced resource footprint. The results invite a rethinking of how much of a large model must truly change for a new task.

text: Recent neural approaches to pragmatic reasoning highlight data-driven learning of cooperative strategies (Andreas and Klein, 2016; Lazaridou and Baroni, 2020; Monroe and Potts, 2015). Frank and Goodman (2012) formalize pragmatic inference via recursive reasoning between speakers and listeners in controlled reference games. Large-scale vision–language models have also been evaluated on image captioning benchmarks to assess pragmatic adequacy (Xu et al., 2015; Anderson et al., 2018).

text: Recent work extends attribution to generative settings by aligning token-level salience with sequence-level preferences (Hase et al., 2021), attention rollout composes heads across layers to estimate effective receptive fields (Abnar and Zuidema, 2020), fidelity metrics quantify sufficiency and comprehensiveness of rationales (DeYoung et al., 2020), large language models can produce self-explanations that correlate with gradient-based scores (Turpin et al., 2023), and counterfactual editing evaluates causal faithfulness of attributions under model perturbations (Vig et al., 2020).

text: Performance Prediction. Prior work has explored predicting the performance of machine learning models from unlabelled data by either measuring (dis)agreements between multiple classifiers (Platanios et al., 2014(Platanios et al., , 2017 or by utilizing underlying information about data distribution (Domhan et al., 2015). In the context of NLP Birch et al. (2008) explored predicting the performance of a Machine Translation system by utilizing different explanatory variables for the language pairs. Lin et al. (2019) proposed a learning to rank approach to choose transfer languages for cross lingual learning using several linguistic and dataset specific features. Specifically predicting the zero-shot performance of MMLMs was first explored in Lauscher et al. (2020), where they used a linear regression model to estimate the cross-lingual transfer performance based on pre-training data size and linguistic relatedness features. Srinivasan et al. (2021) tackled this problem by utilizing XGBoost Regressor for the prediction along with a larger set of features. Recently, Dolicki and Spanakis (2021) explored individual syntactic features for zero-shot performance prediction instead of working with aggregate similarity values, and showed about 2 to 4 times gain in performance. We extend these work by considering a multi-task learning approach, where performance prediction in a task utilizes not only the data available for that task, but also the patterns observed for other tasks.

text: Another popular task is legal topic classification. Nallapati and Manning (2008) highlighted the challenges of legal document classification compared to more generic text classification by using a dataset including docket entries of US court cases. Chalkidis et al. (2020a) classify EU laws into Eu-roVoc concepts, a task earlier introduced by Mencia and Fürnkranzand (2007), with a special interest in few-and zero-shot learning. Luz de Araujo et al. (2020) also studied topic classification using a dataset of Brazilian Supreme Court cases. There are similar interesting applications in contract law (Lippi et al., 2019;Tuggener et al., 2020). Several studies (Chalkidis et al., 2019c;Hendrycks et al., 2021) explored information extraction from contracts, to extract important information such as the contracting parties, agreed payment amount, start and end dates, applicable law, etc. Other studies focus on extracting information from legislation (Cardellino et al., 2017;Angelidis et al., 2018) or court cases (Leitner et al., 2019).

text: Majority of the work in the legal domain has focused on the higher court (Malik et al., 2021;Strickson and De La Iglesia, 2020;Zhong et al., 2020b); however, the lower courts handle the maximum number of cases. We try to address this gap by releasing a large corpus of district court level legal documents.  et al., 2019, 2017) have published legal data in Japanese, along with their English translation. The competition had two sub-tasks, a legal information retrieval task and an entailment identification task between law articles and queries. Multiple datasets in Chinese have been released for different tasks, namely Reading Comprehension (Duan et al., 2019), Similar Case Matching (Xiao et al., 2019), Question Answering (Zhong et al., 2020b). Duan et al. (2019) proposed Chinese judicial reading comprehension (CJRC) dataset with about 10K documents and almost 50K questions with answers. Zhong et al. (2020b) presented JEC-QA, a legal question answering dataset collected from the National Judicial Examination of China with about 26K multiple-choice questions. They augment the dataset with a database containing the legal knowledge required to answer the questions and also assign meta information to each of the questions for in-depth analysis. Xiao et al. (2019) proposed CAIL2019-SCM, a dataset containing 8,964 triplets of the case document, with the objective to identify which two cases are more similar in the triplets. Similar case matching has a crucial application as it helps to identify comparable historical cases. A historical case with similar facts often serves as a legal precedent and influences the judgement. Such historical information can be used to make the legal judgement prediction models more robust. Kleinberg et al. (2017) proposed bail decision prediction as a good proxy to gauge if machine learning can improve human decision making. A large number of bail documents along with the binary decision (granted or denied) makes it an ideal task for automation. In this paper, we also propose the bail prediction task using the HLDC corpus.

text: Our work is related to dataset construction for building persuasive dialogue systems that try to persuade the participant to take a specific action. Hiraoka et al. (2014) annotated 34 dialogues, in which an experienced salesperson tries to convince a customer to buy a camera. Yoshino et al. (2018) requested crowdsourcing workers to generate 200 persuasive dialogues. In each dialogue, one participant persuaded another one to adopt his suggestion such as cleaning a room.  collected 1017 dialogues, in which one of the participants was convinced to donate to a specific charity. We can see that the covered conversation scenarios in these datasets were strictly limited to specific tasks, while our scenarios are more general and can be easily extended to different cases. Also, our constructed dataset is about three times larger than the prior work, indicating the usefulness of the recent pre-trained paradigm.

text: Recent work has also examined cross-modal pretraining for audio-visual grounding (Harwath et al., 2018), multi-task learning for paralinguistic cues (Schuller et al., 2013), and large-scale multilingual speech modeling (Conneau et al., 2021). These studies emphasize dataset scale, label scarcity, and cross-lingual transfer. Several approaches explore data augmentation for robust ASR (Park et al., 2019) and curriculum learning for speech representations (Hsu et al., 2020). Methods for self-training with pseudo-labels show gains on low-resource languages (Kahn et al., 2020). Pretrained acoustic models have been adapted for speaker diarization (Sell et al., 2018) and keyword spotting (Sainath and Parada, 2015), and unsupervised unit discovery remains an open challenge (Lee et al., 2015).

text: In the past few years, ARA research has been primarily focused on textual embeddings and deep learning based architectures. Word embeddings in combination with other attributes such as domain knowledge or language modeling (Cha et al., 2017;Jiang et al., 2018) were used for ARA. A range of neural architectures, from multi attentive RNN (Azpiazu and Pera, 2019) to deep reinforcement learning (Mohammadi and Khasteh, 2019) were proposed. Recent research explored combining transformers with linguistic features (Deutsch et al., 2020;Meng et al., 2020;Lee et al., 2021;Imperial, 2021). While Deutsch et al. (2020) did not report any improvements with linguistic features, the others reported that combining linguistic features with deep learning approaches result in better ARA models. Smith and Jones (2019) introduced curriculum-aware pretraining regimes for educational text analytics. Parallel studies investigated readability in the context of automatic question generation (Liu et al., 2020) and sentence simplification pipelines (Zhao et al., 2018). Transformer pretraining objectives were modified to emphasize discourse markers (Koto et al., 2020). Domain adaptation techniques using adversarial learning were also proposed for low-resource annotation settings (Ruder and Plank, 2018). Several works explored knowledge distillation from large language models to compact predictors (Sanh et al., 2019). Weak supervision strategies aggregated heuristic signals for label construction (Ratner et al., 2017). Cross-corpus calibration methods aligned scale differences between datasets (Vajjala and Meurers, 2012). Multilingual benchmarks were curated to facilitate reproducible comparisons (Rama and Berzak, 2021).
