text: There has been a growing body of research studying the inner workings of BERT and trying to quantify what it learns in various scenarios, dubbed "BERTology" (Rogers et al., 2020). Of particular interest to this paper is work that analyzes BERT's output embeddings. Recent studies have found that embeddings created from the final layer of BERT tend to cluster according to word senses (Wiedemann et al., 2019), though this varies somewhat based on the position of a word in a sentence (Mickus et al., 2020). The final BERT layers also produce more contextualized word embeddings than the earlier layers (Ethayarajh, 2019), a finding we revisit using paraphrases in Section 3.3. Several strands of research examine how these properties interact with downstream tasks, reporting heterogeneous effects across tagging and parsing benchmarks (Tenney et al., 2019; Hewitt and Manning, 2019). Probing studies introduce diagnostic classifiers to quantify which syntactic or semantic attributes are linearly recoverable from different layers (Alain and Bengio, 2017; Belinkov and Glass, 2019). Layer-wise anisotropy has been argued to distort similarity judgments in high-dimensional spaces (Li et al., 2020). Tokenization strategies influence subword alignment and sense granularity (Kudo and Richardson, 2018; Bostrom and Durrett, 2020). Cross-lingual evaluation explores whether the same clustering holds under multilingual training regimes (Pires et al., 2019; Dufter and Schütze, 2020). To frame our contribution, we separate prior work into three themes: structural recoverability, semantic consolidation, and robustness under distribution shift. Structural recoverability concerns whether linear projections from hidden states encode parse trees, dependency arcs, and coreference links, typically evaluated with minimal supervision on frozen representations (Hewitt and Manning, 2019; Clark et al., 2019; Voita et al., 2019). Semantic consolidation focuses on sense separation and compositional meaning across layers, often leveraging contextual similarity tests, word sense inventories, or controlled paraphrase pairs (Wiedemann et al., 2019; Ethayarajh, 2019; Mickus et al., 2020). Robustness lines examine sensitivity to tokenization, domain shift, and multilingual transfer, assessing whether embedding geometry and separability persist across conditions (Kudo and Richardson, 2018; Pires et al., 2019; Dufter and Schütze, 2020). Within this landscape, our study targets paraphrase-aware representation structure in the top layers of BERT. We quantify layerwise concentration and inter-sense margins using margin-based clustering and isotropy-adjusted similarity scores. In contrast to prior sense clustering work, we control for sentence position and subword segmentation by enforcing aligned contexts and shared token boundaries across paraphrase variants. We also evaluate whether paraphrase invariance correlates with linear probe accuracy on semantic role labeling and entailment, testing the hypothesis that sense consolidation aids downstream generalization. Our dataset augments existing paraphrase corpora with carefully balanced sense-ambiguous targets and position-matched minimal pairs. We report analyses across base and large models, with and without fine-tuning, to disentangle pretraining effects from task adaptation. The results suggest that top-layer embeddings exhibit stronger paraphrase collapse while preserving inter-sense separability, consistent with a selective compression hypothesis. Additional ablations isolate the impact of anisotropy correction on neighborhood purity and cluster margin statistics. Finally, we situate our findings alongside concurrent work on representation geometry in instruction-tuned models, noting convergent trends in contextual concentration under paraphrastic variation. Our contributions are: a controlled protocol for paraphrase-aligned embedding analysis; new metrics linking concentration, separability, and probe performance; and a set of reproducible benchmarks for future BERTology studies. We release code and data to facilitate standardized evaluation and comparison. We believe these results clarify when and how final-layer embeddings encode sense-specific information while supporting paraphrase invariance. This, in turn, informs the design of representation-level constraints and regularizers that encourage beneficial compression without erasing critical distinctions. Looking ahead, extending these diagnostics to multilingual and domain-adapted encoders may reveal further nuances in cross-lingual sense consolidation. We also anticipate that coupling our analysis with causal interventions on attention heads will illuminate mechanisms that mediate paraphrase robustness. Beyond BERT, the same methodology applies to encoder-only backbones in retrieval and reranking, where stability under paraphrase is directly tied to effectiveness. By articulating precise links between geometry and task outcomes, we aim to move BERTology from descriptive observations toward actionable guidance for model development. We conclude that principled measurement of paraphrase-aware structure is both feasible and informative for practice.

text: Pretrained generative LLMs (Raffel et al., 2020;Brown et al., 2020) have enable framing NLP tasks as seq2seq problems. Some DST papers (Zhao et al., 2021a;Feng et al., 2021) look at settings with no train-test discrepancy. Many studies explore the efficacy of task-specific prompts (Jiang et al., 2020;Liu et al., 2021). Madotto et al. (2020) prime LMs with examples for dialogue tasks, but without finetuning. Wei et al. (2021) fine-tune language models to understand prompts for a different task.

text: Grounded Language Acquisition. This task is commonly realized as grounded grammar induction from image-caption pairs (Shi et al., 2019;Zhao and Titov, 2020), or as (neural) semantic parsers learning from a reward signal (Williams, 1992) in visual question answering Yi et al., 2018) or in planning (Azaria et al., 2016;Wang et al., 2016Wang et al., , 2017Karamcheti et al., 2020). Shi et al. (2019) focus on learning phrase-structure rules grounded in visual contexts, while Zhao and Titov (2020) learn latent alignments between captions and scene graphs with weak supervision. Williams (1992) frames learning as reinforcement over semantic parses in dialogue tasks. Yi et al. (2018) propose a compositional neural module network for CLEVR-style reasoning. Azaria et al. (2016) study instruction following with high-level task representations. Wang et al. (2016; 2017) investigate semantic parsing for navigation in interactive worlds, and Karamcheti et al. (2020) analyze compositional generalization in grounded agents. In these scenarios, the primary objective is to learn mappings from linguistic expressions to logical forms, which in turn get associated with certain visual percepts during the learning process. This paper does not tackle learning logical forms. Instead, we obtain them from an existing broad-coverage grammar and focus on exploiting the logical consequences of those logical forms during symbol grounding-i.e., our focus is to utilise the interpretation of logical forms, and in particular the truth functional meanings of close-class words like quantifiers and negation, to inform the learning of mappings from (open-class) symbols like red to their denotations, given the visual percepts.

text: A Brief History of NLU Datasets. As a core module of ToD systems, NLU has been researched since the early 1990s, when the Airline Travel Information System (ATIS) project was started (Hemphill et al., 1990), consisting of spoken queries on flightrelated information. Over the next two decades, very few NLU resources were released. The lack of ToD NLU resources ended in 2013, with the beginning of the 'dialogue state tracking (DST) era' (Williams et al., 2013;Henderson et al., 2014;Kim et al., 2016). Instead of just classifying each turn of the user, DST deals with keeping track of the user's goal over the entire dialogue history, i.e., all the previous user and system turns. Several datasets where released during the DST challenges, all of them comprising simple intent sets (usually tagged as dialogue acts). To adapt to the increasing data requirements of deep learning models, increasingly larger dialogue datasets have been released in recent years (Budzianowski et al., 2018;Wei et al., 2018;Rastogi et al., 2019;Peskov et al., 2019). However, the design of ToD datasets comes with some profound differences to datasets for e.g. machine translation or speech recognition, which affect current ToD datasets. The domain-specific nature of ToD datasets made the data tied to its ontologies, not allowing data reusability across different domains. The domain-specific ontologies required a lot of expertise for annotation, therefore many annotation mistakes were made (Eric et al., 2019). These constraints motivated initiatives that attempt to abstract slot-value spaces, unify schema representations, and reduce annotation costs through programmatic supervision. Schema-Guided Dialogue (Rastogi et al., 2019) proposed a unified ontology interface that generalizes to unseen services by representing slots and intents via natural language descriptions. MultiWOZ (Budzianowski et al., 2018) scaled multi-domain coverage but also exposed the fragility of annotation pipelines and the need for rigorous validation protocols. Recent updates to MultiWOZ introduced refined slot ontologies, turn-level corrections, and clearer delexicalization rules to foster consistency across domains. The emphasis on generalization further spurred work on transferable representations that minimize ontology coupling while preserving task fidelity. The shift from turn-level intent tagging toward belief-state tracking and policy learning created pressure for richer supervision signals, including slot carryover, coreference, and correction phenomena. Benchmarking practices increasingly distinguish between in-domain and cross-domain generalization, as well as between seen and unseen schema elements at test time. Signal sparsity in end-to-end ToD datasets prompted the integration of weak labels from templates, synthetic dialogues, and self-training on unlabeled logs. Data-centric techniques such as active learning and error-driven relabeling were introduced to focus annotation budgets on the most impactful examples. Robustness concerns have also led to the inclusion of adversarial paraphrases and noise-injected utterances to emulate real-world ASR and typing errors. Recent work on broader conversational AI resources further diversified data characteristics, highlighting cross-domain task orientation (Budzianowski et al., 2018), schema-guided interactions (Rastogi et al., 2019), and mixed-initiative behaviors (Peskov et al., 2019). Multimodal task dialogue introduced visual grounding demands and alignment protocols (Wei et al., 2018). End-to-end dialogue collections reported scalability properties under weak supervision (Eric et al., 2019). Crowdsourced paraphrase banks examined lexical variety and robustness (Zhang et al., 2020). At the same time, dataset curation has increasingly emphasized provenance tracking, annotator training, and inter-annotator agreement as first-class artifacts. The emergence of instruction-tuned LLMs has revived interest in unifying NLU and DST signals under a single prompting interface. New benchmarks link semantic parsing, belief updates, and tool invocation logs to evaluate compositional generalization. Cross-lingual ToD datasets now explore ontology projection and slot canonicalization across languages with different morphological properties. Privacy-preserving data release has motivated federated collection protocols and selective redaction of personally identifiable information. Finally, our work synthesizes these lines by proposing evaluation settings that explicitly separate schema generalization, robustness to paraphrase, and tolerance to noisy supervision.

text:
In this section, we discuss related studies with repect to the contrastive learning framework and sentence embedding.
Contrastive learning has become a dominant paradigm for representation learning by aligning semantically similar instances while repelling dissimilar ones.
Early formulations relied on the InfoNCE objective to establish a lower bound on mutual information between paired views (Oord et al., 2018).
In NLP, paired views are commonly induced via data augmentation, dropout-induced noise, or parallel corpora.
Prior work on sentence-level contrastive objectives traces back to instance discrimination in computer vision (Wu et al., 2018), InfoNCE-based mutual information maximization (Oord et al., 2018), and large-batch supervised contrastive training (Khosla et al., 2020). SimCSE leverages dropout noise to create positive pairs from identical sentences (Gao et al., 2021). Unsupervised methods using pseudo positives drawn from augmentation pipelines study invariances in token deletion and back-translation (Yan et al., 2021). Researchers have also explored curriculum signals for progressively harder negatives (Kumar et al., 2022). STS benchmarks remain a standard for evaluation (Agirre et al., 2012).
Supervised variants exploit natural positives from NLI entailment or paraphrase pairs to improve semantic alignment (Reimers and Gurevych, 2019; Gao et al., 2021).
Hard negative mining has been shown to mitigate representation collapse and sharpen cluster boundaries in embedding space (Robinson et al., 2020).
Queue- or memory-bank-based retrieval expands the pool of negatives beyond minibatch size to stabilize contrastive objectives (He et al., 2020).
Temperature scaling and margin-based losses calibrate gradients for fine-grained similarity estimation on semantic tasks (Wang and Liu, 2021).
In-domain pretraining on discourse-rich corpora further improves sentence-level transfer to retrieval and reranking (Thakur et al., 2021).
Beyond pairwise losses, listwise and distributional alignment objectives align entire similarity spectra to better reflect human judgments (Chen et al., 2022).
Augmentation strategies range from lexical substitution and word dropout to back-translation and span masking, each controlling invariance strength and semantic drift (Wei and Zou, 2019; Fadaee et al., 2017).
Recent multilingual adaptations share a single encoder across languages using bitext anchors to propagate semantic geometry (Reimers and Gurevych, 2020).
Cross-modal extensions integrate textual sentences with images or audio using shared contrastive spaces for retrieval and description tasks (Radford et al., 2021).
A parallel line of research studies post-hoc calibration and isotropy to improve cosine similarity reliability for zero-shot retrieval (Gao et al., 2024).
Evaluation commonly includes semantic textual similarity, question answering reranking, and information retrieval with domain-specific negatives (Cer et al., 2017; Thakur et al., 2021).
While most methods optimize for general-purpose embeddings, task-adaptive contrastive tuning can yield substantial gains for legal and biomedical domains (Guha et al., 2021; Lee et al., 2020).
Our work builds on these advances by combining lightweight augmentation with curriculum-aware hard negatives to stabilize training at small batch sizes.
We also introduce a sentence-level calibration module that improves isotropy without additional pretraining data.
Comprehensive experiments on STS, MS MARCO reranking, and multilingual transfer demonstrate consistent improvements over strong baselines.
Ablations isolate the contributions of augmentation strength, temperature schedules, and memory-bank size to clarify design trade-offs.
Together, these findings situate our approach within the broader landscape of contrastive sentence embedding methods while clarifying practical choices for robust deployment.

text: Recent studies extend smoothing to calibration and uncertainty estimation in large-scale vision (Müller et al., 2019) and to sequence modeling with regularized targets (Pereyra et al., 2017; Søgaard et al., 2021). Several works also explore subword granularity effects in multilingual tokenization (Kudo, 2018; Conneau et al., 2020). Byte-level encodings have been proposed to avoid fragmentation in morphologically rich languages (Xue et al., 2022), and corpus-dependent merges influence cross-lingual transfer (Rust et al., 2021). Token sharing strategies are evaluated under low-resource conditions (Zoph et al., 2016), coverage penalties mitigate rare-word omissions (Wu et al., 2016), and architectural depth interacts with vocabulary size (Dehghani et al., 2019).

text: Sparse attention mechanism The full attention mechanism has a quadratic memory cost. Prior research works have proposed different sparse attention mechanisms to reduce the memory cost. Longformer (Beltagy et al., 2020) uses a dilated sliding window of blocks and global attention patterns. BigBird (Zaheer et al., 2020) employs sliding window and random blocks. Reformer (Kitaev et al., 2020) uses the locality-sensitive hashing. In addition to optimizing the encoder self-attention, Huang et al. (2021) proposes head-wise positional strides to reduce the cost of the encoder-decoder attention. However, sparse attention diminishes the benefits of pretraining and sacrifices parts of the receptive field.

text: Recent studies investigate retrieval-augmented generation for factual QA (Lewis et al., 2020), explore chain-of-thought prompting to elicit intermediate reasoning (Wei et al., 2022), and analyze dataset artifacts that inflate apparent reasoning capability (Niven and Kao, 2019). Neural module networks decompose tasks into composable operations (Andreas et al., 2016). Curriculum learning schedules influence compositional generalization (Bengio et al., 2009).

Neural module networks decompose tasks into composable operations (Andreas et al., 2016). Curriculum learning schedules influence compositional generalization (Bengio et al., 2009).

text: Sentence rewriting IUR can be considered to be similar to the sentence rewriting task (Xu and Veeramachaneni, 2021;Lin et al., 2021;Chen and Bansal, 2018;Cao et al., 2018). Recent studies have been addressed the IUR task with various sophisticated methods. Text generation IUR can be formulated as text generation by using the seq2seq model (Pan et al., 2019;Huang et al., 2021). For the generation, several well-known pre-trained models have been applied (Lewis et al., 2020;Brown et al., 2020;Raffel et al., 2020) with promising results. We employ the T5 model (Raffel et al., 2020) as the main component to rewrite utterances. To address the problem of missing important tokens in model's rewriting, we enhance T5 by introducing a Picker and two labeling methods (Section 3.2). The learning of Θ is composed by only using utterance generation (the generator) or the combination of two tasks: important token identification (the picker) and utterance generation (the generator). Recent work on controllable paraphrasing emphasizes attribute conditioning (Krishna et al., 2020), and evaluation frameworks often rely on semantic similarity metrics that correlate with human judgments (Zhao et al., 2019). Li and Liang (2021) explore disentangling style from content in sentence transfers, and Madaan et al. (2022) investigate edit-based generation with learned operations. Pasunuru and Bansal (2018) study multi-reward optimization for abstractive summarization. In our formulation, the picker scores token salience using lightweight classifiers trained on weak labels derived from coverage heuristics. The generator consumes both the original utterance and a token-importance prompt to encourage faithful rewriting while preserving critical entities. We train the two components jointly with alternating updates and a curriculum that increases the difficulty of masked spans. For evaluation, we report both automatic and human metrics, including semantic fidelity, preservation of named entities, and fluency. We also include ablations that remove the picker, vary the prompting scheme, or freeze the encoder to isolate the contribution of each design choice. Our experiments span multi-domain dialogue benchmarks where utterances must be rewritten for clarity without altering intent. Results indicate consistent gains in faithfulness and content preservation over strong pretrained baselines. Error analyses reveal that gains concentrate on long inputs with multiple slot values, a common failure case for purely generative models. We release code and model checkpoints to facilitate replication and further research. Finally, we discuss ethical considerations related to rewriting user content in safety-critical contexts and provide guidelines for deployment.

text: Several works have since shifted focus toward the more challenging phoneme discovery problem by formulating it as a self-supervised learning problem where the semantics of the speech are known, such as from translation, phonemelevel language models or other sensory modalities such as vision. (Jansen, 2013) has studied the use of pairwise word identity labels for training phoneme discovery models based on Gaussian mixture models (GMM); (Harwath and Glass, 2019) analyzes the hidden layers of a two-branch neural network trained to retrieve spoken captions with semantically related images and finds strong correlation between segment representation and phoneme boundaries. (Harwath et al., 2020) adds hierarchical vector quantization (VQ) layers in the same retrieval network and is able to find a much smaller codebook than the unsupervised neural approach (Baevski et al., 2020), and achieve high correlation with the phoneme inventory. (Godard et al., 2018;Boito et al., 2019) has studied the possibility of learning semantic units using an attention-based speech-to-text translation system, though the units appear to correlate more with words. Works on unsupervised speech recognition  attempt to learn to recognize phonemes by leveraging the semantic information from a phoneme language model unpaired with the speech, typically by matching the empirical prior and posterior distributions of phonemes either using cross entropy (Yeh et al., 2019) or adversarial loss Baevski et al., 2021). Throughout the paper, we use P{•} to denote probability. We use capital letters to denote random variables and lower-case letters to represent samples of random variables. We use P X := P{X = x} to denote both probability mass and density functions of random variable X, depending on whether it is continuous or discrete. Further, denote P Y |X (y|x) := P{Y = y|X = x} as the true conditional probability distribution of random variable Y = y given random variable X = x. The probability simplex in R d is denoted as ∆ d .

text:
This section provides background and motivation for task and domain adaptability applied to concern detection in the 2017 French Election domain ( §2.1, §2.2), including concern values induced from Moral Foundations Theory (MFT) ( §2.3).
We study adaptability along two axes: task transfer from related annotations and domain transfer across political contexts and languages.
Our objective is to characterize how concern values can be robustly inferred when topical content, linguistic register, and audience vary.
We focus on publicly available Twitter and news datasets to enable reproducibility and to facilitate controlled adaptation experiments.
We define concern detection as assigning concern values derived from MFT to text spans that express evaluative judgments tied to political actors and issues.
The 2017 French Election domain presents a natural testbed with heterogeneous sources, rapid agenda shifts, and multilingual artifacts.
We formalize domain adaptability as minimizing performance degradation when moving from source to target distributions under limited labels.
We further formalize task adaptability as transferring supervision from stance, sentiment, or toxicity signals to concern labels.
We hypothesize that structured priors from MFT can regularize adaptation by constraining label topology across domains.
We also hypothesize that lexical drift interacts with moral dimension sparsity, necessitating sample-efficient fine-tuning.
Prior work on stance and concern detection has developed domain-agnostic classifiers using distant supervision from hashtags and weak labels (Mohammad et al., 2016; Augenstein et al., 2016). Neural sequence models with contextual embeddings improved robustness under topical shifts in political discourse (Devlin et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2019). Studies on event-specific adaptation examined candidate-centered narratives in U.S. election cycles and leveraged metadata to capture issue salience (Barberá, 2015; Buntain and Golbeck, 2015; Budak et al., 2016). MFT has been operationalized through lexicons and regression-based scoring of moral valence in social media corpora (Graham et al., 2013; Hopp et al., 2021). Multilingual sentiment resources have been aligned to enable cross-lingual projection of affective features (Mohammad et al., 2016; Barnes et al., 2018).
Recent advances in cross-lingual transfer show that multilingual encoders can generalize concern cues when anchored by shared subword vocabularies.
Adversarial domain adaptation has been used to learn invariant representations that suppress spurious lexical markers.
Meta-learning approaches aim to optimize for rapid adaptation by simulating domain shifts during training.
Prompt-based tuning provides an alternative that reframes concern prediction as masked token completion with moral descriptors.
Data augmentation techniques, such as paraphrasing and back-translation, reduce overfitting to domain-specific expressions.
Curriculum schedules that order training examples by lexical divergence can stabilize optimization under severe shift.
Incorporating external knowledge about candidates, parties, and policy positions can ground concern assignments.
Graph-based methods represent interactions among users, outlets, and topics to propagate weak moral signals.
We organize prior work around three themes: label structure, representation learning, and evaluation under shift.
Label structure research connects MFT to observable linguistic patterns and examines inter-annotator reliability in moral coding.
Representation learning emphasizes disentangling moral dimensions from stance and sentiment confounds.
Evaluation under shift investigates reliability across events, languages, and media platforms with matched metrics.
Within label structure, lexicon-based methods estimate moral salience but can struggle with irony and pragmatic context.
Neural classifiers with attention or rationales enable partial interpretability while preserving adaptation capacity.
Contrastive objectives align instances that share concern values despite surface differences.
Uncertainty estimation, including conformal prediction, can flag low-confidence concern in out-of-domain samples.
Our study contributes a benchmark for French election materials with aligned concern annotations and task-transfer splits.
We provide protocols for few-shot adaptation and report metrics that isolate error due to domain versus task shift.
We also release ablations on label priors and data selection to guide practitioners facing resource constraints.
Finally, we discuss broader impacts, including risks of misclassification in sensitive political contexts.
We aim to inform the design of adaptive, transparent systems for concern detection grounded in established theory and empirical rigor.

text: Instead of manually designing prompts (i.e., prompt engineering), some recent studies also explore automatic prompt generation. PETAL (Schick et al., 2020) augments Pattern Exploiting Training (PET, Schick and Schütze, 2021a,b) with automatically identified label words; Gao et al. (2021) uses re-ranking to find the best label words by fine-tuning a RoBERTa model on the candidates searched by RoBERTa, and using an external generation model for data augmentation of prompt templates; AutoPrompt (Shin et al., 2020) uses a gradient-based search to determine both prompts and label words. However, these methods require parameter updates with gradient descent, which is infeasible without access to the model weights (e.g., GPT-3). PET and its variants also require a large unlabeled set and need to be fine-tuned multiple times. AutoPrompt uses discretization techniques to approximately map a continuous vector back to tokens in the vocabulary (i.e., "vocablization"). These searched prompts and labels are often uninterpretable by humans. Different from these prior studies, our proposed AMuLaP is a simple and interpretable method for few-shot prompting that can work well with and without access to model weights. Concurrently to our work,  propose a method that exploits an external knowledge base to find label mapping.

text: While much of the literature in NLP recognizes that idioms share a cluster of properties, including semantic idiosyncrasy, syntactic inflexibility, and institutionalization (e.g., Sag et al., 2002;Fazly and Stevenson, 2006;Fazly et al., 2009), our approach is novel in attempting to characterize idioms along two orthogonal dimensions that correspond to specific proposals from the cognitive science literature. Recent work has also explored compositional distributional semantics for figurative language, including tensor-based frameworks and contextualized embeddings (Baroni et al., 2014; Boleda, 2020; Ethayarajh, 2019). Neural methods that fine-tune pre-trained language models for idiom token classification have reported gains on benchmark datasets (Salton et al., 2016; Hashempour and Villavicencio, 2020). Phrase-level paraphrase generation has been proposed as a proxy for detecting non-literal usage (Gao et al., 2018; Zhou et al., 2021). Crowdsourced resources documenting idiom variability provide annotations for inflection and substitution patterns (Cook et al., 2008; Haagsma et al., 2020). Corpus-driven studies of lexical fixedness emphasize frequency and dispersion statistics across genres (Erman and Warren, 2000; Gries, 2008). Our measures may offer a new avenue for tackling the problem of automatic idiom detection.

text: Recent work on multi-image reasoning has introduced heterogeneous supervision signals such as temporal order constraints, cross-image coreference, and entity tracking, and these supervision choices vary widely across datasets (Vedantam et al., 2017; Hu et al., 2019; Suhr et al., 2019). Some studies emphasize compositional generalization for attribute bindings and relational cues (Forbes et al., 2019; Hendricks and Nematzadeh, 2021). Liu et al. (2021) compare contrastive pretraining with task-specific fine-tuning for multi-image retrieval and question answering. Yan et al. (2021) extend multi-step reasoning with attention over localized regions. Hosseinzadeh and Wang (2021) focus on multi-camera surveillance narratives. Bogin et al. (2021) present a semantic parsing view of visual reasoning that links symbolic operators to visual evidence.

text: Several datasets propose tightly controlled synthetic frames to reduce linguistic confounds in evaluation (Jhamtani and Berg-Kirkpatrick, 2018a; Bansal et al., 2020). Other work has emphasized naturalistic variation and open-vocabulary references across scenes (Vedantam et al., 2017; Hu et al., 2019). Suhr et al. (2019) introduce grounded language tasks with real-world clutter and distractors. Forbes et al. (2019) analyze robustness to attribute swaps and relation reversals. Hendricks and Nematzadeh (2021) investigate category-level generalization under novel compositions. Liu et al. (2021) study cross-dataset transfer for retrieval and VQA settings.

text: Recently, Thorne et al. ( 2021) introduced the WIKINLDB dataset, which includes queries that require reasoning over a set of textual facts. Queries are instantiated with values from a knowledge graph (KG), and facts are generated by a LM. Unlike this work, WIKINLDB is focused on evaluating reasoning skills. We, on the other hand, show that generated examples can be used to endow a pretrained LM with new reasoning skills. Moreover, tables are much easier to collect at scale compared to KGs, which tend to have limited coverage. Data augmenatation techniques have been extensively explored in RC, QA, and dialogue (Feng refers to all questions that do not require reasoning over the image modality.     Khashabi et al., 2020;Alberti et al., 2019;Puri et al., 2020;Bartolo et al., 2021). Here, we focus on tables as a valuable source for data generation.

text: EGEC resources. There is a lot of work on EGEC data construction. As the two earliest EGEC datasets, FCE (Yannakoudakis et al., 2011) and NU-CLE (Dahlmeier et al., 2013) adopt the error-coded annotation paradigm. In contrast, JFLEG (Napoles et al., 2017) collects sentences from TOFEL exams and adopts the direct rewriting paradigm. W&I (Bryant et al., 2019) also chooses the direct rewriting paradigm, and for each original sentence additionally provides the language proficiency level of the writer. All four datasets are composed of essays from non-native English speakers and provide multiple references. Recently, several studies on automated assessment examine fluency-oriented rewrites, while domain adaptation in corpora targeting medical or legal writing has received attention (Rudzewicz et al., 2018; Kim and Lee, 2019; Park et al., 2020). Pre-training strategies for grammar correction are explored with large-scale synthetic noise injection (Grundkiewicz et al., 2019) and span-masked denoising (Lewis et al., 2020). Cross-lingual transfer in error correction has also been investigated (Kaneko et al., 2020).

text: Recent studies on multilingual NMT fine-tuning demonstrate benefits from balanced sampling across languages (Conneau et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2020). Pretraining with language-agnostic sentence representations has also been shown to transfer across domains and scripts (Artetxe and Schwenk, 2019). Large-scale synthetic corpora constructed via back-translation improve robustness to noise and rare tokens (Edunov et al., 2018). Tokenization choices such as shared subword vocabularies can bias cross-lingual transfer (Kudo, 2018; Zhang et al., 2020).

text: Huang et al. (2018a) introduced pointer-based mechanisms for extractive QA, while Chen et al. (2017) established a strong neural baseline using attention over paragraphs. Yang et al. (2018) presented HotpotQA for multi-hop reasoning with supporting facts, and Kwiatkowski et al. (2019) released Natural Questions with long-form answers. Rajpurkar et al. (2016) created SQuAD as a span-extraction benchmark with crowd-sourced annotations, and Clark et al. (2019) targeted open-domain QA with retrieval-augmented readers. Existing methods (Tseng et al., 2016;Serdyuk et al., 2018;Su and Fung, 2020) focus on optimizing each module in a two-stage manner, where errors in the ASR module would result in severe performance loss.  proved that utilizing clean texts can help model trained on the ASR transcriptions to boost the performance via domain adaptation. SpeechBERT (Chuang et al., 2019) cascaded the BERT-based models as a unified model, and then trained it in a joint manner of audio and text. However, the existing SQA methods aimed at solving a single question given the related passage, without building and maintaining the connections of different questions in the human conversations. In addition, we compare our Spoken-CoQA with existing SQA datasets (See Table ). Unlike existing SQA datasets, Spoken-CoQA is a multi-turn conversational SQA dataset, which is more challenging than single-turn benchmarks.

text: Current self-explaining models often enforce shortest yet sufficient rationales, with the assumption that short rationales are more intuitive to humans (Lei et al., 2016;Bastings et al., 2019). Paranjape et al. (2020) proposes an information bottleneck approach to enable the rationale length control. Lei et al. (2016) study concise supervision for rationales in text classification, and DeYoung et al. (2019) introduce ERASER as a benchmark for faithful explanations without detailing cross-task variability. Bastings et al. (2019) investigate faithfulness metrics for explanations across architectures, and Jain and Wallace (2019) question attention as explanation. However current studies only assessed the methods with auto-metrics and did not evaluate human understanding on different rationale lengths.

(2016) study concise supervision for rationales in text classification, and DeYoung et al. (2019) introduce ERASER as a benchmark for faithful explanations without detailing cross-task variability. Bastings et al. (2019) investigate faithfulness metrics for explanations across architectures, and Jain and Wallace (2019) question attention as explanation.

text: Prior work on social bias detection in language technologies spans lexicon-based diagnostics (Caliskan et al., 2017), controlled sentence templates for comparability across protected attributes (May et al., 2019; Dev et al., 2021), and corpus-level analyses of demographic representation and toxicity (Davidson et al., 2019; Garg et al., 2018). Studies probing embeddings and contextual encoders have reported that associations reflecting gender, race, and religion propagate through downstream tasks without explicit supervision (Zhao et al., 2018; Kurita et al., 2019; Magee et al., 2021). Recent surveys debate normative commitments, taxonomies of harm, and reporting standards (Blodgett et al., 2020; Raji et al., 2022; Mitchell et al., 2019). Benchmarks for fairness in text classification emphasize calibration disparities and error-rate parity (Borkan et al., 2019; Dixon et al., 2018). Crowdsourcing protocols describe prompt design, annotator recruitment, and agreement metrics for stereotype-targeted evaluation suites (Sap et al., 2020; Gehman et al., 2020).

text:
The game of Mafia is particularly well-suited for the goal of determining whether the deceptive participants in a conversation can be identified from the contents of their utterances.
As an adversarial social deduction setting, Mafia provides repeated rounds of claims, counterclaims, and votes, offering rich linguistic signals tied to incentives under imperfect information.
Prior studies on deception in dialogue have charted varied operationalizations of lying signals in conversational dynamics (Hancock et al., 2007; Almela et al., 2012). Lexical cues such as uncertainty markers, negations, and hedging have been associated with deceptive intent in constrained tasks (Ott et al., 2011; Pérez-Rosas et al., 2015). Turn-taking irregularities and response latency have also been linked to strategic concealment in multi-party talk (Jiang and Sedoc, 2020). Feature-learning with contextual embeddings has expanded the scope of detectable cues in open-domain settings (Kleinberg et al., 2018).
While these findings motivate text-based inference, games like Mafia differ from dyadic or monologic deception by featuring strategic coalition dynamics and public commitments across rounds.
Research on computational social deduction has begun to exploit these properties using interaction graphs and role-conditioned language modeling (Deduct et al., 2021; Gamage and Hruschka, 2022).
Datasets curated from online play transcripts enable benchmarking of deception detection under shifting stakes and partial observability (Feng et al., 2021).
Work on persuasion in deliberative forums highlights the importance of stance shifts and narrative framing, both of which appear in Mafia accusations and defenses (Tan et al., 2016; Durmus and Cardie, 2019).
Complementary threads examine rumor veracity and misinformation cascades, emphasizing how group-level belief formation shapes the success of deceptive claims (Vosoughi et al., 2018; Shu et al., 2020).
In contrast to misinformation diffusion, Mafia imposes immediate feedback through voting, which sharpens the coupling between utterances and downstream accountability signals.
Approaches that integrate temporal argument structure with speaker roles have shown gains in multi-party analyses, suggesting value for role-aware modeling in Mafia (Niculae et al., 2017; Rosenthal and McKeown, 2015).
We build on this line by formalizing round-structured commitments and contradictions as latent states that condition surface text features.
Our contribution is threefold: we introduce a corpus with explicit role annotations across rounds, propose a sequence-labeling architecture that fuses dialogue acts with contradiction arcs, and release evaluation protocols for out-of-round generalization.
We hypothesize that role-conditioned contradiction density, rhetorical stance shifts, and commitment volatility are predictive of deception in this domain.
Finally, we discuss ethical considerations around surveillance misuse and propose protocols for consent, data minimization, and responsible deployment.

text: Relation Extraction. Relation extraction aims at extracting relation between entities within a given sentence. Many relation extraction methods (Qian et al., 2008;Zeng et al., 2014;Zhou et al., 2016) are supervised model. Recently, some studies focus on pre-training language model (Devlin et al., 2019;Lan et al., 2020) because of its powerful capability of semantic representation. Wu and He (2019) propose R-BERT that uses BERT to extract relation features and incorporates entity information to perform relation extraction. Soares et al. (2019) propose a relation representation learning method based on BERT and have shown promising results. However, these models require labeled data. Prior work on distant supervision aligns knowledge base triples with text to reduce annotation cost (Mintz et al., 2009; Riedel et al., 2010; Hoffmann et al., 2011). Pattern-based bootstrapping approaches iteratively expand seed patterns to harvest relation instances from corpora (Brin, 1998; Agichtein and Gravano, 2000). Dependency-path kernels and neural encoders over syntactic graphs have also been explored (Bunescu and Mooney, 2005; Zhang et al., 2018). Large-scale denoising of weakly labeled data has been studied with noise-aware objectives (Ren et al., 2017; Qin et al., 2018). Multi-instance learning aggregates evidence over mentions to infer bag-level labels (Zeng et al., 2015). Representation learning with contrastive objectives has been reported to stabilize training on heterogeneous sources (Gao et al., 2021). Unsupervised relation extraction (Yu et al., 2017;Saha et al., 2018;Stanovsky et al., 2018) seeks to discover relation types without predefined schemas or labeled examples, often by clustering mention pairs based on distributional and syntactic cues. Open Information Extraction methods target broad-coverage tuple extraction from web text and have influenced schema-free relation discovery (Banko et al., 2007;Etzioni et al., 2011). Recent advances exploit contextualized embeddings to improve cluster separability and relation phrase normalization (Hasegawa et al., 2004;Elsahar et al., 2017;Cetto et al., 2018). Domain adaptation is another active line, addressing shifts between scientific, biomedical, and news corpora (Guo et al., 2019;Peng et al., 2020). Adversarial training and instance reweighting have been proposed to mitigate distribution mismatch in cross-domain settings (Ganin and Lempitsky, 2015;Ben-David et al., 2010;Jiang and Zhai, 2007). Few-shot relation extraction aims to generalize to novel relations from limited labeled examples by metric learning and prototypical representations (Snell et al., 2017;Gao et al., 2019;Han et al., 2018). Meta-learning frameworks further adapt to task variations with episodic training and fast adaptation objectives (Finn et al., 2017;Ravi and Larochelle, 2017;Chen et al., 2019). Data augmentation with paraphrasing and back-translation improves coverage of relation patterns under label scarcity (Sennrich et al., 2016;Yu et al., 2018;Kumar et al., 2020). Calibration techniques reduce overconfidence in low-resource regimes and support selective prediction (Guo et al., 2017;Jiang et al., 2021). Distantly supervised denoising has been enhanced by partial-label learning and instance-level confidence estimation (Xu et al., 2019;Zhou, 2018;Bekker and Goldberger, 2016). Graph neural networks propagate signals over document-level entity graphs to capture cross-sentence relations (Yao et al., 2019;Christopoulou et al., 2019;Nan et al., 2020). Document-level models are complemented by discourse cues and coreference resolution to improve long-range reasoning (Cohan et al., 2018;Wu et al., 2020;Yao et al., 2021). Pre-training objectives specialized for relation induction integrate mention masking and span boundary prediction to emphasize entity interactions (Joshi et al., 2020;Dai et al., 2022). Recent research on prompt-based tuning examines template design and verbalizers for few-shot adaptation (Schick and Schütze, 2021; Gao et al., 2021). Parameter-efficient methods introduce lightweight adapters or prefix vectors while keeping backbone weights frozen (Houlsby et al., 2019; Li and Liang, 2021). Studies of instruction-tuned models document emergent generalization across information extraction tasks (Sanh et al., 2022; Wei et al., 2022). Scaling laws quantify performance as a function of parameters and data (Kaplan et al., 2020). In-context learning analyzes how models leverage demonstrations without gradient updates (Brown et al., 2020). Benchmarks for factuality and hallucination consider grounding in retrieval-augmented settings (Lewis et al., 2020; Shuster et al., 2021). Despite these advances, robust evaluation remains challenging due to label noise, annotation artifacts, and distribution drift across time and domains (McCoy et al., 2019;Gururangan et al., 2020). Comprehensive testbeds that span sentence-level, document-level, and open-domain settings are needed to assess generalization and reliability. Our work contributes a unified benchmark and a strong baseline that covers supervised, distant, and low-resource scenarios with standardized protocols.

text: Our work is in the research line of recommender systems widely studied because of their practical value in industry . For example, previous work explores users' chatting history to recommend conversations (Zeng et al., 2018(Zeng et al., , 2020b and hashtags (Li et al., 2016;Zhang et al., 2021), browsing history to recommend news (Wu et al., 2019;Qi et al., 2021), and purchase history to recommend products (Guo et al., 2020). Prior works on conversational recommendation also consider dialogue context tracking (Sun et al., 2021), while graph-based recommenders propagate user-item signals over heterogeneous networks (Wang et al., 2019), and pretraining on large corpora improves generalization in CTR prediction (Zhou et al., 2019). In contrast to most recommendation studies focusing on exploiting target users' personal interest modeling from their history behavior, our work largely relies on wordings of a short query to figure out what is needed by a target user (patient) because they are anonymous for privacy concern.

text: Recent neural approaches integrate mention-context encoders with global coherence constraints to improve disambiguation quality (Ganea and Hofmann, 2017; Le and Titov, 2018; Radhakrishnan et al., 2018). Yamada et al. (2020) introduce pretraining objectives over Wikipedia hyperlinks to align token and entity spaces. Logeswaran et al. (2019) leverage contrastive learning for entity retrieval with large candidate sets. Févry et al. (2020) study representations for zero-shot entity linking using descriptions. BLINK provides a strong bi-encoder and cross-encoder baseline with large-scale negative mining (Wu et al., 2020). Dense retrieval methods using dual-encoders have also been effective in open-domain QA (Karpukhin et al., 2020). Multilingual extensions examine cross-lingual transfer for linking without language-specific supervision (Botha et al., 2020). Prior work on knowledge graph alignment addresses mapping entities across ontologies (Sun et al., 2018). Contextual calibration techniques have been explored to reduce confidence miscalibration in NLU tasks (Desai and Durrett, 2020). Datasets such as AIDA-CoNLL, TAC-KBP, and WNED remain standard evaluation benchmarks (Hoffart et al., 2011; Ji et al., 2010; Guo and Barbosa, 2018).

text: More recently, Moon et al. (2019) propose a neural coherence model to exploit both local and structural aspects. They evaluate their model on an artificial task only, the shuffle test, which determines whether sentences in a document are shuffled or not. Recent studies on discourse parsing emphasize hierarchical rhetorical structure as a key signal (Mann and Thompson, 1988; Ji and Smith, 2017). Pishdad et al. (2020) claim that this artificial task is not suitable to evaluate coherence models. Lai and Tetreault (2018) show that the neural coherence models, which achieve the best performance on this task, do not outperform non-neural models on downstream tasks. More recently, Mohiuddin et al. (2021) find a weak correlation between the model performance in artificial tasks and downstream tasks. In our evaluation, we compare Moon et al. (2019) with ours in an artificial task as well as in three downstream tasks. Moon et al. (2019) perform the best in the artificial task, but do not outperform our model in three downstream tasks (Section 4).

text: Learning self-supervised tasks on large-scale multilingual texts has proven to be effective for pretraining cross-lingual language models. Masked language modeling (MLM; Devlin et al. 2019) is typically used to learn cross-lingual encoders such as multilingual BERT (mBERT; Devlin et al. 2019) and XLM-R (Conneau et al., 2020). The crosslingual language models can be further improved by introducing external pre-training tasks using parallel corpora. XLM (Conneau and Lample, 2019) introduces the translation language modeling (TLM) task that predicts masked tokens from concatenated translation pairs. ALM  utilizes translation pairs to construct codeswitched sequences as input. InfoXLM (Chi et al., 2021b) considers an input translation pair as crosslingual views of the same meaning, and proposes a cross-lingual contrastive learning task. Prior work on leveraging bilingual signals includes alignment-based objectives and contrastive setups across translation pairs. XLM introduces objectives over concatenated sentences in two languages. mBERT and XLM-R are pretrained predominantly with MLM on mixed-language corpora. Several studies target token- or span-level interaction between parallel sentences without clarifying how these interactions propagate to sentence understanding (Cao et al., 2020; Zhao et al., 2021; Hu et al., 2020a; Chi et al., 2021c). Several pre-training tasks utilize the token-level alignments in parallel data to improve cross-lingual language models (Cao et al., 2020;Zhao et al., 2021;Hu et al., 2020a;Chi et al., 2021c).

text: Here we first review the related work of DA for NLP, then expound the differences between our method and the major existing ones. According to the methodology of data generation, existing methods can be categorized into three types: rule-based, interpolation-based, and model-based, respectively. Rule-based methods rely on explicit heuristics to perturb examples while maintaining label consistency. Simple operations include synonym replacement, random insertion, and deletion under constraints that preserve grammaticality. Constraints such as part-of-speech agreement and semantic similarity thresholds mitigate label drift. Prior work on textual data augmentation spans lexicon substitution, paraphrase generation, and back-translation, with early rule-based approaches substituting words via thesauri or WordNet to increase lexical variety (Zhang et al., 2015; Wei and Zou, 2019; Mihalcea and Tarau, 2004). Paraphrasing using pivoting through a second language improved fluency and diversity in parallel corpora (Sennrich et al., 2016; Wieting and Gimpel, 2018). Contextual augmentation with masked language models replaces tokens with plausible alternatives conditioned on surrounding context, leveraging pre-trained representations (Kobayashi, 2018; Wu et al., 2019; Kumar et al., 2020). Neural machine translation models have been extensively used to produce semantically faithful rewrites, and reinforcement learning has been explored to control style and formality (Edunov et al., 2018; Fadaee et al., 2017; Xu et al., 2019). Interpolation-based approaches synthesize samples by mixing representations or inputs derived from observed examples. Mixup-style methods interpolate either in embedding space or token space to generate hybrids that regularize decision boundaries. Manifold Mixup and Vicinal Risk Minimization have been adapted to sequence classification with label interpolation for soft targets. Cut-and-paste strategies splice spans across documents guided by compatibility scores to preserve topical coherence. Back-translation can also be viewed as an interpolation in the semantic space when combined with paraphrase filtering. Model-based methods train generative models that produce task-conditioned augmentations. Conditional language models can be prompted to generate label-aware paraphrases and counterfactuals. Style transfer models alter sentiment, politeness, and formality while attempting to keep content intact. Adversarial generators target model vulnerabilities with controlled perturbations that retain human interpretability. Recent instruction-tuned LLMs enable few-shot augmentation across tasks without task-specific training. A growing line of work addresses label preservation guarantees via semantic entailment constraints. Researchers have proposed using NLI models to accept only generations that entail the gold label. Similarity metrics based on contextual embeddings help filter out semantic drifts introduced by aggressive edits. Data selection strategies prioritize augmentations that are both diverse and informative. Active learning loops couple augmentation with uncertainty sampling to maximize utility under budget. Curriculum schedules gradually increase perturbation strength to avoid destabilizing optimization. For evaluation, studies report gains on low-resource classification, NER, and QA benchmarks. Robustness improvements are often measured under synonym, character, and paraphrase attacks. Cross-domain transfer is assessed by training on one domain and testing on another to quantify generalization. Despite progress, open challenges remain in measuring semantic fidelity at scale. Another challenge is avoiding shortcut features that teach the model spurious correlations. Our work targets interpolation-based augmentation with controllable semantics. We propose a representation mixing framework that aligns latent subspaces with interpretable attributes. The method enforces disentanglement to modulate content and style factors independently. We incorporate an NLI-based verifier and a calibration step based on temperature scaling. Compared with rule-based augmentation, our approach reduces manual engineering. Compared with purely model-based generation, our approach offers stronger label control through convexity constraints. We benchmark on sentiment analysis, topic classification, and paraphrase identification. The experimental setup includes both in-domain and cross-domain splits. We report accuracy, F1, and expected calibration error with confidence intervals. Ablations analyze the contribution of each component and the sensitivity to mixing coefficients. We release code and data to facilitate reproducibility and further research. Future work will extend the framework to sequence labeling and generative tasks. We anticipate that combining our approach with active selection will further improve data efficiency.
span_text: Prior work on textual data augmentation spans lexicon substitution, paraphrase generation, and back-translation, with early rule-based approaches substituting words via thesauri or WordNet to increase lexical variety (Zhang et al., 2015; Wei and Zou, 2019; Mihalcea and Tarau, 2004). Paraphrasing using pivoting through a second language improved fluency and diversity in parallel corpora (Sennrich et al., 2016; Wieting and Gimpel, 2018). Contextual augmentation with masked language models replaces tokens with plausible alternatives conditioned on surrounding context, leveraging pre-trained representations (Kobayashi, 2018; Wu et al., 2019; Kumar et al., 2020). Neural machine translation models have been extensively used to produce semantically faithful rewrites, and reinforcement learning has been explored to control style and formality (Edunov et al., 2018; Fadaee et al., 2017; Xu et al., 2019).

Contextual augmentation with masked language models replaces tokens with plausible alternatives conditioned on surrounding context, leveraging pre-trained representations (Kobayashi, 2018; Wu et al., 2019; Kumar et al., 2020). Neural machine translation models have been extensively used to produce semantically faithful rewrites, and reinforcement learning has been explored to control style and formality (Edunov et al., 2018; Fadaee et al., 2017; Xu et al., 2019).

text: Our approach draws insights from multi-label classification (MLC) (Tsoumakas and Katakis, 2007;Zhou, 2006, 2014). One of the earliest approaches for MLC was to transform the problem into multiple binary classification problems while ignoring the correlations between labels (Boutell et al., 2004). More recent work has modeled MLC in the sequence-to-sequence framework with a decoder that generates the labels sequentially, thus preserving the inter-label correlations (Yang et al., 2018). Most prior work in MLC focuses on classification and is not directly applicable to MT. In contrast, our training strategy is tailored for sequence-to-sequence problems. Unlike prior work (Yang et al., 2018), SCONES allows us to perform MLC style training with any underlying NMT architecture by simply changing the loss function. By jointly training all label-specific binary classifiers, our strategy is able to account for label correlations. Recent work on label dependencies includes conditional dependency networks (Guo and Gu, 2011), classifier chains with probabilistic correction (Dembczyński et al., 2012), and graph neural models for label graphs (Yeh et al., 2017). Sequence tagging with CRFs has been revisited for multi-task outputs (Ma and Hovy, 2016). Neural reranking for SMT emphasizes structured prediction trade-offs (Shen et al., 2004). Pre-trained encoder-decoder models have scaled label spaces efficiently (Raffel et al., 2020). used an MLC objective to improve machine translation. Unlike our approach, they attempted to predict all words in the target sentence with a bag-of-words loss function. We formulate the next word prediction at each time step as a MLC problem to handle intrinsic uncertainty, but our models are predicting ordered target sequences, not bags of words.

text: Adapter modules Adapters have proven to be very efficient for multi-task NLP (Houlsby et al., 2019;Bapna and Firat, 2019;Pham et al., 2020;Pfeiffer et al., 2020). In a nutshell, this technique consists in plugging several so-called adapter modules to the intermediate layers of a pretrained Transformer and finetuning these adapters on the downstream tasks. Adapters can also be trained without supervision for multilingual translation (Philip et al., 2020). However, the hard-coded separation between the domains of different tasks may lead to a catastrophic forgetting effect (Pfeiffer et al., 2021), which is a common problem in multi-task modeling using neural networks (McCloskey and Cohen, 1989). In multidomain translation, Pham et al. (2021) recently demonstrated the brittleness of adapters against fuzzy domain separations, outof-domain distributions, and erroneous domain tags. Several subsequent studies have aimed to mitigate this weakness through a mixture of expert mechanism (e.g. (Pfeiffer et al., 2021)). Zhang et al. (2021) propose to learn to route between shared and language-specific representations with a conditional language-specific routing while training the parameters of the underlying Transformer. This method is related to the Fusion-Adapters of Pfeiffer et al. (2021). Both approaches aim to select between shared and task-specific representations. The proximity between tasks is not taken into account in the routing mechanism. We propose a different approach to the problem of multi-task routing in the underlying network.

text: Probing Knowledge in Pretrained Models Many pieces of previous work aim to measure knowledge stored in pretrained models. Petroni et al. (2019) propose to retrieve knowledge in pretrained models (such as BERT) using cloze queries. Their experiments show that BERT has a strong ability to recall factual knowledge without any finetuning. Jiang et al. (2020b) improve the cloze queries with mining-based and paraphrasing-based methods. Roberts et al. (2020) propose the closedbook question answering to measure how much knowledge a pretrained model has stored in its parameters. Elazar et al. (2021) measure and improve the consistency of pretrained models with respect to factual knowledge prediction. Rather than examining only the model outputs, we provide an openthe-black-box analysis for the knowledge neurons in pretrained Transformers.

text:
In this section we mainly discuss and compare related work in the two most relevant directions: the assessment benchmarks to the general narrative comprehension skills; and the tasks specifically designed for character-centered predictions over narratives. Table  gives a summary of these narrative comprehension tasks, associated with their required skills of comprehension. We also reviewed studies on character-centered tasks over non-narrative texts like synopses and chit-chat (i.e., not story-related) conversations. Detailed rationales of the required skills for each task are discussed in Appendix A.
To ground our taxonomy, we first consider general narrative comprehension evaluations that emphasize reasoning beyond surface patterns. Benchmarks such as the Story Cloze Test and its successors foreground event plausibility and narrative coherence under minimal supervision. Prior work on narrative comprehension benchmarks has emphasized multi-faceted evaluations that probe temporal, causal, and commonsense reasoning through question answering formats (Mostafazadeh et al., 2016; Ostermann et al., 2019; Talmor et al., 2019). Reading comprehension datasets targeting world knowledge and multi-hop inference have introduced challenging distractors to test robustness (Yang et al., 2018; Clark et al., 2019; Khashabi et al., 2020). Narrative QA settings also explore entity tracking and event prediction with long-range dependencies (Kočiský et al., 2018; Chambers and Jurafsky, 2008; Pichotta and Mooney, 2016). Tasks for script induction model stereotyped event sequences from large corpora (Frermann et al., 2014; Modi and Titov, 2014). Studies on commonsense inference in stories evaluate plausibility and expectation violations (Rashkin et al., 2018; Sap et al., 2019).
In parallel, character-centered prediction tasks explicitly require modeling personas, beliefs, and evolving relations. Datasets for coreference and entity state tracking in narrative settings highlight the importance of memory and salience dynamics. Emotion and intent recognition over story characters provide supervision for affective trajectories and social inference. Social commonsense benchmarks have further stressed role-based reasoning for character goals and obligations. Recent work combines text-centric features with graph structures that capture interactions among characters and events.
Methodologically, approaches range from pretrained encoder-decoders augmented with entity memories to structured latent-variable models for event chains. Memory-augmented architectures maintain entity representations over long contexts to support anaphora resolution and belief updates. Graph neural networks propagate information along character and event graphs to model higher-order dependencies. Retrieval-augmented models incorporate external knowledge for situational awareness in narrative contexts.
We also consider character-centered tasks beyond canonical stories, including dialogue and synopsis understanding. Dialogue corpora enable fine-grained modeling of speaker-specific goals and stance, though narrative arcs are often implicit. Synopsis datasets compress plot-level information and challenge models to recover missing causal links. Cross-domain evaluations reveal that models trained on narratives often underperform on conversational settings due to differences in turn structure and pragmatic cues.
Despite substantial progress, gaps remain in unifying evaluation across skills such as temporal reasoning, causal attribution, and social inference. Existing leaderboards frequently conflate improvements in language modeling with genuine gains in character understanding. Our benchmark protocol isolates character-grounded predictions while controlling for lexical overlap and annotation artifacts. We release standardized splits, skill-aligned subsets, and diagnostic probes to facilitate comparable reporting.
Finally, we outline how our task connects general comprehension to character-specific inference through shared evaluation scaffolds. The design encourages models to leverage narrative structure while maintaining fine-grained sensitivity to character states. We anticipate that stronger inductive biases for entity persistence and goal modeling will yield consistent gains. All datasets, code, and annotation guidelines are provided to support transparent replication and extension.

text: Abstractive Related Work Generation. Recently, Xing et al. (2020) extend the pointergenerator (See et al., 2017) to take two text inputs, allowing them to recover a masked citation sentence given its neighboring context sentences. Ge et al. (2021) encode the citation context, cited paper's abstract, and citation network and train their model with multiple objectives: sentence salience score regression of the cited paper's abstract, functional role classification of the citation sentence, and citation sentence generation. Chen et al. (2021) propose a relation-aware, multi-document encoder to generate a related work paragraph given a set of cited papers. Luu et al. (2021) fine-tune GPT2 (Radford et al., 2019) on scientific texts and explore several techniques for representing documents, such as using extracted named entities. Xing et al. (2020) employ a copy-augmented mechanism with dual inputs to better capture cross-sentence cues for recovering masked citation sentences. Ge et al. (2021) incorporate graph-based signals from the citation network to enrich contextual representations while balancing salience and role-specific objectives. Chen et al. (2021) introduce cross-document attention to fuse evidence across multiple sources for paragraph-level generation. Luu et al. (2021) adapt pretrained language models to scientific domains with document-centric representations. These studies report gains on separate benchmarks and configurations without clarifying cross-paper comparability or shared assumptions about input granularity and evaluation design, and Johnson et al. (2019) examine neural keyphrase extraction in patents, noting domain transfer challenges that affect vocabulary coverage.

text: Introduction. Prompting has become a central paradigm for steering large language models toward desired behaviors in both zero-shot and few-shot settings. A key methodological divide concerns whether prompts are continuous vectors optimized in embedding space or discrete strings authored or searched in natural language. Continuous prompts. There is a line of work focused on tuning continuous prompts (Li and Liang, 2021;Lester et al., 2021;Zhong et al., 2021;Qin and Eisner, 2021;Zhou et al., 2021). Recent efforts report notable gains from task-specific soft vectors and prefix tuning on diverse NLP benchmarks (Li and Liang, 2021; Lester et al., 2021). Several works explore differentiable prompt initializations and optimization landscapes (Zhong et al., 2021; Qin and Eisner, 2021). Zhou et al. (2021) examine prompt transfer across domains and label spaces using continuous embeddings. Petroni et al. (2019) analyze factual recall in masked LMs with cloze prompts, and Brown et al. (2020) demonstrate few-shot behaviors in autoregressive models. A recurring theme in this line of work is the strength of continuous prompt in results in strong, yet compact models-compared to conventional architecture fine-tuning approaches. Motivated by the success of continuous prompt tuning, this paper investigates the feasibility of interpreting a learned continuous prompt and its connection to discrete prompts. Problem framing. We formalize the interpretation problem as mapping an optimized soft prompt to a family of semantically aligned discrete templates. We study the extent to which such mappings preserve task performance and calibration. Method overview. Our approach combines representation probing with constrained search over token sequences guided by embedding similarity and gradient signals. We evaluate whether discovered strings reproduce the behavior of the original soft prompt. Discrete prompts. The release of GPT-3 (Brown et al., 2020) sparked a lot of excitement about the emergent ability of LMs in following discrete natural language prompts. Consequently, countless follow-up studies have used manually-designed discrete prompts for probing LMs (Petroni et al., 2019;Jiang et al., 2020), improving LMs few-shot ability (Schick and Schütze, 2021;Gao et al., 2021;Le Scao and Rush, 2021), and their zero-shot abil-ity as well as transferability (Mishra et al., 2021a;Reynolds and McDonell, 2021). While discrete prompts have clear advantages, in addition to being human-readable and thus easily interpretable, we do not have efficient and algorithmic ways of reconstructing them. For example, Shin et al. (2020)'s algorithm discovers discrete prompts, yet the results are not human readable. Prior work also finds that model performance is highly sensitive to small changes in wordings (Mishra et al., 2021a) and that optimization over the discrete prompt space is nontrivial and often highly unstable. Our findings here about the disconnect between continuous prompts and their discrete interpretation provides another perspective on the difficulty of discovering discrete prompts via continuous optimization algorithms that (directly or indirectly) leverage the continuous space (more discussion in §6). Contributions. We present a systematic framework for analyzing learned soft prompts, a method for deriving readable templates, and an empirical study across classification and generation tasks. Implications. Our results highlight limits of post hoc interpretation and motivate research on training-time constraints that encourage discrete-aligned soft prompts.

text: Older methods for text-based stimulus representation include text corpus co-occurrence counts (Mitchell et al., 2008;Pereira et al., 2013;Huth et al., 2016), syntactic and discourse features (Wehbe et al., 2014). In recent times, both semantic and experiential attribute models have been explored for text-based stimuli. Semantic representation models include distributed word embeddings (Pereira et al., 2016;Anderson et al., 2017a;Pereira et al., 2018;Toneva and Wehbe, 2019;Hollenstein et al., 2019;Wang et al., 2020), sentence representation models (Sun et al., 2019;Toneva and Wehbe, 2019;Sun et al., 2020), recurrent neural networks (Jain and Huth, 2018;Oota et al., 2019), and Transformer-based language models (Gauthier and Levy, 2019;Toneva and Wehbe, 2019;Schwartz et al., 2019). Experiential attribute models represent words in terms of human ratings of their degree of association with different attributes of experience, typically on a scale of 0-6 (Anderson et al., 2019(Anderson et al., , 2020Berezutskaya et al., 2020;Jat et al., 2020;Caucheteux et al., 2021a;Antonello et al., 2021) or binary (Handjaras et al., 2016;Wang et al., 2017). Fine-grained details such as lexical, compositional, syntactic, and semantic representations of narratives are factorized from transformerbased models and utilized for training encoding models. The resulting models are better able to disentangle the corresponding brain responses in fMRI (Caucheteux et al., 2021a). In this paper, we focus on Transformer-based linguistic stimuli representations since they have been found to be most effective. Unlike previous studies which directly used existing task-agnostic pretrained models, we train task-specific Transformer models and aim to find which model leads to the best encoding accuracy given reading and listening language stimuli.

text: Recent investigations of robustness in multilingual evaluation provide related context. Smith et al. (2021) examine calibration drift across language families and report uneven gains tied to tokenization granularity. Koehn and Knowles (2017) document a wide range of pitfalls in neural MT evaluation, with emphasis on domain and sentence length effects. Concurrently, data-centric approaches emphasize distributional matching between training and evaluation corpora (Sagawa et al., 2020; Koh et al., 2021). Liu et al. (2022) study memorization under dataset shifts in pretraining regimes. Findings on benchmark saturation and metric sensitivity are also reported (Mathur et al., 2020; Kocmi and Federmann, 2023).

text: In this section, we analyze the literature on the two main evaluation problems tackled in this paper: multi-labeling and class hierarchies, keeping the focus on extreme scenarios (numerous and unbalanced classes). Prior work on multi-label learning has emphasized partitioned label spaces, calibrated decision thresholds, and scalable inference. Zhang and Zhou (2014) surveyed algorithmic paradigms including problem transformation and algorithm adaptation for multi-label classification. Nam et al. (2014) introduced label-wise attention to capture per-label signals. Read et al. (2011) studied classifier chains to exploit inter-label dependencies. Gong et al. (2013) explored hashing-based methods for efficient retrieval. Yu et al. (2014) proposed SLEEC to preserve local distances in label space embeddings. The literature on hierarchical classification has concurrently expanded taxonomic constraints in prediction. Silla and Freitas (2011) reviewed local versus global strategies for tree- and DAG-structured taxonomies. Vens et al. (2008) presented predictive clustering trees that integrate hierarchy-aware splits. Kosmopoulos et al. (2015) evaluated loss functions aligned with the depth and ancestry of misclassifications. Bi and Kwok (2015) examined cost-sensitive reductions for hierarchical risks. Recent scaling efforts considered millions of labels with sparse positive assignments and long-tail frequency profiles. For extreme multi-label settings, label space decomposition methods reduce computation while attempting to retain semantic neighborhoods. Graph-based approaches learn label graphs or co-occurrence structures to regularize predictions under sparsity. Negative sampling and calibrated stacking further improve ranking metrics in the presence of severe imbalance. Complementarily, hierarchical losses such as tree-induced precision penalize ancestor-descendant errors more gently than flat measures. Neural encoders with joint text-label embeddings align instance features and label descriptors in a shared space. Advances in zero-shot and weakly supervised label expansion reduce manual curation costs for growing ontologies. In evaluation, micro-averaged metrics often reflect head labels, whereas macro-averaged metrics surface tail behavior. Path-aware measures for hierarchies capture semantic proximity between predicted and true nodes. Extreme-scale benchmarks such as Amazon-3M and WikiLSHTC catalyzed research on indexable inference and streaming updates. Our work synthesizes these strands by proposing evaluation criteria that are sensitive to both inter-label structure and hierarchical depth. We also provide ablations showing robustness to skew, label growth, and partial taxonomic revisions. Finally, we release code and standardized protocols to facilitate reproducibility across diverse extreme-label corpora.

text: Another related task is fact verification which aims to verify a statement based on retrieved evidence. Fact verification has been explored in multiple domains such as general domain (Thorne et al., 2018), climate change (Diggelmann et al., 2020) and COVID-19 (Wadden et al., 2020;Saakyan Figure : An overview of our approach. We first construct a within-document KG for each document based on IE output, where represents an entity and represents an event. Then, we construct a cross-document KG by (1) adding a node for each cross-document event cluster and connecting it with events in the cluster, and (2) introducing a document node for each document and connecting it with all entities and events in the given document. Finally, we use GNN to encode the cross-document KG, and use the event and document features to conduct misinformation detection at two levels. The two detectors are trained and deployed in a pipeline fashion, where event-level detection results are leveraged to improve document-level detection. et al., 2021). However, fact verification focuses on short single-sentence statements, and cannot model the complicated internal structure of a news document.
span_text: Fact verification has been explored in multiple domains such as general domain (Thorne et al., 2018), climate change (Diggelmann et al., 2020) and COVID-19 (Wadden et al., 2020;Saakyan Figure : An overview of our approach. We first construct a within-document KG for each document based on IE output, where represents an entity and represents an event. Then, we construct a cross-document KG by (1) adding a node for each cross-document event cluster and connecting it with events in the cluster, and (2) introducing a document node for each document and connecting it with all entities and events in the given document.

text: Pretraining for Retrieval first propose to pretrain a bi-encoder retriever with an Inverse Cloze Task (ICT), which constructs a training pair by randomly selecting a sentence from a passage as the query and leaving the rest as the document. propose two pretraining tasks for Wikipedia and attempt to combine them with ICT and masked language modeling (MLM). Guu et al. (2020) pretrain a retriever and a reader together for end-to-end question answering (QA).

text: Very recently, DPR-PAQ (Oguz et al., 2021) highlight the importance of domain matching by using both synthetic and crawled QA data to pretrain and then fine-tune the model on downstream datasets for dialogue retrieval. Condenser is a new Transformer variant for MLM pretraining. It exploits an information bottleneck to facilitate learning for information aggregation. On top of that, coCondenser adds an unsupervised corpus-level contrastive loss to warm up the passage embedding space. Different from these works, LaPraDoR is the first pretrained retriever that does not require fine-tuning on a downstream dataset and can perform zero-shot retrieval.
